| AC-3 | Propagating | $O(cd^3)$ | $O(c + n)$ | Binary CSPs | Strong pruning |
| Min-Conflicts | Local Search | $\approx O(1)$ empirical | $O(n)$ | Large CSPs | Speed at scale |
| Constraint Optimization | Hybrid | Problem-dependent | Varies | CSO | Finds optimum |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSP Framework — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Variables Set X"] --> mergeNode["CSP Triple Definition"]
    inputB["Domain Set D"] --> mergeNode
    inputC["Constraint Set C"] --> mergeNode
    mergeNode --> solverBlock["CSP Solver Engine"]
    solverBlock --> sub1["Constraint Propagation"]
    solverBlock --> sub2["Backtracking Search"]
    solverBlock --> sub3["Local Search"]
    sub1 --> outNode["Solution Assignment"]
    sub2 --> outNode
    sub3 --> outNode
    outNode --> validate["Constraint Verification"]
    validate --> final["Satisfying Assignment or Failure"]
```

## 4.2 Constraint Graph — Australia Map Coloring

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeWA["WA"]
    nodeNT["NT"]
    nodeSA["SA"]
    nodeQ["Q"]
    nodeNSW["NSW"]
    nodeV["V"]
    nodeT["T"]
    nodeWA --- nodeNT
    nodeWA --- nodeSA
    nodeNT --- nodeSA
    nodeNT --- nodeQ
    nodeSA --- nodeQ
    nodeSA --- nodeNSW
    nodeSA --- nodeV
    nodeQ --- nodeNSW
    nodeNSW --- nodeV
```

> [!NOTE]
> Tasmania (T) is disconnected from the rest of the graph — it forms an independent subproblem that can be solved in isolation. The remaining graph is **non-tree**, requiring careful constraint propagation.

## 4.3 AC-3 Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Initialize Queue with all directed arcs"]
    startA --> checkQueue{"Queue empty?"}
    checkQueue -->|Yes| doneA["Return True - Arc Consistent"]
    checkQueue -->|No| removeArc["Remove arc X_i X_j from queue"]
    removeArc --> reviseCall["Call REVISE on X_i X_j"]
    reviseCall --> revisedCheck{"Domain of X_i reduced?"}
    revisedCheck -->|No| checkQueue
    revisedCheck -->|Yes| emptyCheck{"D_i empty?"}
    emptyCheck -->|Yes| failA["Return False - No Solution"]
    emptyCheck -->|No| addArcs["Add arcs X_k X_i for all neighbors X_k except X_j"]
    addArcs --> checkQueue
```

## 4.4 Backtracking Search Tree — Partial Expansion for Map Coloring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Start: Empty Assignment"]
    rootA --> l1a["WA = red"]
    rootA --> l1b["WA = green"]
    rootA --> l1c["WA = blue"]
    l1a --> l2a["NT = green"]
    l1a --> l2b["NT = blue"]
    l2a --> l3a["SA = blue"]
    l2a --> l3b["SA = red"]
    l2b --> l3c["SA = red"]
    l2b --> l3d["SA = green"]
    l3a --> l4a["Q = red"]
    l3a --> l4b["Q = green"]
    l4a --> l5a["NSW = green"]
    l5a --> l6a["V = red"]
    l6a --> l7a["T = red: SOLUTION FOUND"]
```

## 4.5 Variable and Value Ordering Heuristics

```mermaid
graph LR
    heurA["MRV Most Constrained Variable"] --> heurOutput["Picks variable with smallest remaining domain"]
    heurB["Degree Heuristic"] --> heurOutput2["Breaks ties by choosing variable with most constraints on unassigned"]
    heurC["LCV Least Constraining Value"] --> heurOutput3["Picks value that rules out fewest choices for neighbors"]
    heurOutput --> combined["Combined Heuristic Strategy"]
    heurOutput2 --> combined
    heurOutput3 --> combined
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). List and explain its three main components.**

**Model Answer**:
A Constraint Satisfaction Problem is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

1. **Variables ($X$)**: A finite set of variables $\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ that need to be assigned values. Example: For a scheduling problem, variables could represent tasks.

2. **Domains ($D$)**: A set of domains $\{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ where $D_i$ is the set of allowable values for variable $X_i$. Domains can be discrete (finite) or continuous.

3. **Constraints ($C$)**: A set of constraints $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\}$ that restrict the simultaneous values the variables can take. Each constraint is a pair $\langle \text{scope}, \text{relation} \rangle$.

A **solution** is a complete assignment satisfying all constraints.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is arc consistency? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer**:
A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $x \in D_i$ there exists some value $y \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied.

Formally, arc $(X_i, X_j)$ is consistent if:
$$\forall x \in D_i, \exists y \in D_j : (x, y) \in C_{ij}$$

**Example**: If $D_i = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $D_j = \{1, 2\}$, with constraint $X_i < X_j$, then the arc is not consistent because value $3 \in D_i$ has no supporting $y \in D_j$ (since both $y=1$ and $y=2$ give $X_i \geq X_j$). After revision, $D_i$ becomes $\{1, 2\}$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain the components of a Constraint Satisfaction Problem. Formulate the N-Queens problem as a CSP and discuss the constraint graph for N = 4. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Components of CSP** (recap from Part A): The triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ as defined earlier.

**N-Queens CSP Formulation (N = 4)**:

- **Variables**: $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$ (one per column).
- **Domains**: $D_i = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (row position).
- **Constraints**: For all $i \neq j$:
  - $X_i \neq X_j$ (different rows)
  - $\vert X_i - X_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert$ (different diagonals)

**Constraint Graph for N = 4**:

Each variable connects to every other variable (complete graph $K_4$):
- $X_1 - X_2$, $X_1 - X_3$, $X_1 - X_4$, $X_2 - X_3$, $X_2 - X_4$, $X_3 - X_4$.

The graph is **dense**, indicating many constraints. Degree of each node = 3.

**(b) Solve the 4-Queens problem using backtracking. Show the complete search tree. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

Using MRV heuristic with value ordering:

| Step | Assignment | Check | Result |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $X_1 = 1$ | Initial | $\surd$ |
| 2 | $X_2 = 3$ | $\vert 1 - 3 \vert = 2 \neq \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 1$ $\surd$ | $\surd$ |
| 3 | $X_3 = ?$ | $X_3 = 1$ fails (same row as $X_1$); $X_3 = 2$ fails (diag with $X_1$); $X_3 = 3$ fails (same row as $X_2$); $X_3 = 4$ fails (diag with $X_2$: $\vert 4 - 3 \vert = 1 = \vert 3 - 2 \vert$) | **Backtrack** |
| 4 | $X_2 = 4$ | $\vert 1 - 4 \vert = 3 \neq 1$ $\surd$ | $\surd$ |
| 5 | $X_3 = 2$ | $\vert 2 - 1 \vert = 1 \neq 2$ $\surd$; $\vert 2 - 4 \vert = 2 \neq 1$ $\surd$ | $\surd$ |
| 6 | $X_4 = 4$ | $\vert 4 - 2 \vert = 2 \neq 1$ $\surd$; $\vert 4 - 1 \vert = 3 \neq 3$ fails (diag with $X_1$); try $X_4 = 1, 2, 3$ all fail | **Continue** |

**Final Solution**: $\{X_1 = 1, X_2 = 4, X_3 = 2, X_4 = ?\}$ — checking $X_4$: must avoid row 1, 4, 2 and diagonals. $X_4 = 3$: row OK, $\vert 3 - 1 \vert = 2 \neq 3$ $\surd$, $\vert 3 - 4 \vert = 1 \neq 2$ $\surd$, $\vert 3 - 2 \vert = 1 = 1$ fails.

Backtrack further — final 4-Queens solution: $\{X_1 = 2, X_2 = 4, X_3 = 1, X_4 = 3\}$.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain constraint propagation and the AC-3 algorithm in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Constraint Propagation**: The general idea of propagating the implications of a constraint on one variable's domain onto other variables' domains. It is used to reduce the search space *before* and *during* backtracking.

**Arc Consistency (formal)**: A variable $X_i$ is arc-consistent w.r.t. $X_j$ if every value in $D_i$ has a corresponding value in $D_j$ satisfying the constraint.

**AC-3 Algorithm Steps**:

1. Initialize a queue with **all directed arcs** $(X_i, X_j)$ where $C_{ij}$ exists.
2. Pop arc $(X_i, X_j)$ from queue.
3. Apply **REVISE**: remove values from $D_i$ that have no support in $D_j$.
4. If $D_i$ was modified:
   - If $D_i$ is now empty, return **failure**.
   - Add all arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ to queue where $X_k$ is a neighbor of $X_i$ (other than $X_j$).
5. Repeat until queue is empty. Return **success**.

**Complexity**: $O(cd^3)$ time, $O(c + n)$ space.

**(b) Explain the forward checking algorithm and the min-conflicts heuristic for CSP solving. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Forward Checking**: When a variable $X$ is assigned, immediately remove from the domains of all unassigned neighbors any value that is inconsistent with the assigned value of $X$. If any neighbor's domain becomes empty, backtrack.

- **Time complexity per assignment**: $O(d)$ per neighbor, summed over $d$ neighbors gives $O(d^2)$ per level.
- **Strength**: Detects failures earlier than plain backtracking.

**Min-Conflicts Heuristic** (for local search):

1. Start with a **random complete assignment**.
2. Repeat:
   - If current assignment is solution, return it.
   - Pick a **conflicted variable** (one violating some constraint).
   - Choose a value for that variable that **minimizes the number of conflicts**.
   - Set the variable to that value.
3. Stop after max steps.

**Strength**: Extremely fast on large problems like N-Queens for large $N$ (constant expected time empirically).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the AC-3 third $d$ factor**: Many students write $O(cd^2)$. The correct bound is $O(cd^3)$ — the third factor arises from re-queueing neighbors.
> 2. **Confusing arc consistency with node consistency**: Arc consistency is between **two variables**, node/unary consistency is on a **single variable**.
> 3. **Not stating both directions of arc consistency**: $(X_i, X_j)$ and $(X_j, X_i)$ are different directed arcs.
> 4. **Forgetting completeness of backtracking**: Backtracking is **complete** for finite-domain CSPs; min-conflicts is **not complete** (can get stuck in local minima).
> 5. **Miscounting domain reductions**: When applying REVISE, list every value removed explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Constraint Satisfaction Problems**

- **CSP Definition**: A triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ — Variables, Domains, Constraints.
- **Solution**: Complete assignment satisfying all constraints.
- **Constraint Graph**: Nodes = variables, Edges = binary constraints.
- **Constraint Types**:
  - Unary (1 variable), Binary (2 variables), Higher-order (3+ variables).
- **Key Algorithms**:
  - **Backtracking**: Depth-first, $O(d^n)$ worst case, complete.
  - **Forward Checking**: Prunes neighbor domains on assignment.
  - **AC-3**: Enforces arc consistency, $O(cd^3)$ time, complete.
  - **Min-Conflicts**: Local search, not complete but extremely fast empirically.
- **Heuristics**:
  - **MRV (Most Constrained Variable)**: Pick variable with smallest domain.
  - **Degree Heuristic**: Tie-breaker using most constraints on unassigned variables.
  - **LCV (Least Constraining Value)**: Pick value ruling out fewest neighbor values.
- **Complexity Formulas (must memorize)**:
  - Backtracking: $O(d^n)$
  - AC-3: $O(cd^3)$ time, $O(c + n)$ space
- **Real-World Examples**: Map coloring, N-Queens, Sudoku, scheduling, robot task planning.
- **CSP vs CSO**: CSP finds any solution; CSO (Constraint Satisfaction Optimization) finds the best solution according to an objective function.
- **Commutativity Property**: Order of variable assignments does not affect reachability of solutions.
- **Standard Worked Examples to Revise**:
  - Australia map coloring (7 regions, 3 colors).
  - 4-Queens problem (16 nodes in full backtracking tree, 2 solutions).
  - AC-3 trace on a 3-variable CSP.
- **Board Exam Strategy**: Always state the CSP triple explicitly, draw the constraint graph, list heuristics used, and show all backtracking levels with pruning reasons.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adversarial Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Adversarial Search # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Adversarial Search** is a class of search algorithms used in Artificial Intelligence to find an optimal strategy for an agent (or player) operating in a **competitive multi-agent environment**, where the success of one agent is directly opposed by the actions of one or more rival agents. In the formal language of game theory, adversarial search operates on **game trees** that explicitly model the sequential decision-making between two or more rational, opposing players. The most foundational model is the **two-player, zero-sum, deterministic game of perfect information** (e.g., Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe, Checkers). The defining mathematical property of a zero-sum game is given by: $$u_A(s) + u_B(s) = C \quad \text{(where } C \text{ is a constant, typically 0)}$$ where $u_A(s)$ and $u_B(s)$ are the utility values of states for players A and B, respectively. If $C = 0$, then a gain for player A is exactly a loss for player B. ## 1.2 Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus | Term | Formal Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Game Tree** | A directed graph representing all possible sequences of moves from the initial state to terminal states. | | **Initial State** | The board configuration and identification of the player to move first. | | **Successor Function** | A function $\text{Moves}(s)$ returning the legal moves from state $s$. | | **Terminal Test** | A predicate $\text{Is-Terminal}(s)$ returning **true** if the game is over. | | **Utility Function** | A function $\text{Utility}(s, p)$ giving the final numeric value to player $p$ at a terminal state. | | **Optimal Strategy** | A policy $\pi^*(s)$ that guarantees the highest achievable utility against a perfect opponent. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Chess Grandmaster > [!NOTE] > **The "Two Minds, One Board" Analogy** > Imagine you are sitting at a chess board playing against a grandmaster. Before you move a pawn, you don't just think, *"What is my best move?"* — you think, *"What is my best move, given that my opponent will then play the move that is worst for me, and I will respond optimally to that, and so on?"* > > **Adversarial Search is exactly this recursive "look-ahead" thinking**, formalized into a tree of possibilities. Each branch is an opponent's countermove, and the algorithm computes the best outcome assuming the opponent is equally (and maximally) intelligent. ## 1.4 Why Adversarial Search Matters in Robotics (KTU 2024 Context) For the **PEBRT525 — AI for Robotics** course, adversarial search is foundational because: - **Robot Soccer / RoboCup** — Multi-robot teams must plan against an opposing team. - **Autonomous Racing** — Vehicles must anticipate adversarial lane-changes of rival cars. - **Warehouse Logistics** — A robot negotiating pick-up rights with another AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) is, in essence, solving a small adversarial game. - **Manipulation** — A robot arm in a crowded workspace must plan paths that adversarial human workers might block. ## 1.5 Assumptions of the Standard Adversarial Framework > [!WARNING] > Standard KTU adversarial search problems assume the following five axioms. Deviation from any of these shifts the algorithm family entirely: > 1. **Two players** taking alternate turns. > 2. **Zero-sum** outcome. > 3. **Deterministic** transitions (no randomness). > 4. **Perfect information** (no hidden cards or dice). > 5. **Bounded rationality** in practice — true optimal play is intractable for large trees, so we use **depth-limited search with evaluation functions**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Minimax Theorem (Foundational Pillar) The entire framework of adversarial search rests on **John von Neumann's Minimax Theorem (1928)**, which states that in any finite, two-player, zero-sum game with perfect information, there exists a **saddle point** (a pure or mixed strategy pair) such that neither player can improve their expected outcome by unilaterally changing their strategy. For deterministic, perfect-information games, this reduces to a single **pure strategy** known as the **Minimax Value**, denoted $V^*(s)$: $$V^*(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s, p) & \text{if } \text{Is-Terminal}(s) \\ \max_{a \in \text{Moves}(s)} V^*(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } p = \text{MAX} \\ \min_{a \in \text{Moves}(s)} V^*(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } p = \text{MIN} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.2 Properties of the Minimax Algorithm | Property | Value | Engineering Implication | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Completeness** | Yes (if tree is finite) | Guaranteed to find a solution if one exists. | | **Optimality** | Yes (against optimal opponent) | Best achievable against a perfect adversary. | | **Time Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ | Exponential in branching factor $b$ and depth $m$. | | **Space Complexity** | $O(bm)$ | Linear via depth-first recursion. | Where: - $b$ = **branching factor** (average number of legal moves per state). - $m$ = **maximum depth** of the game tree (in ply, i.e., half-moves). > [!NOTE] > **Ply vs. Move**: One *ply* is one player's turn; one *move* is a complete MAX-then-MIN pair. KTU questions often use "depth $d$" as plies, so a tree of depth $d$ contains $b^d$ leaves. ## 2.3 Alpha-Beta Pruning — The Practical Workhorse Pure minimax is computationally infeasible for games like Chess ($b \approx 35$, $m \approx 80$). **Alpha-Beta Pruning** eliminates subtrees that cannot influence the final decision, yielding identical results with a drastically reduced node count. **Two parameters** track bounds propagated down the tree: - $\alpha$ = the value of the **best (highest)** choice found so far **for MAX** along the path to the root. - $\beta$ = the value of the **best (lowest)** choice found so far **for MIN** along the path to the root. **Pruning Condition:** $$\text{If } \alpha \geq \beta, \text{ prune the remaining children of the current node.}$$ **Initialization:** $\alpha = -\infty$ and $\beta = +\infty$ at the root. ### Best-Case Complexity of Alpha-Beta | Ordering Quality | Effective Branching Factor | Nodes Examined | Time Complexity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Perfect ordering** | $b' = \sqrt{b}$ | $O(b^{m/2})$ | $O(b^{m/2})$ | | **Random ordering** | $b' \approx b^{3/4}$ | $O(b^{3m/4})$ | $O(b^{3m/4})$ | | **Worst case** | $b' = b$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ (no improvement) | > [!IMPORTANT] > Perfect ordering effectively **doubles the solvable depth** in the same time budget. This is why in practice, iterative deepening + transposition tables + move ordering (e.g., killer moves, history heuristic) are combined with alpha-beta. ## 2.4 Depth-Limited Search and Evaluation Functions Since real games have trees far too large to reach terminal states, we truncate the search at depth limit $d$ and apply a heuristic **Evaluation Function** $E(s)$ that estimates the true utility of a non-terminal state $s$. **Standard evaluation function for Chess-like games:** $$E(s) = w_1 \cdot M(s) + w_2 \cdot C(s) + w_3 \cdot K(s) + w_4 \cdot R(s)$$ where (for example): - $M(s)$ = material balance (pieces on the board weighted by piece values). - $C(s)$ = center control metric. - $K(s)$ = king safety indicator. - $R(s)$ = rook activity score. - $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = learned or tuned weights. The modified minimax with cutoff and evaluation becomes: $$V^*(s, d) = \begin{cases} \text{Evaluate}(s) & \text{if } d = 0 \text{ or } \text{Is-Terminal}(s) \\ \max_{a} V^*(\text{Result}(s,a), d-1) & \text{if MAX's turn} \\ \min_{a} V^*(\text{Result}(s,a), d-1) & \text{if MIN's turn} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.5 Real-World Utility (Beyond Board Games) | Domain | Adversarial Search Application | | :--- | :--- | | **Robocup (Soccer)** | Multi-agent adversarial planning with imperfect information. | | **Cybersecurity** | Attackers and defenders modeled as MIN/MAX in penetration games. | | **Algorithmic Trading** | Adapting strategies to adversarial market participants. | | **Defense Robotics** | Pursuit-evasion games with adversarial targets. | | **Autonomous Driving** | Negotiating intersections with human-driven cars. | ## 2.6 Compact KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Zero-sum game | $u_A(s) + u_B(s) = 0$ | | 2 | Minimax value (MAX node) | $V(s) = \max_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a))$ | | 3 | Minimax value (MIN node) | $V(s) = \min_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a))$ | | 4 | Minimax time | $O(b^m)$ | | 5 | Alpha-Beta prune trigger | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | | 6 | Alpha-Beta best case | $O(b^{m/2})$ | | 7 | Effective $b'$ (perfect order) | $\sqrt{b}$ | | 8 | Horizon effect | Caused by fixed depth cutoff ignoring opponent's threat past horizon. | | 9 | Quiescence search | Extension that resolves "noisy" volatile positions beyond cutoff. | | 10 | Singular Extension | Deep search along best move to catch horizon-effect blunders. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Minimax Computation Consider a depth-3 game tree (MAX at root, MIN at depth 1, MAX at depth 2, with terminal leaves at depth 3). Branching factor $b = 2$. **Terminal leaf values (left to right):** 3, 12, 2, 15, 5, 8, 6, 9. The minimax value is computed bottom-up, alternating min and max operations. ### Step 1 — Compute MAX-layer values at depth 2 (nodes D, E, F, G) $$V(D) = \max(3, 12) = 12$$ $$V(E) = \max(2, 15) = 15$$ $$V(F) = \max(5, 8) = 8$$ $$V(G) = \max(6, 9) = 9$$ ### Step 2 — Compute MIN-layer values at depth 1 (nodes B, C) $$V(B) = \min(V(D), V(E)) = \min(12, 15) = 12$$ $$V(C) = \min(V(F), V(G)) = \min(8, 9) = 8$$ ### Step 3 — Compute root value (MAX at depth 0, node A) $$V(A) = \max(V(B), V(C)) = \max(12, 8) = 12$$ ### Step 4 — Determine the optimal first move Since $V(B) = 12 > V(C) = 8$, the optimal move for MAX is to play towards node **B**, achieving a guaranteed utility of **12** against optimal play by MIN. **Result:** The minimax algorithm examines **all 8 leaves** and reports $V(A) = 12$, best move → B. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning on the Same Tree We use the **left-to-right traversal order** of the same tree. The convention used by KTU and Russell & Norvig is: - At every **MAX** node, update $\alpha \leftarrow \max(\alpha, v_{\text{current}})$. - At every **MIN** node, update $\beta \leftarrow \min(\beta, v_{\text{current}})$. - Prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$. ### Step 1 — Initialize at root A $$\alpha = -\infty, \quad \beta = +\infty$$ ### Step 2 — Descend to MIN node B (first child of A) Pass $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$ to B. - Descend to MAX node D (first child of B). - Pass $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$ to D. - **Visit leaf 3** (D's first child). D's running value = 3. Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$. - **Visit leaf 12** (D's second child). D's running value = 12. Update $\alpha = \max(3, 12) = 12$. - All D's children examined. Return $V(D) = 12$ to B. - B's running value = 12. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. So B now has $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = 12$. - Descend to MAX node E (second child of B) with $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = 12$. - **Visit leaf 2** (E's first child). E's running value = 2. Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 2) = 2$. - Check prune condition: $\alpha = 2 < \beta = 12$. **No prune. Continue.** - **Visit leaf 15** (E's second child). E's running value = 15. Update $\alpha = \max(2, 15) = 15$. - Return $V(E) = 15$ to B. - B's running value = $\min(12, 15) = 12$. $\beta$ remains 12. - All B's children examined. Return $V(B) = 12$ to A. ### Step 3 — Back at root A A's running value = 12. Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 12) = 12$. So A now has $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$. ### Step 4 — Descend to MIN node C (second child of A) with $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$ - Descend to MAX node F (first child of C) with $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$. - **Visit leaf 5** (F's first child). F's running value = 5. Update $\alpha = \max(12, 5) = 12$. - Check prune: $\alpha = 12 < \beta = +\infty$. **No prune. Continue.** - **Visit leaf 8** (F's second child). F's running value = 8. Update $\alpha = \max(12, 8) = 12$. - Return $V(F) = 12$ to C. - C's running value = 12. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. So C has $\alpha = 12, \beta = 12$. - Descend to MAX node G (second child of C) with $\alpha = 12, \beta = 12$. - **Check prune condition: $\alpha = 12 \geq \beta = 12$.** **PRUNE!** - G's subtree (leaves 6 and 9) is **never visited**. - C's value = 12. ### Step 5 — Final root computation $$V(A) = \max(V(B), V(C)) = \max(12, 12) = 12$$ ### Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison | Metric | Plain Minimax | Alpha-Beta (L→R) | Alpha-Beta (Perfect Order) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Leaves examined** | 8 | 7 | 4 | | **Total nodes visited** | 15 | 13 | 7 | | **Speedup factor** | 1.00× | 1.14× | 2.14× | | **Pruned leaves** | 0 | 1 (leaf 6, leaf 9) | 4 | > [!NOTE] > The maximum theoretical pruning gain is achieved when the **best moves are always examined first**. The general formula for the number of leaves examined under perfect ordering is $b^{\lceil m/2 \rceil} + b^{\lfloor m/2 \rfloor} - 1$, which approximates $O(b^{m/2})$. --- ## 3.3 Mathematical Justification of the Pruning Condition We now formally prove that pruning when $\alpha \geq \beta$ does not affect the correctness of the minimax value. **Proof Skeleton:** 1. Let $v$ be the value a MAX node is currently guaranteed (lower-bounded by $\alpha$). 2. Let $v'$ be the best value a sibling MIN node is guaranteed (upper-bounded by $\beta$). 3. If $\alpha \geq \beta$, then $v \geq v'$. 4. Therefore, the MIN ancestor will **never choose the path through this MAX node** because the sibling already offers a value $\leq v$. 5. Hence, exploring further descendants of this MAX node cannot change the minimax decision at the root. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Time-Complexity Derivation for Alpha-Beta (Perfect Order) For a tree of depth $m$ and branching factor $b$ under perfect move ordering: $$T(b, m) = \begin{cases} O(1) & \text{if } m = 0 \text{ or } b = 1 \\ b \cdot T(b, m-1) & \text{if symmetric} \\ O(b^{m/2}) & \text{under best-case ordering} \end{cases}$$ Under perfect ordering, the algorithm only needs to examine **one child fully** and can prune the rest after a single comparison. The recurrence becomes: $$T(b, m) = (b-1) + b \cdot T(b, m-2) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T(b, m) \approx b^{m/2} + b^{m/2} - 1$$ This is the **square root of the original** $b^m$, allowing exploration of **twice the depth** within the same time budget — a critical engineering fact for game-playing systems and adversarial robotic planners. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1 — Game Tree Structure (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional architecture of an adversarial game tree** with the same leaf values used in our worked example. It models MAX/MIN layering, leaf values, and the **alpha-beta pruning decision point** at node G. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["A : MAX Player — Root"]:::maxNode nodeB["B : MIN Player"]:::minNode nodeC["C : MIN Player"]:::minNode nodeD["D : MAX Player"]:::maxNode nodeE["E : MAX Player"]:::maxNode nodeF["F : MAX Player"]:::maxNode nodeG["G : MAX Player — PRUNED"]:::prunedNode leaf1["Leaf : 3"]:::terminal leaf2["Leaf : 12"]:::terminal leaf3["Leaf : 2"]:::terminal leaf4["Leaf : 15"]:::terminal leaf5["Leaf : 5"]:::terminal leaf6["Leaf : 8"]:::terminal leaf7["Leaf : 6 — NOT VISITED"]:::prunedLeaf leaf8["Leaf : 9 — NOT VISITED"]:::prunedLeaf nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> leaf1 nodeD --> leaf2 nodeE --> leaf3 nodeE --> leaf4 nodeF --> leaf5 nodeF --> leaf6 nodeG -.-> leaf7 nodeG -.-> leaf8 classDef maxNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B5651D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef minNode fill:#9CC2E5,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef terminal fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef prunedNode fill:#C00000,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef prunedLeaf fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Figure 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning Decision Process (Flowchart) This block diagram models the **recursive algorithmic flow** of alpha-beta pruning as executed by the minimax search procedure. Each block represents a logical decision or update, with explicit branches for pruning. ```mermaid graph TD startBlock["Start : State s, depth d, alpha, beta"]:::entry terminalCheck{"Is Terminal OR d = 0 ?"}:::decision evalBlock["Return Evaluation Function E(s)"]:::terminalOp playerCheck{"Which Player s Turn ?"}:::decision maxInit["Set v = minus infinity
alpha unchanged"]:::maxOp maxLoop["For each action a in Moves s"]:::maxOp maxRecurse["v = max( v, AlphaBeta( Result(s,a), d-1, alpha, beta ) )"]:::maxOp maxAlpha["alpha = max( alpha, v )"]:::maxOp maxPruneCheck{"alpha greater or equal beta ?"}:::pruneDecision maxBreak["BREAK : Prune remaining actions"]:::pruneOp minInit["Set v = plus infinity
beta unchanged"]:::minOp minLoop["For each action a in Moves s"]:::minOp minRecurse["v = min( v, AlphaBeta( Result(s,a), d-1, alpha, beta ) )"]:::minOp minBeta["beta = min( beta, v )"]:::minOp minPruneCheck{"alpha greater or equal beta ?"}:::pruneDecision minBreak["BREAK : Prune remaining actions"]:::pruneOp returnBlock["Return v to caller"]:::terminalOp startBlock --> terminalCheck terminalCheck -- YES --> evalBlock terminalCheck -- NO --> playerCheck playerCheck -- MAX --> maxInit playerCheck -- MIN --> minInit maxInit --> maxLoop maxLoop --> maxRecurse maxRecurse --> maxAlpha maxAlpha --> maxPruneCheck maxPruneCheck -- YES --> maxBreak maxPruneCheck -- NO --> maxLoop maxBreak --> returnBlock minInit --> minLoop minLoop --> minRecurse minRecurse --> minBeta minBeta --> minPruneCheck minPruneCheck -- YES --> minBreak minPruneCheck -- NO --> minLoop minBreak --> returnBlock evalBlock --> returnBlock classDef entry fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef maxOp fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef minOp fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef pruneDecision fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pruneOp fill:#C00000,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef terminalOp fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Figure 3 — The Horizon Effect (Conceptual Block Diagram) This diagram illustrates the **horizon effect**, a critical pitfall of fixed-depth adversarial search. A delayed opponent threat just beyond the depth limit causes a catastrophic move choice. ```mermaid graph TD hRoot["Root : MAX Player"]:::maxNode hSafe["Move 1 : Looks Safe at Depth d"]:::safeNode hBad["Move 2 : Hidden Mate-in-3 by Opponent"]:::badNode hDepth["Depth Limit d Reached"]:::cutoff hEval["Evaluation Function Returns PLUS Infinity"]:::evalBad hActual["True Outcome : Checkmate in 3 plies"]:::truth hRoot --> hSafe hRoot --> hBad hSafe --> hDepth hDepth --> hEval hBad --> hActual hEval -. FAILS .-> hActual classDef maxNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B5651D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef safeNode fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef badNode fill:#C00000,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef cutoff fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef evalBad fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef truth fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Horizon Diagram:** Because the evaluation function cannot see beyond depth $d$, it gives a falsely optimistic score to Move 1. The opponent's forced sequence (dashed line) leads to checkmate that the truncated search never noticed. The mitigation is **quiescence search** — extending the depth limit in *volatile* positions such as captures, checks, and threats. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]` **Define adversarial search. Explain the concept of a zero-sum game with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Adversarial Search** is a search technique used in multi-agent environments where the goal of one agent is in direct conflict with the goal of another agent. It is typically formalized on a **game tree** where each node represents a game state, and branches represent legal moves. A **zero-sum game** is one in which the total utility for all players is constant for every possible outcome. In a two-player zero-sum game with constant $C = 0$, the gain of one player is exactly equal to the loss of the other. Mathematically: $$u_A(s) + u_B(s) = 0 \quad \forall s \in \text{Terminal States}$$ **Example:** In a game of Chess, if White wins, Black loses — and the utility pair is $(+1, -1)$ or $(1, 0, -1)$ depending on the win/draw/loss convention. No third party is affected, and the sum is constant. *(1 Mark for definition, 1 Mark for the equation, 1 Mark for the example.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **What is the time complexity of the standard minimax algorithm? Show how alpha-beta pruning improves it in the best case.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The standard **minimax algorithm** explores the entire game tree. If the branching factor is $b$ and the maximum depth is $m$ (in plies), the time complexity is: $$T_{\text{minimax}} = O(b^m)$$ This is exponential, making it infeasible for large games like Chess where $b \approx 35$ and $m \approx 80$. **Alpha-Beta Pruning** eliminates subtrees that cannot influence the final minimax value. Under **perfect move ordering** (best move always examined first), the time complexity reduces to: $$T_{\alpha\beta}^{\text{best}} = O\!\left(b^{m/2}\right)$$ This effectively **doubles the search depth** achievable within the same time budget, since $b^{m/2}$ corresponds to the same computation as $b^{m'}$ with $m' = m/2$ plain minimax. *(1 Mark for $O(b^m)$, 1 Mark for $O(b^{m/2})$, 1 Mark for the "doubles depth" interpretation.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEBRT525]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Minimax algorithm** in detail. Outline its recursive structure, optimality conditions, and computational complexity. State clearly when and why the algorithm is **complete** and **optimal**. **Model Solution:** **Definition and Setup (2 Marks):** The Minimax algorithm computes the optimal move for MAX in a two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information, deterministic game. It assigns a minimax value $V(s)$ to every state $s$ recursively, assuming both players play optimally. **Recursive Definition (3 Marks):** $$V(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s) & \text{if } \text{Is-Terminal}(s) \\ \max_{a \in \text{Moves}(s)} V(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if MAX to move} \\ \min_{a \in \text{Moves}(s)} V(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if MIN to move} \end{cases}$$ The algorithm implements a **depth-first traversal** of the game tree. At MAX nodes, it backs up the **maximum** of successor values; at MIN nodes, the **minimum**. The move achieving the backed-up value at the root is the optimal move. **Properties (2 Marks):** - **Completeness:** Yes, if the tree is finite. - **Optimality:** Yes, against an optimal opponent (it returns the *game-theoretic value*). - **Time Complexity:** $O(b^m)$ where $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = maximum depth in plies. - **Space Complexity:** $O(bm)$ using depth-first recursion. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to specify *against an optimal opponent* when stating optimality. Partial credit (½ mark) is given if this qualifier is missing. Also, do not confuse $m$ (plies) with $n$ (moves); using the wrong unit loses 1 mark. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the game tree below where MAX plays at the root and depths alternate MAX/MIN. Terminal leaf values from left to right are: **3, 12, 2, 15, 5, 8, 6, 9**. The tree structure is: - Root A (MAX) has children B, C - B (MIN) has children D, E - C (MIN) has children F, G - D, E, F, G (MAX) each have two terminal leaf children **Apply the alpha-beta pruning algorithm in a left-to-right traversal. List all pruned leaf nodes. State the final minimax value and the optimal first move.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initialize (½ Mark):** At root A: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. **Step 2 — Explore subtree under B (1½ Marks):** - At B, pass $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$. - Explore D: leaves 3, 12 visited. $V(D) = \max(3, 12) = 12$. Return to B. - Update B: $v = 12$, $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. - Explore E with $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = 12$: leaf 2 visited. $v_E$ so far = 2. Prune check: $2 < 12$, continue. Leaf 15 visited. $V(E) = \max(2, 15) = 15$. Return to B. - $V(B) = \min(12, 15) = 12$. **Step 3 — Back at root A (1 Mark):** - $v_A = 12$. $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 12) = 12$. **Step 4 — Explore subtree under C with $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$ (2½ Marks):** - Explore F with $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$: leaf 5 visited. $v_F = 5$. $\alpha = \max(12, 5) = 12$. Prune check: $12 < +\infty$, continue. Leaf 8 visited. $V(F) = \max(5, 8) = 8$. Return to C. - Update C: $v = 8$, $\beta = \min(+\infty, 8) = 8$. - Explore G with $\alpha = 12, \beta = 8$: **PRUNE CHECK TRIGGERED**. Since $\alpha = 12 \geq \beta = 8$, **prune G's children (leaves 6 and 9) entirely**. - $V(C) = 8$ (from F). **Step 5 — Final root computation (1 Mark):** $$V(A) = \max(V(B), V(C)) = \max(12, 8) = 12$$ **Step 6 — Conclusion (½ Mark):** - **Pruned leaves:** 6 and 9. - **Final minimax value:** 12. - **Optimal first move for MAX:** Move to node **B** (which yields the higher backed-up value of 12). **Performance summary:** 6 of 8 leaves examined; 2 leaves pruned — a 25% reduction in leaf evaluations. --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEBRT525 — Alternative Choice]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major **types of games** encountered in AI and robotics. For each type, give one example and explain how the choice of search algorithm changes. **Model Solution:** **1. Deterministic vs. Stochastic Games (3 Marks)** | Type | Definition | Example | Search Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Deterministic** | Outcomes fully determined by moves | Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe | Minimax, Alpha-Beta | | **Stochastic** | Outcomes involve chance (dice, shuffled cards) | Backgammon, Monopoly, Robot in noisy environment | Expectimax, Expectiminimax | For stochastic games, the chance nodes are evaluated by **expected value** rather than min or max: $$V_{\text{chance}}(s) = \sum_{r} P(r) \cdot V(\text{Result}(s, r))$$ **2. Perfect vs. Imperfect Information (2 Marks)** - **Perfect Information:** All relevant state variables visible (e.g., Chess, Go). - **Imperfect Information:** Hidden information exists (e.g., Poker, Bridge, multi-robot exploration with occluded sensors). - Search strategy: **Information sets**; algorithms include **determinization** (assume each hidden state is the true one) and **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)**. **3. Zero-Sum vs. General-Sum (2 Marks)** - **Zero-Sum:** Win of one is loss of another → Minimax applies. - **General-Sum / Cooperative:** Players may have partially aligned goals → **Nash Equilibrium** search, mechanism design, multi-agent RL. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Backgammon is deterministic" — **wrong**. The dice rolls are stochastic and must be modeled with chance nodes. Confusing imperfect information with stochastic games is a frequent error. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **evaluation functions** used in depth-limited adversarial search. State the desirable properties of a good evaluation function. Discuss the **horizon effect** and how **quiescence search** mitigates it. **Model Solution:** **Role of Evaluation Functions (2 Marks):** When the game tree is too large to expand to terminal states, search is cut off at a fixed depth $d$. The **evaluation function** $E(s)$ estimates the true game-theoretic value of a non-terminal state. The backed-up minimax value becomes: $$V(s, d) \approx E(s) \text{ if cutoff triggered at depth } d$$ **Desirable Properties (2 Marks):** 1. **Order-preserving:** States that are clearly winning for MAX should yield higher $E(s)$ than clearly losing ones. 2. **Computationally cheap:** Must be evaluated in microseconds at millions of nodes. 3. **Smooth / Non-sparse:** Small changes in state should produce small changes in $E(s)$, so search behavior is stable. 4. **Endgame-accurate:** $E(s) = \text{Utility}(s)$ for terminal states. 5. **Domain-specific:** Encodes human knowledge (chess: material, mobility, king safety) **or** is learned (deep value networks in AlphaZero). **Horizon Effect (2 Marks):** The **horizon effect** occurs when a fixed-depth search fails to "see" an opponent threat that is delayed past the depth cutoff. The player mistakenly believes a position is safe, when in reality the opponent can force a loss just beyond the horizon. *(See the conceptual block diagram in Section 4.3.)* **Quiescence Search (1 Mark):** **Quiescence search** extends the depth limit in *volatile* positions — those involving captures, checks, promotions, or direct threats — until the position becomes **quiescent** (no major tactical instability). This prevents the evaluation function from being applied to "noisy" mid-tactic states where the heuristic is unreliable. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked to write an evaluation function for Tic-Tac-Toe, students sometimes use $E(s) = \text{number of my marks} - \text{number of opponent marks}$. This is **incorrect** for Tic-Tac-Toe (since terminal scoring already handles that), but is the correct idea for Chess. Always tie your evaluation function to the *true utility* of the game. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Adversarial Search** = search in competitive multi-agent settings; formalized on game trees. - The **zero-sum** property means $u_A(s) + u_B(s) = 0$ (or constant), enabling the minimax formulation. - The **Minimax value** is computed by alternating max (at MAX ply) and min (at MIN ply) up the tree. - **Minimax is complete and optimal against an optimal opponent**, with time $O(b^m)$ and space $O(bm)$. - **Alpha-Beta Pruning** uses two parameters — $\alpha$ (best for MAX so far) and $\beta$ (best for MIN so far) — and prunes when $\alpha \geq \beta$. - Under **perfect move ordering**, alpha-beta runs in $O(b^{m/2})$, doubling the effective searchable depth. - **Depth-limited search** uses an **evaluation function** $E(s)$ as a heuristic stand-in for terminal utility. - A good evaluation function must be **order-preserving, fast, smooth, endgame-accurate, and domain-specific**. - The **horizon effect** = a fixed cutoff hides delayed threats; mitigated by **quiescence search** and **singular extension**. - **Stochastic games** (Backgammon) require **Expectimax** — replace min/max with **expected value** over chance nodes. - **Imperfect information games** (Poker, robotic exploration) require **information sets, determinization, or Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)**. - **Real robotics applications** of adversarial search include RoboCup soccer, pursuit-evasion, intersection negotiation, and adversarial manipulation. - In alpha-beta, **always check pruning at every node** — partial pruning credit is **not** awarded. - The **optimal first move** is the child of the root whose backed-up minimax value equals $V(\text{root})$.

topics.title: Knowledge Representation and First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Knowledge Representation and First Order Logic # 📘 Knowledge Representation and First Order Logic — Module 2 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Knowledge Representation (KR)? **Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the formal field of Artificial Intelligence concerned with the design of data structures and inference mechanisms that allow a machine (especially a robot) to store, organize, reason over, and act upon information about the world in a way that resembles human-like reasoning. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > *Knowledge Representation is the methodology by which intelligent agents encode real-world information into a formal, machine-tractable format that supports logical inference, planning, and decision-making.* In a **robotic context**, KR is the "brain memory" that allows a robot to know: - *What objects exist in the environment?* - *Where they are located?* - *What actions are safe to perform?* - *How to plan a path from A to B?* --- ### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Knowledge (Russell & Norvig Framework) | Pillar | Type | Example | |---|---|---| | **Objects** | Facts about things | `Robot R1 is at location L1` | | **Events** | Facts about actions | `R1 moved from L1 to L2 at time t=5s` | | **Performance** | Meta-knowledge | `Knowledge about how to use other knowledge` | | **Meta-knowledge** | Knowledge about knowledge | `Knowing that I don't know` | > [!NOTE] > **Kantian Analogy:** Just as a human baby first learns the *names* of objects (mama, ball, dog) before learning the *rules* governing them, a robotic agent first represents **facts** and then learns the **logic** to manipulate those facts. --- ### 1.3 Real-World Intuition — The "Smart Library" Analogy Imagine a **smart library**: - **Books** = Pieces of knowledge (facts) - **Catalog cards** = Structured representation of those books - **Librarian** = The inference engine - **Reading rules** = The logic (FOL rules) Without proper cataloging (representation), even the best librarian (inference engine) cannot retrieve the right book. This is precisely why **good representation precedes good reasoning** — the foundational AI principle. --- ### 1.4 Requirements of a Good KR System (KTU Exam Favorite ❤) A good knowledge representation scheme must satisfy the following properties: 1. **Representational Adequacy** — Ability to represent all kinds of required knowledge. 2. **Inferential Adequacy** — Ability to manipulate the representation to derive new structures. 3. **Inferential Efficiency** — Ability to incorporate additional information that helps focus the inference. 4. **Acquisitional Efficiency** — Ability to acquire new knowledge easily through automated or semi-automated means. --- ### 1.5 Introduction to First-Order Logic (FOL) **First-Order Logic (FOL)**, also called **Predicate Logic** or **First-Order Predicate Calculus (FOPC)**, is the most widely used knowledge representation language in AI. It extends propositional logic by introducing: - **Objects** (terms) - **Predicates** (relations) - **Quantifiers** (∀, ∃) - **Functions** > [!NOTE] > **Why FOL for Robots?** > Propositional logic cannot express general statements like *"All robots have sensors"*. FOL can: $\forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasSensor}(x))$. --- ### 1.6 The Knowledge-Based Agent (KBA) Architecture A robot's knowledge base works in a **Tell-Ask** cycle: - **TELL:** Add new sentences (knowledge) to the KB. - **ASK:** Query the KB to know what to do. - **Inference Engine:** Derives new sentences from existing ones using logical rules. $$\text{Knowledge Base (KB)} = \text{Sentences in FOL} + \text{Inference Engine (Algorithms)}$$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Knowledge Representation Schemes KTU examiners love asking: *"Compare different KR schemes."* Memorize the table below. | KR Scheme | Structure | Best For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Propositional Logic** | Flat boolean variables | Simple true/false facts | Cannot represent objects/relations | | **First-Order Logic (FOL)** | Quantified predicates | General reasoning, robotics | Undecidable in general | | **Semantic Networks** | Graph (nodes + labeled edges) | Concept hierarchies | No standard semantics | | **Frames** | Object-oriented slots & fillers | Structured object descriptions | Inheritance paradoxes | | **Conceptual Graphs** | Bipartite graph | Natural language semantics | Computational complexity | | **Production Rules** | IF-THEN rules | Expert systems | Poor at deep reasoning | | **Ontologies (OWL)** | Hierarchical class definitions | Web semantics, IoT | Heavy to construct | --- ## 2.2 Syntactic Elements of First-Order Logic ### 2.2.1 Basic Symbols (The Alphabet of FOL) - **Constants:** Concrete objects → `R1, John, 5, L2` - **Variables:** Placeholders → `x, y, z` - **Predicates:** Properties/relations → `At(R1, L2)`, `Red(Ball)` - **Functions:** Map objects to objects → `Distance(origin, x)` - **Connectives:** $\land, \lor, \neg, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ - **Equality:** $=$ - **Quantifiers:** $\forall$ (universal), $\exists$ (existential) ### 2.2.2 The Atomic Sentence An **atomic sentence** is a predicate symbol applied to a tuple of terms: $$P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$$ Example: $\text{At}(\text{Robot1}, \text{Lab2})$ — "Robot1 is at Lab2". --- ## 2.3 The Two Master Quantifiers ### 2.3.1 Universal Quantifier (∀) — "For All" $$\forall x \, P(x) \equiv \text{"For every x, P(x) is true"}$$ > **Robotics Example:** $\forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasSensor}(x, \text{Lidar}))$ > *"Every robot has a LiDAR sensor."* ### 2.3.2 Existential Quantifier (∃) — "There Exists" $$\exists x \, P(x) \equiv \text{"There exists at least one x such that P(x) is true"}$$ > **Robotics Example:** $\exists x \, (\text{Obstacle}(x) \land \text{InFrontOf}(x, \text{Robot1}))$ > *"There is an obstacle in front of Robot1."* ### 2.3.3 The Famous Quantifier Duality Laws $$\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$ $$\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trick Question (Frequently Asked):** > *"Express 'Not all robots are autonomous' in two ways."* > Answer: $\neg \forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{Autonomous}(x)) \equiv \exists x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \land \neg \text{Autonomous}(x))$ --- ## 2.4 De Morgan's Laws for Quantifiers (KTU High-Yield Table) | Propositional Form | Quantified FOL Form | |---|---| | $\neg (P \land Q)$ | $\neg P \lor \neg Q$ | | $\neg (P \lor Q)$ | $\neg P \land \neg Q$ | | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | $\neg \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | $\neg \forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Universal quantifier | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | True iff P(x) true for **every** x in domain | | 2 | Existential quantifier | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | True iff P(x) true for **at least one** x | | 3 | Quantifier duality 1 | $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | Negation swaps quantifier & flips predicate | | 4 | Quantifier duality 2 | $\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | Same rule applied to ∃ | | 5 | Modus Ponens | $\frac{P, \; P \rightarrow Q}{Q}$ | Core inference rule | | 6 | Modus Tollens | $\frac{\neg Q, \; P \rightarrow Q}{\neg P}$ | Contrapositive reasoning | | 7 | Universal Elimination (UE) | $\frac{\forall x \, P(x)}{P(c)}$ | Substitute any constant `c` | | 8 | Existential Introduction (EI) | $\frac{P(c)}{\exists x \, P(x)}$ | Lift constant to variable | | 9 | Unification condition | $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(p, q)$ | $\theta$ = most general unifier (MGU) | | 10 | Forward Chaining | Data-driven | Starts from known facts | | 11 | Backward Chaining | Goal-driven | Starts from query, works backward | --- ## 2.6 Inference Rules for FOL (The Holy Quartet) ### Rule 1: Universal Elimination (Instantiation) $$\frac{\forall x \, P(x)}{P(c)} \quad \text{where c is any ground term}$$ *Example:* From $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasMotor}(x)$, infer $\text{Robot}(\text{R1}) \rightarrow \text{HasMotor}(\text{R1})$. ### Rule 2: Existential Elimination $$\frac{\exists x \, P(x)}{P(c)} \quad \text{where c is a fresh Skolem constant}$$ *Example:* From $\exists x \, \text{Obstacle}(x)$, infer $\text{Obstacle}(c)$ where $c$ is a new name (e.g., $O_{17}$). ### Rule 3: Existential Introduction $$\frac{P(c)}{\exists x \, P(x)} \quad \text{where c is any term}$$ ### Rule 4: Universal Introduction $$\frac{P(c)}{\forall x \, P(x)} \quad \text{where c is arbitrary (not named in KB)}$$ --- ## 2.7 Unification — The Heart of FOL Inference **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ (theta) that makes two logical expressions identical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965):** > 1. If both expressions are constants, unify only if they are equal. > 2. If first is a variable, substitute it with the second. > 3. If both are function/predicate applications, unify arguments pairwise. > 4. **Occur-check:** A variable cannot be bound to a term containing itself (e.g., $x$ cannot be bound to $f(x)$). ### 2.7.1 Worked Example: Compute UNIFY **Find $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(P(x, f(y)), P(a, f(b)))$:** | Step | Expression 1 | Expression 2 | Substitution Update | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P(x, f(y))$ | $P(a, f(b))$ | $\theta = \{\}$ | | 2 | $x$ | $a$ | $\theta = \{x/a\}$ | | 3 | $f(y)$ | $f(b)$ | $\theta = \{x/a,\; y/b\}$ | | 4 | $y$ | $b$ | $\theta = \{x/a,\; y/b\}$ ✓ | **Result:** $\theta = \{x/a, y/b\}$ — the Most General Unifier (MGU). --- ## 2.8 Engineering Utility of FOL in Robotics | Robotics Sub-domain | FOL Application | |---|---| | **Autonomous Navigation** | $\text{At}(R, L_1) \land \text{Connected}(L_1, L_2) \rightarrow \text{CanMove}(R, L_2)$ | | **Human-Robot Interaction** | $\text{Says}(\text{Human}, \text{Stop}) \land \text{Trustworthy}(\text{Human}) \rightarrow \text{Stop}(R)$ | | **Multi-Agent Systems** | $\forall x, y \, \text{ConflictingGoal}(x, y) \rightarrow \text{Negotiate}(x, y)$ | | **Fault Diagnosis** | $\text{Sensor}(s) \land \text{Failed}(s) \rightarrow \text{Unreliable}(\text{Reading}(s))$ | | **Task Planning** | $\forall x \, (\text{Block}(x) \land \text{Clear}(x)) \rightarrow \text{CanPickup}(\text{Arm}, x)$ | > [!NOTE] > Modern **industrial robots** (e.g., KUKA, FANUC) and **autonomous vehicles** (Waymo, Tesla FSD) use hybrid systems — FOL for high-level reasoning + neural networks for perception. This is the **neuro-symbolic AI** paradigm. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Translating English to FOL (KTU Repeated Pattern) > **Statement (Robotics Context):** > *"Every robot that is in the lab has at least one sensor. The mobile robot R1 is in the lab. Therefore, R1 has at least one sensor."* ### Step 1: Identify Vocabulary - **Objects/Constants:** R1, Lab - **Predicates:** $R(x) \equiv \text{Robot}(x)$, $L(x) \equiv \text{InLab}(x)$, $S(x) \equiv \text{HasSensor}(x)$ ### Step 2: Translate the Premises **Premise 1:** "Every robot that is in the lab has at least one sensor." **Step 2a:** Identify the universal pattern — "Every X has Y" → $\forall x$. **Step 2b:** Identify the existential pattern — "at least one" → $\exists y$. The translated FOL form is: $$\forall x \, \big( R(x) \land L(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, S(y, x) \big)$$ **Premise 2:** "The mobile robot R1 is in the lab." $$L(R1)$$ ### Step 3: Apply Universal Elimination (UE) From Premise 1, instantiate $x = R1$: $$R(R1) \land L(R1) \rightarrow \exists y \, S(y, R1)$$ ### Step 4: Apply Modus Ponens (MP) Given $L(R1)$ is true, and assuming $R(R1)$ is also known (or implicit), we obtain: $$\exists y \, S(y, R1)$$ ### Step 5: Apply Existential Elimination (EE) Introduce Skolem constant $s_1$: $$S(s_1, R1)$$ **Conclusion:** *"R1 has at least one sensor."* — Proved in FOL. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Inference Chain with Unification > **Knowledge Base (KB):** > - Rule 1: $\forall x, y \, \text{King}(x) \land \text{Greedy}(x) \rightarrow \text{Evil}(x)$ > - Rule 2: $\forall x \, \text{Greedy}(x)$ > - Fact: $\text{King}(\text{John})$ > **Query:** Is John Evil? — i.e., prove $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$. ### Forward Chaining Trace **Step 1:** Start from facts. We have $\text{King}(\text{John})$ and $\forall x \, \text{Greedy}(x)$. **Step 2:** Apply UE to Rule 2 with $x = \text{John}$: $$\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$$ **Step 3:** Now we have both $\text{King}(\text{John})$ and $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$. **Step 4:** Apply UE to Rule 1 with $x = \text{John}, y = \text{John}$: $$\text{King}(\text{John}) \land \text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \rightarrow \text{Evil}(\text{John})$$ **Step 5:** Apply Modus Ponens: $$\therefore \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Unification with Nested Functions > **Task:** Find MGU of $Q(f(a, g(x)), y)$ and $Q(f(h(b), g(c)), d)$ | Step | Term 1 | Term 2 | Decision | Substitution | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q$ | $Q$ | Same predicate, proceed | $\theta = \{\}$ | | 2 | $f(a, g(x))$ | $f(h(b), g(c))$ | Same functor, recurse | $\theta = \{\}$ | | 3 | $a$ | $h(b)$ | Constants differ, no var | **FAIL ✗** | **Result:** UNIFY fails because $a$ and $h(b)$ are different constants. --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Skolemization > **Statement:** "Every person has a mother." > $$\forall x \, \text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, \text{Mother}(y, x)$$ **Step 1:** Eliminate $\exists$ by replacing $y$ with a function of $x$ (Skolem function $m(x)$): $$\forall x \, \text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mother}(m(x), x)$$ **Step 2:** Drop the universal quantifier (implicit in CNF): $$\text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mother}(m(x), x)$$ **Step 3:** Convert to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF): $$\neg \text{Person}(x) \lor \text{Mother}(m(x), x)$$ --- ## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example: Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** is the most powerful single inference rule for FOL: $$\frac{p_1', \; p_2', \; \ldots, \; p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \ldots \land p_n \rightarrow q)}{\text{UNIFY}(p_i', p_i) = \theta_i \text{ for each } i \quad \therefore q\theta_1\theta_2 \cdots \theta_n}$$ > **Worked Example:** Given: - $p_1' : \text{King}(\text{John})$, $p_2' : \text{Greedy}(\text{John})$ - Rule: $\text{King}(x) \land \text{Greedy}(x) \rightarrow \text{Evil}(x)$ **Step 1:** Find $\theta_1$ for $p_1'$ and $\text{King}(x)$: $$\theta_1 = \{x / \text{John}\}$$ **Step 2:** Find $\theta_2$ for $p_2'$ and $\text{Greedy}(x)$: $$\theta_2 = \{x / \text{John}\}$$ **Step 3:** Apply substitutions to $q$: $$q \theta_1 \theta_2 = \text{Evil}(x) \cdot \{x/\text{John}\} = \text{Evil}(\text{John})$$ **Step 4:** Conclude: $$\therefore \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.6 The Resolution Refutation (Proof-by-Contradiction) > [!NOTE] > **Why Resolution?** > Resolution is **complete** for FOL — if a conclusion follows from the KB, resolution will find it. This is the basis of **PROLOG** and **Theorem Provers**. ### Algorithm: Resolution 1. Convert all KB sentences to **CNF** (Conjunctive Normal Form). 2. Negate the goal/query sentence. 3. Apply the **resolution rule** repeatedly until either: - Empty clause $\square$ is derived (proof successful), or - No new clauses can be derived (proof failed). **Resolution Rule (Single Literal):** $$\frac{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_i, \quad m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_j}{(\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \cdots \lor \ell_n \lor m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \cdots \lor m_k) \theta}$$ where $\ell_i$ and $\neg m_j$ unify with MGU $\theta$. ### Worked Resolution Example > **KB:** > - (i) $\text{Robot}(R1)$ > - (ii) $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasSensor}(x)$ > - (iii) $\forall x \, \text{HasSensor}(x) \rightarrow \text{Navigates}(x)$ > **Goal:** Prove $\text{Navigates}(R1)$. **Step 1:** Convert (ii) to CNF: $$\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{HasSensor}(x)$$ **Step 2:** Convert (iii) to CNF: $$\neg \text{HasSensor}(x) \lor \text{Navigates}(x)$$ **Step 3:** Negate goal: $$\neg \text{Navigates}(R1)$$ **Step 4:** Resolve (iii) CNF with negated goal using $\theta = \{x/R1\}$: $$\neg \text{HasSensor}(R1) \lor \text{Navigates}(R1) \quad \text{and} \quad \neg \text{Navigates}(R1)$$ $$\therefore \neg \text{HasSensor}(R1)$$ **Step 5:** Resolve (ii) CNF with $\neg \text{HasSensor}(R1)$ using $\theta = \{x/R1\}$: $$\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{HasSensor}(x) \quad \text{and} \quad \neg \text{HasSensor}(R1)$$ $$\therefore \neg \text{Robot}(R1)$$ **Step 6:** Resolve fact (i) with $\neg \text{Robot}(R1)$: $$\text{Robot}(R1) \quad \text{and} \quad \neg \text{Robot}(R1)$$ $$\therefore \square \text{ (empty clause — CONTRADICTION FOUND)}$$ **Conclusion:** Goal proven by refutation. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.7 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining (Analytical Matrix) | Parameter | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | |---|---|---| | **Direction** | Data → Goal | Goal → Data | | **Strategy** | Eager / Bottom-up | Lazy / Top-down | | **Trigger** | New fact added | New query asked | | **Output** | All derivable facts | Only answers to query | | **Completeness** | Yes (for Datalog) | Yes (for Datalog) | | **Efficiency** | May derive irrelevant facts | Focused, often faster | | **Best For** | Monitoring, reactive systems | Query answering, expert systems | | **Real Example** | CLIPS, Production Systems | PROLOG, SQL query planners | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Forward = Facts Forward (push); Backward = Backward-reason from the goal (pull)."* # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of a Knowledge-Based Agent (Robot Brain) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Perception Module
Sensors Input] --> B[Knowledge Base
FOL Sentences] C[Learning Module
Induction] --> B B --> D[Inference Engine
Resolution + Unification] D --> E[Reasoning Output
Derived Facts] E --> F[Action Selection
Utility Module] F --> G[Actuator Commands
Motors and Effectors] G --> H[Physical Robot World] H --> A ``` --- ## 4.2 Unification Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Start: Two FOL Expressions P and Q] --> Q{Functor
Equal?} Q -- No --> R[Return FAIL] Q -- Yes --> S{Number of
Args Equal?} S -- No --> R S -- Yes --> T[Apply UNIFY to
arg1 vs arg1] T --> U{All args
unified?} U -- No --> R U -- Yes --> V[Return MGU theta] R --> W[Stop with FAILURE] V --> X[Stop with SUCCESS] ``` --- ## 4.3 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FC[Forward Chaining - Data Driven] F1[Facts in KB] --> F2[Match Rule Premises] F2 --> F3[Apply UE and MP] F3 --> F4[Derive New Fact] F4 --> F1 end subgraph BC[Backward Chaining - Goal Driven] B1[Query: Goal G] --> B2[Find Rule with G in Head] B2 --> B3[Set Sub-goals to Premises] B3 --> B4{Sub-goals
in KB?} B4 -- No --> B2 B4 -- Yes --> B5[Return YES to Goal G] end ``` --- ## 4.4 FOL Syntax Hierarchy Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Z[FOL Sentence] --> Z1[Atomic Sentence
Predicate terms] Z --> Z2[Complex Sentence
Connectives] Z1 --> Z11[Predicate Symbol] Z1 --> Z12[Term] Z12 --> Z121[Function Symbol] Z12 --> Z122[Constant] Z12 --> Z123[Variable] Z2 --> Z21[Universal forall x] Z2 --> Z22[Existential exists x] Z2 --> Z23[Not, AND, OR, Implies, IFF] ``` --- ## 4.5 Resolution Refutation Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: KB plus Goal G] --> B[Convert KB to CNF] B --> C[Negate Goal: NOT G] C --> D[Add NOT G to Clause Set] D --> E[Loop: Pick Two Resolvable Clauses] E --> F{Resolution
Possible?} F -- No --> G[No Proof: FAIL] F -- Yes --> H[Compute Resolvent] H --> I{Empty Clause
Derived?} I -- Yes --> J[Proof Success: G is TRUE] I -- No --> K[Add Resolvent to Clause Set] K --> E ``` --- ## 4.6 Semantic Network Example (Robot World) ```mermaid graph TD N1[Robot] -->|ISA| N2[Agent] N1 -->|HAS| N3[Sensor] N1 -->|HAS| N4[Actuator] N1 -->|HAS| N5[Battery] N3 -->|ISA| N6[Lidar] N3 -->|ISA| N7[Camera] N5 -->|POWERS| N1 N8[MobileRobot] -->|ISA| N1 N9[IndustrialArm] -->|ISA| N1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Semantic networks lack a **formal semantics** — meaning is implicit in the structure. This is why FOL is preferred for rigorous robotics applications. --- ## 4.7 Frame-Based Representation (Robot Object Frame) ```mermaid graph TD FR[Frame: RobotR1] --> S1[Slot: type = MobileRobot] FR --> S2[Slot: sensors = Lidar + Camera] FR --> S3[Slot: batteryLevel = 87 percent] FR --> S4[Slot: location = Lab2] FR --> S5[Slot: status = Active] FR --> M1[Method: moveTo loc - Updates location] FR --> M2[Method: charge - Increases batteryLevel] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define *Knowledge Representation* in the context of AI for robotics. List any **four** requirements of a good KR scheme. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Knowledge Representation (KR) is the formal methodology used in Artificial Intelligence to encode real-world information into a machine-tractable format that supports reasoning, planning, and decision-making in intelligent agents such as autonomous robots. **Four requirements of a good KR scheme (½ mark each):** 1. **Representational Adequacy** — must represent all kinds of knowledge. 2. **Inferential Adequacy** — must support derivation of new knowledge. 3. **Inferential Efficiency** — must allow focused reasoning using heuristics. 4. **Acquisitional Efficiency** — must allow easy addition of new knowledge. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State the **quantifier duality laws** of First-Order Logic and explain their significance with an example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The two quantifier duality laws are: $$\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$ $$\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$ **Significance:** These laws allow us to push negations inside quantifiers, converting statements into a normalized form suitable for inference algorithms such as resolution and Skolemization. **Example (1 mark):** $\neg \forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{Autonomous}(x) \equiv \exists x \, \text{Robot}(x) \land \neg \text{Autonomous}(x)$ — *"There exists a robot that is not autonomous."* --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** What is **First-Order Logic (FOL)**? Compare FOL with Propositional Logic on at least **four** parameters. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Represent the following statements in FOL and then apply **Forward Chaining** to derive the conclusion. *(7 marks)* > *Premise 1: All robots in the lab have sensors.* > *Premise 2: All sensors enable navigation.* > *Premise 3: R1 is a robot in the lab.* > *Conclusion: R1 can navigate.* --- #### 📘 Model Solution ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 marks):** First-Order Logic (FOL) is a knowledge representation language that extends propositional logic by introducing **objects**, **predicates**, **functions**, and **quantifiers** (∀, ∃), enabling the representation of general statements and relations among objects in a domain. **Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per row):** | Parameter | Propositional Logic | First-Order Logic | |---|---|---| | **Representation Unit** | Boolean variables (P, Q) | Predicates with terms (At(x, y)) | | **Expressiveness** | Limited (facts only) | Rich (general rules, relations) | | **Quantification** | None | $\forall$ and $\exists$ | | **Inference Complexity** | NP-complete | Semi-decidable (undecidable in general) | | **Domain** | Boolean world | Objects and relations | | **Example** | $\text{Raining}$ | $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasSensor}(x)$ | **Conclusion (1 mark):** FOL is strictly more expressive than propositional logic and is the standard language for representing general knowledge in AI-based robotic systems. --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Identify Vocabulary (½ mark)** - Constants: $R1, Lab$ - Predicates: $R(x) = \text{Robot}(x)$, $L(x) = \text{InLab}(x)$, $S(x) = \text{HasSensor}(x)$, $N(x) = \text{Navigates}(x)$ **Step 2: Translate Premises to FOL (2 marks)** Premise 1: $$\forall x \, (R(x) \land L(x) \rightarrow S(x))$$ Premise 2: $$\forall x \, (S(x) \rightarrow N(x))$$ Premise 3: $$R(R1) \land L(R1)$$ **Step 3: Apply Universal Elimination (UE) to Premise 1 with $x = R1$ (1 mark):** $$R(R1) \land L(R1) \rightarrow S(R1)$$ **Step 4: Apply Modus Ponens with Premise 3 (1 mark):** $$S(R1)$$ **Step 5: Apply UE to Premise 2 with $x = R1$ (1 mark):** $$S(R1) \rightarrow N(R1)$$ **Step 6: Apply Modus Ponens (1 mark):** $$\therefore N(R1)$$ **Conclusion:** R1 can navigate. $\blacksquare$ --- ### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the **Unification algorithm** in FOL with a suitable example. What is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**? *(7 marks)* **(b)** What is **Resolution** in First-Order Logic? Convert the following sentences to **CNF** and prove the goal using resolution refutation. *(7 marks)* > *P1: $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasSensor}(x)$* > *P2: $\forall x \, \text{HasSensor}(x) \rightarrow \text{CanNavigate}(x)$* > *P3: $\text{Robot}(\text{Atlas})$* > *Goal: $\text{CanNavigate}(\text{Atlas})$* --- #### 📘 Model Solution ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 marks):** Unification is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution** $\theta$ that makes two first-order logical expressions syntactically identical. The substitution that imposes the fewest constraints is called the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**. **Algorithm Steps (2 marks):** 1. Recursively compare predicate symbols and their arity. 2. If a variable is encountered, bind it to the corresponding term (unless already bound — then apply the existing binding). 3. **Occur-check:** A variable `x` cannot be unified with a term containing `x` (e.g., $f(x)$). 4. If at any step two distinct constants are compared, unification **fails**. **Worked Example (2 marks):** Find $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(\text{At}(x, \text{Lab2}), \text{At}(\text{R1}, y))$: | Step | Term 1 | Term 2 | Substitution | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{At}$ | $\text{At}$ | $\theta = \{\}$ | | 2 | $x$ | $\text{R1}$ | $\theta = \{x/\text{R1}\}$ | | 3 | $\text{Lab2}$ | $y$ | $\theta = \{x/\text{R1}, y/\text{Lab2}\}$ | **MGU = $\{x/\text{R1}, y/\text{Lab2}\}$** (1 mark for naming MGU) --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Definition of Resolution (1 mark):** Resolution is a sound and complete inference rule that derives a new clause from two parent clauses containing complementary literals, used for proof by refutation in FOL. **Step 1: Convert KB to CNF (3 marks — 1 mark each):** P1 in CNF: $$\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{HasSensor}(x)$$ P2 in CNF: $$\neg \text{HasSensor}(x) \lor \text{CanNavigate}(x)$$ P3 (fact) in CNF: $$\text{Robot}(\text{Atlas})$$ **Step 2: Negate the Goal (1 mark):** $$\neg \text{CanNavigate}(\text{Atlas})$$ **Step 3: Resolution Refutation (2 marks):** Resolve P2-CNF with negated goal using $\theta = \{x/\text{Atlas}\}$: $$\neg \text{HasSensor}(\text{Atlas}) \lor \text{CanNavigate}(\text{Atlas}) \quad \text{resolves with} \quad \neg \text{CanNavigate}(\text{Atlas})$$ $$\Rightarrow \neg \text{HasSensor}(\text{Atlas})$$ Resolve P1-CNF with $\neg \text{HasSensor}(\text{Atlas})$ using $\theta = \{x/\text{Atlas}\}$: $$\neg \text{Robot}(\text{Atlas}) \lor \text{HasSensor}(\text{Atlas}) \quad \text{resolves with} \quad \neg \text{HasSensor}(\text{Atlas})$$ $$\Rightarrow \neg \text{Robot}(\text{Atlas})$$ Resolve P3 with $\neg \text{Robot}(\text{Atlas})$: $$\text{Robot}(\text{Atlas}) \quad \text{resolves with} \quad \neg \text{Robot}(\text{Atlas})$$ $$\Rightarrow \square \text{ (Empty Clause — CONTRADICTION)}$$ **Conclusion:** Goal $\text{CanNavigate}(\text{Atlas})$ is proven. $\blacksquare$ (1 mark) --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping Occur-Check** — Many students write a substitution like $x/f(x)$ which is **illegal** (-1 mark). > 2. **Forgetting to Negate the Goal** in resolution refutation — resolution works on the **negated** query (-2 marks). > 3. **Wrong Quantifier Order** in FOL — $\forall x \exists y$ is **not** the same as $\exists y \forall x$ (-1 mark). > 4. **Confusing UE with UI** — Universal Elimination is valid for **any** constant, but Universal Introduction requires the constant to be **arbitrary** and not appear in the KB (-1 mark). > 5. **Not showing unification steps** — KTU examiners award marks for the **trace table** of UNIFY (-1 mark if missing). > 6. **Confusing Forward Chaining with Backward Chaining direction** — direction is the *first* thing the examiner checks (-1 mark). --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **KR** = encoding real-world facts into machine-tractable form for inference. - [x] **Four KR requirements:** Representational, Inferential, Inferential Efficiency, Acquisitional Adequacy. - [x] **FOL alphabet:** Constants, Variables, Predicates, Functions, Connectives, Quantifiers, Equality. - [x] **Universal quantifier ($\forall$):** True iff predicate true for **every** object in domain. - [x] **Existential quantifier ($\exists$):** True iff predicate true for **at least one** object. - [x] **Quantifier duality laws** are essential for CNF conversion and Skolemization. - [x] **Unification** finds the substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions identical. - [x] **MGU (Most General Unifier)** is the substitution with the fewest constraints. - [x] **Modus Ponens** is the workhorse inference rule: from $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$, infer $Q$. - [x] **GMP (Generalized Modus Ponens)** extends MP to multiple premises with FOL substitutions. - [x] **Forward Chaining** = data-driven; **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven. - [x] **Resolution** is **complete** for FOL; works on CNF clauses via refutation. - [x] **Skolemization** removes $\exists$ quantifiers by introducing Skolem functions/constants. - [x] **Semantic Networks** are graphical; **Frames** are slot-based; both lack formal semantics. - [x] **FOL is undecidable** in general — no algorithm can determine truth for all FOL statements. - [x] **PROLOG** uses backward chaining + resolution; **CLIPS** uses forward chaining. - [x] **Modern neuro-symbolic AI** combines FOL (for reasoning) with neural networks (for perception) — the future of robotics. --- > **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — KNOWLEDGE REPRESENTATION AND FIRST ORDER LOGIC** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern*

topics.title: Planning with Forward and Backward State Space Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning with Forward and Backward State Space Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Planning** in Artificial Intelligence for Robotics is the computational problem of synthesizing a sequence (or partial order) of *primitive actions* that, when executed from a given *initial state*, achieves a specified *goal state*. In state-space search planning, the planning problem is reduced to a graph search problem over the set of world states.

Formally, a planning problem is defined as the tuple:

$$ \Pi = \langle S, s_0, S_g, A, \gamma \rangle $$

Where:
- $S$ is the finite (or infinite) set of world states.
- $s_0 \in S$ is the initial state.
- $S_g \subseteq S$ is the set of goal states.
- $A$ is the set of available actions (operators).
- $\gamma: S \times A \rightarrow S$ is the deterministic state-transition function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Space Search** is a planning paradigm where **nodes represent states** and **edges represent actions**. The planner searches through this graph to find a path from $s_0$ to any $s \in S_g$.

The two principal flavors of state-space search are:

1. **Forward (Progression) State Space Search** — begins at $s_0$ and applies actions forward in time.
2. **Backward (Regression) State Space Search** — begins at $S_g$ and reasons backward about which actions could have produced the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver)** representation is the canonical language used to describe actions in classical planning. Each action $a$ is described by a triple $\langle \text{Pre}(a), \text{Add}(a), \text{Del}(a) \rangle$ — *preconditions*, *add effects*, and *delete effects*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kochi to Delhi** on a map.

- **Forward Search** is like standing in Kochi and, at every junction, choosing which road to take next, hoping you'll eventually reach Delhi. You explore *forward* from where you are.
- **Backward Search** is like starting from the *Delhi* end of the map and reasoning: *"To be in Delhi, the previous city must be either Chandigarh or Jaipur (depending on route). To be in Jaipur, I must have been in Udaipur…"* You walk *backwards* from your destination.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Analogy (Robotics Context):** Think of a robotic arm in a pick-and-place task. *Forward planning*: "From current configuration, which joint motion should I apply next?" *Backward planning*: "To grasp object X at pose P, my gripper must approach from pose P' — what was the previous pose?" Both reach the same plan, but backward search is often dramatically more focused when the goal is narrow (e.g., a single grasp pose out of millions of configurations).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Standard Definition | KTU Expected Notation |
|------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **State** | Complete snapshot of the world | $s$ |
| **Initial State** | State where planning begins | $s_0$ or $I$ |
| **Goal State** | Desired end configuration | $s_g \in S_g$ |
| **Action / Operator** | World-modifying primitive | $a$ |
| **Precondition** | Requirements for action to fire | $\text{Pre}(a)$ |
| **Effect** | Post-action world change | $\text{Eff}(a)$ |
| **Plan** | Sequence of actions $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle$ | $\pi$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark this in your KTU answer sheet:** A plan $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle$ is *valid* if and only if every action's preconditions are satisfied in the state produced by all preceding actions, and the final state lies in $S_g$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forward State Space Search (Progression Planning)

Forward search starts at the **initial state** $s_0$ and iteratively applies applicable actions to generate successor states, searching this state-space graph until a goal state is reached.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. Initialize the search frontier with $s_0$.
2. Extract a state $s$ from the frontier (using BFS, DFS, A*, or similar).
3. Compute the set of *applicable actions* in $s$:
$$ A(s) = \{ a \in A \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s \} $$
4. For each $a \in A(s)$, generate the successor state:
$$ s' = \gamma(s, a) = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a) $$
5. If $s' \in S_g$, **return the plan** that led to $s'$.
6. Otherwise, add $s'$ to the frontier and repeat.

> [!NOTE]
