> **Why it works:** Forward search is *sound* (any plan returned is valid) and *complete* (a plan is returned if one exists), but suffers from a large branching factor because the agent must consider *all* applicable actions at *every* state.

**The Forward Branching Factor:**

The worst-case branching factor is:
$$ b_{\text{forward}} = \max_{s \in S} \vert A(s) \vert $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic for Forward Search:** The *ignore-delete-list heuristic* $h^{\text{add}}$ estimates the cost of achieving a goal by counting, for each goal fact, the cheapest action that adds it (ignoring deletions). This makes $A^*$ efficient in forward planning.

---

### 2.2 Backward State Space Search (Regression Planning)

Backward search starts at the **goal** and reasons about which prior state could have led to it. Instead of full states, it manipulates **goal sets (subgoals)**.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. Initialize the search with the goal description $G$ (a set of ground literals).
2. Choose a literal $\ell \in G$.
3. Select an action $a$ such that $\ell \in \text{Add}(a)$ and $a$ is *relevant* (it achieves some unmet goal without clobbering existing goals).
4. Compute the *regressed subgoal*:
$$ G' = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a) \setminus \text{Del}(a)^{-1} $$
   More precisely, for STRIPS:
$$ \text{Regression}(G, a) = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a) $$
   combined with *protection*: remove from $G$ any literal that $a$ deletes (clobbered goals).
5. If $G' \subseteq s_0$, return the plan.
6. Otherwise, recurse on $G'$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Backward search is highly *goal-directed* — it only explores actions that are relevant to the goal. Its branching factor is often much smaller than forward search, but the regression operation can be more complex (e.g., handling conditional effects).

**The Backward Branching Factor:**

$$ b_{\text{backward}} = \max_{G} \vert \{ a \in A \mid a \text{ achieves some } \ell \in G \} \vert $$

In practice, $b_{\text{backward}} \ll b_{\text{forward}}$.

---

### 2.3 Comparison: Forward vs. Backward State Space Search

| Criterion | Forward (Progression) | Backward (Regression) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Search Start** | Initial state $s_0$ | Goal description $G$ |
| **Search Direction** | $s_0 \rightarrow S_g$ | $S_g \rightarrow s_0$ |
| **Node Type** | World states | Goal sets (subgoals) |
| **Branching Factor** | High (all applicable actions) | Low (only goal-relevant actions) |
| **Termination Check** | $s \in S_g$ | $G \subseteq s_0$ |
| **Clobbering Concern** | None in node generation | Must protect against deleted subgoals |
| **Information Availability** | Full state known | Partial state (subgoal literals) |
| **Soundness** | Yes | Yes |
| **Completeness** | Yes (for finite spaces) | Yes (for STRIPS) |
| **Common Use** | Model Checkers, FF Planner | GOAP, Hierarchical Task Networks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *backward chaining* in rule-based expert systems with *backward state-space search* in planning. They are different: backward chaining matches rules; backward search regresses goal literals through actions.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | State Transition (Forward) | $s' = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$ | STRIPS-style |
| 2 | Applicable Actions | $A(s) = \{ a \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s \}$ | Subset relation over literals |
| 3 | Goal Regression | $G' = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$ | After removing clobbered goals |
| 4 | Forward Branching Factor | $b_f = \max_s \vert A(s) \vert$ | Can be very large |
| 5 | Backward Branching Factor | $b_b = \max_G \vert \text{relevant}(A, G) \vert$ | Usually $\ll b_f$ |
| 6 | Ignore-Preconditions Heuristic | $h(s) = \vert G \setminus s \vert$ | Number of unsatisfied goals |
| 7 | Ignore-Delete-List Heuristic | $h^{\text{add}}(s) = \sum_{\ell \in G} h_{\ell}(s)$ | Sum of cheapest achievers |
| 8 | Plan Validity Condition | $s_n = \gamma(s_{n-1}, a_n) \in S_g$ | Recursive forward check |
| 9 | Plan Length (A* Bound) | $g(n) = \text{cumulative action cost}$ | Admissible for unit costs |
| 10 | Heuristic Optimality (A*) | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Admissibility requirement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in Robotics:** Forward state-space search underpins modern **PDDL-based planners** (Fast Downward, LAMA) used for warehouse robot routing. Backward regression is the heart of **Goal-Oriented Action Planning (GOAP)** in game AI and autonomous agents — used by F.E.A.R. and Halo.

### 2.5 Why Use Backward Search in Robotics?

In real-world robotic domains, the **goal is narrow** (e.g., "object grasped" or "battery ≥ 80%") but the **state space is enormous** (continuous joint angles, sensor noise, etc.). Backward search exploits this asymmetry by pruning the space of states to *only those reachable by goal-relevant action chains*. This yields orders-of-magnitude reductions in search effort.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: The Blocks World

Let us illustrate forward and backward search on a **Blocks World** problem.

**Domain:**
- Two blocks: A and B, on a table.
- Actions: $\text{Stack}(x, y)$, $\text{Unstack}(x, y)$, $\text{Pickup}(x)$, $\text{Putdown}(x)$.
- Each action has STRIPS-style preconditions, add, and delete effects.

**Initial State:**
$$ s_0 = \{\ \text{On}(A, \text{Table}),\ \text{On}(B, \text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{armempty}\ \} $$

**Goal:**
$$ G = \{\ \text{On}(A, B),\ \text{clear}(A)\ \} $$

---

### 3.2 Forward Search Trace (Exhaustive)

We start with $s_0$. Applicable actions are those whose preconditions hold:

$$ A(s_0) = \{ \text{Pickup}(A),\ \text{Pickup}(B) \} $$

We apply $\text{Pickup}(A)$. Its STRIPS specification:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{Pickup}(A)) &= \{ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{armempty} \} \\
\text{Add}(\text{Pickup}(A)) &= \{ \text{holding}(A) \} \\
\text{Del}(\text{Pickup}(A)) &= \{ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty} \}
\end{aligned} $$

Applying $\text{Pickup}(A)$ to $s_0$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
s_1 &= (s_0 \setminus \text{Del}(\text{Pickup}(A))) \cup \text{Add}(\text{Pickup}(A)) \\
&= (s_0 \setminus \{\text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty}\}) \cup \{\text{holding}(A)\} \\
&= \{\ \text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{holding}(A)\ \}
\end{aligned} $$

Goal test on $s_1$: $G \subseteq s_1$? $G$ requires $\text{On}(A,B)$ and $\text{clear}(A)$. $s_1$ lacks $\text{On}(A,B)$. **Not a goal.**

Now from $s_1$, applicable actions include $\text{Stack}(A, B)$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) &= \{ \text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B) \} \\
\text{Add}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) &= \{ \text{On}(A,B),\ \text{armempty} \} \\
\text{Del}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) &= \{ \text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B) \}
\end{aligned} $$

Apply:

$$ \begin{aligned}
s_2 &= (s_1 \setminus \text{Del}(\text{Stack}(A,B))) \cup \text{Add}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) \\
&= \{\ \text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{armempty}\ \}
\end{aligned} $$

Goal test on $s_2$: Does $G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\} \subseteq s_2$? **Yes!** Plan returned: $\pi = \langle \text{Pickup}(A),\ \text{Stack}(A,B) \rangle$.

---

### 3.3 Backward Search Trace (Exhaustive)

We start with the goal set $G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\}$.

**Step 1:** Choose a goal literal. Let us pick $\text{On}(A,B)$. Find an action $a$ with $\text{On}(A,B) \in \text{Add}(a)$ — this is $\text{Stack}(A,B)$.

Check relevance: $a$'s preconditions $\{\text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\}$ do not clobber $G$ (they don't delete $\text{clear}(A)$ or $\text{On}(A,B)$). ✓

**Step 2:** Regress the goal through $a$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
G_1 &= (G \setminus \text{Add}(\text{Stack}(A,B))) \cup \text{Pre}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) \\
&= (\{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\} \setminus \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{armempty}\}) \cup \{\text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\} \\
&= \{\text{clear}(A),\ \text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3:** Now we need to achieve $\text{holding}(A)$. The only achiever is $\text{Pickup}(A)$ (or $\text{Unstack}$). Choose $\text{Pickup}(A)$.

Check: Does $\text{Pickup}(A)$ clobber any current subgoals? $\text{Del}(\text{Pickup}(A)) = \{\text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty}\}$. Neither appears in $G_1$. ✓

**Step 4:** Regress $G_1$ through $\text{Pickup}(A)$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
G_2 &= (G_1 \setminus \text{Add}(\text{Pickup}(A))) \cup \text{Pre}(\text{Pickup}(A)) \\
&= (\{\text{clear}(A),\ \text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\} \setminus \{\text{holding}(A)\}) \cup \{\text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{armempty}\} \\
&= \{\text{clear}(A),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty}\}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 5:** Goal test: $G_2 \subseteq s_0$?

$$ \begin{aligned}
s_0 &= \{\text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(A),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{armempty}\} \\
G_2 &= \{\text{clear}(A),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty}\}
\end{aligned} $$

Since $G_2 \subseteq s_0$, the planner succeeds. The plan is the actions in order: $\pi = \langle \text{Pickup}(A),\ \text{Stack}(A,B) \rangle$. Same result as forward search, but reached by reasoning from the goal back to the start.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Same Problem)

| Metric | Forward Search | Backward Search |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **States/Goals Explored** | $s_0, s_1, s_2$ (3 nodes) | $G, G_1, G_2$ (3 subgoals) |
| **Actions Considered** | 2 at $s_0$ (Pickup A/B), 1 at $s_1$ | 2 for $\text{On}(A,B)$ achievers, 1 for $\text{holding}(A)$ |
| **Plan Length** | 2 | 2 |
| **Termination** | $G \subseteq s_2$ | $G_2 \subseteq s_0$ |
| **Clobbering Handled** | N/A | Yes (protection step) |

---

### 3.5 Heuristic Derivation: The Ignore-Preconditions Heuristic

The simplest admissible heuristic for forward search is the *ignore-preconditions heuristic*:

$$ h(s) = \vert G \setminus s \vert $$

It simply counts the number of goal literals not yet true in $s$, ignoring whether they can be achieved. This is **admissible** because it never *overestimates* the cost (you need at least one action per missing literal, and possibly more).

> [!TIP]
> **More accurate:** $h^{\text{add}}(s)$ solves a relaxed planning graph where delete effects are ignored. This is the engine behind the LAMA planner (winner of multiple International Planning Competitions).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forward State Space Search — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial State s0] --> A1{Goal Test}
    A1 -- No --> A2[Compute Applicable Actions A of s]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Action a via State Transition s' equals s minus Del union Add]
    A3 --> A4[Add s' to Frontier]
    A4 --> A1
    A1 -- Yes --> A5[Return Plan pi equals sequence of actions]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Nodes are states; the planner iterates over the frontier, expanding states by applying actions, and stops when a goal state is reached.

---

### 4.2 Backward State Space Search — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Goal Set G] --> B1{Goal Subset of s0}
    B1 -- No --> B2[Pick Unsatisfied Literal ell in G]
    B2 --> B3[Find Achiever Action a with ell in Add of a]
    B3 --> B4{Action a Clobbers G or Not}
    B4 -- Yes --> B2
    B4 -- No --> B5[Regress G through a to G prime equals G minus Add union Pre]
    B5 --> B1
    B1 -- Yes --> B6[Return Plan pi equals actions in reverse regression order]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Nodes are goal sets; the planner regresses subgoals by replacing achieved literals with their preconditions, and stops when the residual subgoal is satisfied by the initial state.

---

### 4.3 Unified Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD [FORWARD PROGRESSION]
        F1[Start at s0] --> F2[Apply Actions Forward]
        F2 --> F3[Generate Successor States]
        F3 --> F4[Test if Goal Reached]
    end
    subgraph BACKWARD [BACKWARD REGRESSION]
        B1[Start at Goal G] --> B2[Pick Literal in G]
        B2 --> B3[Find Achieving Action]
        B3 --> B4[Regress Goal Set]
        B4 --> B5[Test if Subgoal in s0]
    end
    FORWARD -- Same Plan Length --> RESULT[Valid Plan pi]
    BACKWARD -- Same Plan Length --> RESULT
```

**Reading the diagram:** Both paradigms produce the same valid plan when both terminate, but they traverse the search space in *opposite directions*, with different branching behavior and computational characteristics.

---

### 4.4 Planning State Graph (Toy Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    s0((s0)) -- Pickup A --> s1((s1))
    s0 -- Pickup B --> s2((s2))
    s1 -- Stack A B --> sg((sg Goal))
    s1 -- Putdown A --> s3((s3))
    s2 -- Stack B A --> s4((s4))
    s3 -- Pickup A --> s1
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the actual state-space graph the forward planner explores. The boxed/circle node `sg` is the goal. The forward planner would, in BFS fashion, expand `s0`, then `s1` and `s2`, then `s3` and `s4` (and `sg` via `s1`), finding the plan `Pickup A → Stack A B`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Forward State Space Search in classical planning. What are its two main limitations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Forward State Space Search (also called **Progression Planning**) is a planning algorithm that begins at the **initial state** $s_0$ and repeatedly applies applicable actions to generate successor states, until a state satisfying the **goal condition** $S_g$ is reached. The plan is the sequence of actions along the successful path.

**Two main limitations:**
1. **Large branching factor** — at every state, the planner must consider *all* applicable actions, leading to combinatorial blowup in domains with many actions (e.g., logistics, robotics).
2. **No goal-directedness** — the planner may explore states that are completely irrelevant to the goal, wasting computational effort. This is mitigated by heuristics (e.g., $h^{\text{add}}$) but not eliminated.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to say "forward search is slow" without identifying the *cause* (branching factor and irrelevant state exploration). Always tie the limitation to a specific technical reason to score full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between *relevant* and *consistent* actions in backward state space search with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **backward (regression) planning**, an action $a$ is considered for regressing a goal literal $\ell$ if:

- **Relevant**: $a$ *achieves* $\ell$, i.e., $\ell \in \text{Add}(a)$.
- **Consistent** (non-clobbering): $a$ does not *delete* any other literal in the current subgoal set $G$. Formally, $\text{Del}(a) \cap G = \emptyset$.

**Example (Blocks World):** Suppose current subgoal is $G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\}$. To achieve $\text{On}(A,B)$, consider $\text{Stack}(A,B)$:
- *Relevant?* Yes, $\text{On}(A,B) \in \text{Add}(\text{Stack}(A,B))$.
- *Consistent?* $\text{Del}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) = \{\text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\}$. Neither is in $G$. ✓ Yes, consistent.

So $\text{Stack}(A,B)$ is a valid choice for regression.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of **Forward State Space Search (Progression Planning)**. Apply it to find a plan for the following Blocks World problem. **(7 marks)**

**Initial State:**
$$ s_0 = \{\text{On}(C, A),\ \text{On}(A, \text{Table}),\ \text{On}(B, \text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{clear}(C),\ \text{armempty}\} $$

**Goal:**
$$ G = \{\text{On}(A, B),\ \text{On}(C, A),\ \text{clear}(C)\} $$

**Model Answer:**

Forward search starts at $s_0$, applies actions whose preconditions are satisfied, and tests each resulting state against $G$.

**Step 1: Identify applicable actions at $s_0$.**

$$ A(s_0) = \{\text{Unstack}(C, A),\ \text{Pickup}(B)\} $$
- $\text{Unstack}(C,A)$: pre = $\{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{clear}(C),\ \text{armempty}\}$ ✓
- $\text{Pickup}(B)$: pre = $\{\text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{armempty}\}$ ✓

(Note: $\text{Pickup}(A)$ fails because $C$ is on $A$, so $A$ is not clear.)

**Step 2: Try $\text{Pickup}(B)$.**

$$ s_1 = (s_0 \setminus \{\text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{armempty}\}) \cup \{\text{holding}(B)\} $$
$$ s_1 = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{clear}(C),\ \text{holding}(B)\} $$

Goal test: $G \subseteq s_1$? Missing $\text{On}(A,B)$. **Not a goal.**

**Step 3: From $s_1$, apply $\text{Stack}(B, A)$** (since B is on the table... wait, B is being held, so use $\text{Stack}$ to put B on A, but A is not clear; use $\text{Putdown}$ first, or use a different sequence).

Let us reconsider. The optimal path is:
- $\text{Unstack}(C, A)$: removes C from A, holds C.
- $\text{Putdown}(C)$: places C on table.
- $\text{Pickup}(A)$: now A is clear.
- $\text{Stack}(A, B)$: places A on B.

$$ \pi = \langle \text{Unstack}(C,A),\ \text{Putdown}(C),\ \text{Pickup}(A),\ \text{Stack}(A,B) \rangle $$

This is a 4-step plan. The search tree explores states $s_0 \rightarrow s_a \rightarrow s_b \rightarrow s_c \rightarrow s_g$.

**(b)** What is the *ignore-preconditions heuristic*? Why is it admissible? Use the same Blocks World problem to compute $h(s_0)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **ignore-preconditions heuristic** is the simplest admissible heuristic for forward planning. It estimates the cost-to-goal as the number of goal literals that are *not* yet true in the current state:

$$ h(s) = \vert G \setminus s \vert $$

It ignores whether those literals can actually be achieved (preconditions are ignored) and assumes each missing literal requires at least one action.

**Why admissible:** In a unit-cost action model, you need *at least* one action to make each missing goal literal true, so the true optimal cost $h^*(s) \geq \vert G \setminus s \vert = h(s)$. Since $h(s)$ never *overestimates*, it is admissible, ensuring $A^*$ returns an optimal plan.

**Computation for our problem:**

$$ G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(C,A),\ \text{clear}(C)\} $$
$$ s_0 = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,\text{Table}),\ \text{On}(B,\text{Table}),\ \text{clear}(B),\ \text{clear}(C),\ \text{armempty}\} $$

$$ G \cap s_0 = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{clear}(C)\} $$
$$ G \setminus s_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B)\} $$
$$ h(s_0) = \vert \{\text{On}(A,B)\} \vert = 1 $$

So $A^*$ will prefer states that bring in the missing $\text{On}(A,B)$ literal, directing search toward the goal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Backward State Space Search (Regression Planning)**. What is meant by *clobbering* and *protection*? Illustrate with a Blocks World example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Backward State Space Search (Regression Planning)** is a goal-directed planning approach that starts from the goal description $G$ and works *backward* by selecting actions that achieve the goal (or subgoals), regressing the goal through them, until the regressed subgoal is satisfied by the initial state.

The **state transition** in backward search is the *regression* of a goal set $G$ through an action $a$:

$$ \text{Regression}(G, a) = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a) $$

subject to the **protection condition**:

$$ \text{Del}(a) \cap G = \emptyset $$

**Clobbering:** When an action $a$ selected to achieve a literal $\ell \in G$ inadvertently *deletes* another literal in $G$, that literal is said to be *clobbered*. This would undo progress on another subgoal.

**Protection:** The regression algorithm must *protect* subgoals from being clobbered. This is done by:
1. Rejecting any action $a$ where $\text{Del}(a) \cap G \neq \emptyset$, OR
2. Adding *ordering constraints* (e.g., re-achieve the clobbered literal later).

**Illustration (Blocks World):**

Goal: $G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\}$.

To achieve $\text{On}(A,B)$, we try $\text{Stack}(A,B)$:
- $\text{Pre}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) = \{\text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\}$.
- $\text{Del}(\text{Stack}(A,B)) = \{\text{holding}(A),\ \text{clear}(B)\}$.
- $G = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(A)\}$ — $\text{Del}(a) \cap G = \emptyset$. ✓ No clobbering.

Now suppose the goal were $G' = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{clear}(B)\}$ and we tried $\text{Stack}(A,B)$:
- $\text{Del}(a) \cap G' = \{\text{clear}(B)\} \neq \emptyset$. ✗ Clobbering!
- A protected regression would reject this action, or insert a constraint to re-achieve $\text{clear}(B)$ later.

**(b)** Compare Forward and Backward State Space Search on the basis of branching factor, completeness, soundness, and suitability for robotic applications. Justify why **Goal-Oriented Action Planning (GOAP)** used in robotics uses **backward search**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Forward | Backward |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Branching Factor** | High (all applicable actions) | Low (only goal-relevant) |
| **Soundness** | Yes | Yes (with protection) |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite spaces) | Yes (STRIPS) |
| **Goal-Directedness** | Heuristic-dependent | Inherent |
| **Suitability for Robotics** | Good for trajectory planning in fully observable worlds | **Excellent** for narrow goals in large state spaces |

**Why GOAP uses Backward Search in Robotics:**

1. **Narrow Goal, Wide World:** In robotics, the goal is often a specific condition (e.g., "object grasped at pose P"), but the state space is enormous (joint angles, velocities, sensor states). Backward search exploits this *asymmetry* by focusing on the goal.

2. **Action Relevance:** GOAP selects actions based on *preconditions matching the current world state* and *effects matching the current goal*. This is a natural fit for backward search — each iteration "unlocks" a precondition.

3. **Efficiency with Symbolic World States:** Robotic GOAP systems represent the world as a set of boolean facts (e.g., `hasKey=true`, `doorOpen=false`). Backward search's symbolic regression aligns perfectly with this representation.

4. **Dynamic Re-planning:** When the world changes (e.g., an obstacle appears), GOAP re-plans by re-running the backward search from the same goal — much faster than re-running forward search from a new initial state.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Part B:** Students often list pros and cons without *justifying* their claims. Always tie the comparison to specific properties (e.g., "backward is more goal-directed because it only expands actions whose add-effects include a goal literal"). Such justifications fetch full marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing progression with regression diagrams** — drawing forward search but labeling it as backward, or vice versa. Label nodes clearly as states $s_i$ (forward) or goal sets $G_i$ (backward).
> 2. **Forgetting the protection condition** in backward search. Without it, plans may be invalid (clobbered subgoals). Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Mixing STRIPS with ADL** — STRIPS assumes literals only, no equality, no conditional effects. Don't add features not specified.
> 4. **Computing the wrong branching factor** — $b_f$ is a *worst-case* over all states, not the average. $b_b$ counts only *relevant* actions.
> 5. **Skipping the goal test in traces** — every expansion must include a *Goal Test* step, even if it fails. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete traces.
> 6. **Not using proper regression formula** — write $G' = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$ explicitly; do not describe it in words only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Forward & Backward State Space Search):**

- [x] **Planning Tuple**: $\Pi = \langle S, s_0, S_g, A, \gamma \rangle$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Forward Search** = *Progression*, starts at $s_0$, applies actions forward, tests $s \in S_g$.
- [x] **Backward Search** = *Regression*, starts at $G$, regresses subgoals, tests $G' \subseteq s_0$.
- [x] **STRIPS Action**: $\langle \text{Pre}, \text{Add}, \text{Del} \rangle$ — the canonical representation.
- [x] **State Transition**: $s' = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$.
- [x] **Goal Regression**: $G' = (G \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$.
- [x] **Protection Condition**: $\text{Del}(a) \cap G = \emptyset$.
- [x] **Relevant Action**: An action $a$ is *relevant* to subgoal $\ell$ if $\ell \in \text{Add}(a)$.
- [x] **Consistent Action**: A relevant action is *consistent* if it does not clobber any subgoal.
- [x] **Heuristic $h(s) = \vert G \setminus s \vert$** is admissible and trivial to compute.
- [x] **Heuristic $h^{\text{add}}(s)$** uses delete relaxation and is the basis of LAMA.
- [x] **Forward branching factor** $b_f$ is high; **backward** $b_b$ is low.
- [x] **GOAP** in robotics uses **backward search** because goals are narrow and the world is wide.
- [x] **Soundness & Completeness** of both paradigms — be ready to justify in 2-mark questions.
- [x] **Blocks World** is the *go-to example* for KTU exams — practice at least 2 traces.
- [x] **Examiner loves** regression traces with explicit protection checks — practice writing them.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "F.A.B.S."** for KTU exams:
> - **F**orward = **F**rom the start (applies all actions)
> - **A**pplicable actions (must satisfy Pre)
> - **B**ackward = **B**uild from goal (regress literals)
> - **S**ound and complete (both)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Order Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Partial Order Planning # Partial Order Planning — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Partial Order Planning (POP)** is a class of least-commitment AI planning algorithms in which a plan is represented as a **partially ordered multiset of plan steps** (actions/operators) connected by a set of ordering constraints, causal links, and binding constraints. Unlike Total Order Planning (TOP), a POP algorithm only introduces an ordering between two actions when it is **strictly necessary** to preserve a precondition or to resolve a threat. This embodies the **principle of least commitment** — never make a decision earlier than it is required. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:** > *“A Partial Order Plan is a 4-tuple $\langle A, O, L, B \rangle$ where $A$ is a set of plan steps (actions), $O$ is a set of ordering constraints of the form $S_i \prec S_j$, $L$ is a set of causal links of the form $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$, and $B$ is a set of variable binding constraints.”* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Parallel Cooking" Metaphor Imagine you are the head chef orchestrating a multi-dish dinner for a banquet: - A **Total Order Plan** would force you to boil pasta, then fry garlic, then boil sauce, then mix — one strict, linear sequence. Even when two dishes can simmer simultaneously, you wait. - A **Partial Order Plan** lets you say: *"Start the pasta water boiling, then while the water heats up, chop the garlic and start the sauce."* You only enforce the orderings that truly matter (e.g., *sauce must be stirred before pasta is added to it*), and everything else is free to be **interleaved or parallelized** by whoever (or whatever) is executing the plan. In robotic AI, this matters hugely: while the robot arm screws bolt A, the vision system can simultaneously inspect bolt B — POP lets the planner commit to this parallelism without prematurely deciding *exactly* which action goes first. > [!NOTE] > **Why “Partial Order”?** A plan is *partially* ordered because some actions have a known precedence (e.g., pickup → place), while many others remain unordered with respect to each other. When you later choose a total ordering (called a **linearization**), the plan becomes executable. ## 1.3 Core Terminology Table | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Plan Step | $S_i$ | An action/operator instance in the plan | | Ordering Constraint | $S_i \prec S_j$ | $S_i$ must occur before $S_j$ | | Causal Link | $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ | $S_i$ achieves precondition $Q$ for $S_j$ | | Threat | — | A step $S_k$ that may negate a causal link's protected condition | | Open Precondition | $Q$ | A precondition of some step not yet achieved | | Binding | $v = c$ | A variable $v$ is bound to constant $c$ | | Promotion | — | Threat-resolution: order $S_k$ **after** $S_j$ | | Demotion | — | Threat-resolution: order $S_k$ **before** $S_i$ | ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome Mapping | CO ID | Course Outcome | Coverage in this Topic | |---|---|---| | **CO2** | Apply classical planning techniques to robotic task sequences | Construct POP plans for sample robotic domains | | **CO3** | Analyze least-commitment strategies in AI planning | Compare POP vs. TOP trade-offs | | **CO5** | Evaluate plan correctness, completeness, and threat resolution | Detect and resolve planning threats | # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Least-Commitment Philosophy The classical planner **STRIPS** (and its descendants like POP, SIPE, and O-Plan) operate under a guiding heuristic: *do not commit to a choice until forced*. Partial Order Planning is the most rigorous embodiment of this heuristic. It contrasts with **state-space search** (forward/backward chaining) which builds one fully specified world state at a time. > [!NOTE] > **Two competing commitments a planner makes:** > 1. **Ordering commitment** — *When* an action is executed. > 2. **Binding commitment** — *What specific object* an action uses. > POP defers BOTH until absolutely necessary. ## 2.2 Anatomy of a Partial Order Plan A POP plan at any stage of refinement is the tuple: $$P = \langle A,\; O,\; L,\; B \rangle$$ | Component | Description | Set-theoretic form | |---|---|---| | $A$ | Plan steps (actions + Start + Finish) | $A = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n, S_\infty\}$ | | $O$ | Ordering constraints | $O \subseteq A \times A$ | | $L$ | Causal links | $L \subseteq A \times \text{Literals} \times A$ | | $B$ | Binding constraints (unifier) | $B \subseteq \text{Vars} \times \text{Terms}$ | The two distinguished steps are: - **$S_0$ (Start)** — has the **initial state** as its effect. - **$S_\infty$ (Finish)** — has the **goal description** as its precondition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary / Invariant Conditions of a valid POP:** > 1. $\forall (S_i \prec S_j) \in O$: the partial order is a **strict partial order** (irreflexive, asymmetric, transitive). > 2. Every causal link $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ is **protected** from threats by $O$ (no $S_k$ between $S_i$ and $S_j$ that negates $Q$ is allowed without promotion/demotion). > 3. No cycles in $O$ (e.g., $S_i \prec S_j$ and $S_j \prec S_i$ is **forbidden**). ## 2.3 The POP Refinement Loop — Conceptual Steps A partial-order planner repeatedly does one of the following refinements until the plan is **consistent and complete**: 1. **Resolve an open precondition** of a non-Finish step by either: - Adding a new step whose effect unifies with the precondition, or - Linking to an existing step that already produces the precondition. 2. **Resolve a threat** detected between steps via: - **Promotion**: $S_k \prec S_i$ (force the threatener before the producer). - **Demotion**: $S_j \prec S_k$ (force the threatener after the consumer). - **Binding constraint**: ground variables so the threat disappears. 3. **Return failure** if neither promotion nor demotion yields an acyclic partial order. ## 2.4 Threat — Formal Definition > [!WARNING] > **This is the single most-tested definition in KTU university exams.** A **threat** to a causal link $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ is a plan step $S_k$ such that: $$S_i \prec S_k \prec S_j \quad \text{(i.e., } S_k \text{ lies between } S_i \text{ and } S_j \text{)}$$ and the effect of $S_k$ is $\neg Q$ (negation of the protected literal $Q$). If $S_k$ is a **variable-step** with a possibly negating effect (e.g., Move(x, y) could move a block on top of another), then the threat is **potential** and binding constraints must also be considered. ## 2.5 Promotion vs. Demotion — The Resolution Duality | Resolution | New Ordering Added | Effect | When to Choose | |---|---|---|---| | **Promotion** | $S_k \prec S_i$ | Threatener must occur **before** the producer $S_i$ | When $S_i$'s effect is needed for OTHER downstream steps too — moving $S_i$ forward would break too much | | **Demotion** | $S_j \prec S_k$ | Threatener must occur **after** the consumer $S_j$ | When $S_k$ is required to do its work before $S_j$ can complete | Both are equally valid; the planner may explore both via a non-deterministic branch, or use a **domain heuristic** to pick one first. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Plan tuple | $P = \langle A, O, L, B \rangle$ | $S_0, S_\infty \in A$ | | 2 | Plan complete | Every precondition of every step is achieved by some causal link | No open preconditions remain | | 3 | Plan consistent | No cycle in $O$; no unprotected causal link | The invariant the algorithm maintains | | 4 | Threat condition | $\exists S_k$ s.t. $S_i \prec S_k \prec S_j$ and $S_k$ has $\neg Q$ in its add/delete list | The protected literal $Q$ | | 5 | Promotion | Add $S_k \prec S_i$ | $S_k$ before producer | | 6 | Demotion | Add $S_j \prec S_k$ | $S_k$ after consumer | | 7 | Causal link notation | $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ | $S_i$ provides $Q$ for $S_j$ | | 8 | Open precondition | A precondition of $S_j$ with no incoming causal link | Must be resolved | | 9 | Subgoal ordering | $S_0 \prec S_1 \prec S_2 \prec \ldots \prec S_\infty$ | Implicit chain in any consistent plan | | 10 | Search complexity | PSPACE-complete (by Nebel’s 2000 result for propositional POP) | Pure forward search is exponential — POP is more efficient | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Partial Order Planning - **Robotic assembly lines** — robotic arms perform grasping and welding in parallel; POP captures the slack. - **Spacecraft mission planning** (NASA's **CASPER** system) — daily re-planning of on-board activities; POP allows efficient re-planning because the existing partial order is preserved. - **Manufacturing scheduling** — least-commitment lets a human operator inject execution choices at runtime. - **Autonomous vehicles** — lane-change overtaking, merging, and signal-obeying maneuvers are often modeled as partial-order subtasks. # Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — The "Blocks-World" POP We illustrate POP on a **classic 3-block problem**. We work it out exhaustively so every KTU-marks-worthy logical step is on the page. ## 3.1 Problem Statement > **Initial State (I):** $\text{On}(A, B) \land \text{On}(B, C) \land \text{On}(C, \text{Table}) \land \text{clear}(A)$ > **Goal State (G):** $\text{On}(C, B) \land \text{On}(B, A) \land \text{On}(A, \text{Table})$ > [!NOTE] > *In words:* Stack the tower in reverse — A on the table, B on A, C on B. ### 3.1.1 Available Operators (PDDL-style) | Operator | Preconditions | Add-Effects | Delete-Effects | |---|---|---|---| | **Stack(x, y)** | $\text{Hold}(x) \land \text{clear}(y)$ | $\text{On}(x, y)$, $\text{clear}(x)$, $\neg \text{Hold}(x)$ | $\text{clear}(y)$, $\text{Hold}(x)$ | | **Unstack(x, y)** | $\text{On}(x, y) \land \text{clear}(x)$ | $\text{Hold}(x)$, $\text{clear}(y)$ | $\text{On}(x, y)$, $\text{clear}(x)$ | | **Pickup(x)** | $\text{OnTable}(x) \land \text{clear}(x)$ | $\text{Hold}(x)$ | $\text{OnTable}(x)$, $\text{clear}(x)$ | | **Putdown(x)** | $\text{Hold}(x)$ | $\text{OnTable}(x)$, $\text{clear}(x)$ | $\text{Hold}(x)$ | ## 3.2 Step 0 — Initialize the Empty Plan We instantiate: - $A = \{S_0, S_\infty\}$ - $O = \{S_0 \prec S_\infty\}$ - $L = \emptyset$ - $B = \emptyset$ (all variables free) The initial state is attached as the **effect of $S_0$**, and the goal literals as **preconditions of $S_\infty$**. > **Open preconditions of $S_\infty$:** $\{\text{On}(C,B), \text{On}(B,A), \text{On}(A,\text{Table})\}$. ## 3.3 Step 1 — Resolve Open Precondition $\text{On}(A, \text{Table})$ The plan adds a new step: $$S_1 = \text{Putdown}(A) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{Stack}(A, \text{Table})$$ We choose **Putdown(A)** for variety. The POP algorithm records: $$S_1 : \text{Pre} = \{\text{Hold}(A)\},\quad \text{Eff}^+ = \{\text{On}(A, \text{Table}), \text{clear}(A)\},\quad \text{Eff}^- = \{\text{Hold}(A)\}$$ A causal link is created: $$L_1 = S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{On}(A, \text{Table})} S_\infty$$ and the orderings $S_0 \prec S_1$ and $S_1 \prec S_\infty$ are inserted. > **Remaining open preconditions:** $\{\text{On}(C,B), \text{On}(B,A), \text{Hold}(A)\}$. ## 3.4 Step 2 — Resolve Open Precondition $\text{Hold}(A)$ for $S_1$ The planner looks for a step whose effect is $\text{Hold}(A)$. **Pickup(A)** is the standard choice, requiring $\text{OnTable}(A) \land \text{clear}(A)$. We add: $$S_2 = \text{Pickup}(A)$$ with causal link $L_2 = S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(A)} S_1$ and ordering $S_0 \prec S_2 \prec S_1$. ## 3.5 Step 3 — Threat Detection on $L_2$ Consider the (potential) introduction of any future step $S_k$ whose effect is $\neg \text{Hold}(A)$ (e.g., another Putdown, or a Stack). When we eventually add a step to handle $\text{On}(B,A)$, the candidate $S_3 = \text{Stack}(B,A)$ has $\neg \text{Hold}(A)$ in its delete list. **Threat check:** if we ever set $S_2 \prec S_3 \prec S_1$, then $S_3$ would un-hold A *after* Pickup and *before* Putdown, **breaking the causal link $L_2$**. > **Resolution (via Demotion):** The planner forces $S_1 \prec S_3$ so that A is already put down before B is stacked on A. This is a **demotion** of the threat. After this demotion, the orderings are: $$S_0 \prec S_2 \prec S_1 \prec S_3 \prec S_\infty$$ ## 3.6 Step 4 — Resolve $\text{On}(B, A)$ for $S_\infty$ Add $S_3 = \text{Stack}(B, A)$ with preconditions $\text{Hold}(B) \land \text{clear}(A)$. Causal link: $$L_3 = S_3 \xrightarrow{\text{On}(B, A)} S_\infty$$ The plan also needs $\text{clear}(A)$ at $S_3$ — this is provided by $S_1$ (its add-effect $\text{clear}(A)$), giving causal link $L_4 = S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(A)} S_3$. ## 3.7 Step 5 — Resolve $\text{Hold}(B)$ for $S_3$ We need to get B held. A standard sub-plan: **Unstack(B, C)** → which requires $\text{On}(B, C)$ and $\text{clear}(B)$. These are both true in the initial state. Add: $$S_4 = \text{Unstack}(B, C) \quad \text{with effect} \quad \text{Hold}(B),\ \text{clear}(C),\ \neg \text{On}(B,C),\ \neg \text{clear}(B)$$ Causal link $L_5 = S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(B)} S_3$, orderings $S_0 \prec S_4 \prec S_3$. ## 3.8 Step 6 — Threat Detection on $L_5$ Now $L_5 = S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(B)} S_3$. Any future step that does $\neg \text{Hold}(B)$ between $S_4$ and $S_3$ threatens it. The natural candidate is $S_3$ itself, since $\text{Stack}(B, A)$ deletes $\text{Hold}(B)$ — but $S_3$ is the **consumer** of the link, not an interleaving step, so no threat. What about an intermediate step that might put B down? None needed for $\text{On}(C, B)$. So $L_5$ is **safe**. ## 3.9 Step 7 — Resolve $\text{On}(C, B)$ for $S_\infty$ Add: $$S_5 = \text{Stack}(C, B) \quad \text{with preconditions} \quad \text{Hold}(C) \land \text{clear}(B)$$ Causal link $L_6 = S_5 \xrightarrow{\text{On}(C, B)} S_\infty$, with orderings $S_0 \prec S_5 \prec S_\infty$. ## 3.10 Step 8 — Resolve $\text{clear}(B)$ for $S_5$ The effect $\text{clear}(B)$ is provided by $S_4 = \text{Unstack}(B, C)$, which adds $\text{clear}(B)$. $$L_7 = S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(B)} S_5$$ > **THREAT:** Now $S_3 = \text{Stack}(B, A)$ also touches B, and $S_4 \prec S_3 \prec S_5$ would mean B is held → placed on A → not clear when C is stacked. But the ordering $S_4 \prec S_3$ is already fixed. The threat is that $S_3$ (which precedes $S_5$ in the chain) negates $\text{clear}(B)$. Since $S_3$ **does not** delete $\text{clear}(B)$ (it only requires it), there is **no threat**. If, however, a hypothetical $S_6 = \text{Putdown}(B)$ were introduced between $S_4$ and $S_5$, it would delete $\text{Hold}(B)$ — threatening $L_5$ — and could be resolved by demoting it after $S_3$. ## 3.11 Step 9 — Resolve $\text{Hold}(C)$ for $S_5$ Add: $$S_6 = \text{Unstack}(C, \text{Table})$$ with effect $\text{Hold}(C), \text{clear}(\text{Table}), \neg \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \neg \text{clear}(C)$. Causal link $L_8 = S_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(C)} S_5$, orderings $S_0 \prec S_6 \prec S_5$. ## 3.12 Step 10 — Resolve $\text{clear}(C)$ for $S_6$ $\text{clear}(C)$ is in the initial state — provided by $S_0$: $$L_9 = S_0 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(C)} S_6$$ > **THREAT:** Does $S_4 = \text{Unstack}(B, C)$ threaten $L_9$? $S_4$ deletes $\text{clear}(B)$, not $\text{clear}(C)$. **No threat.** $S_4$ does delete $\text{On}(B, C)$, but that is not the protected literal. ## 3.13 Step 11 — Final Orderings & Plan Verification The complete plan is: | Step | Action | Causal Links Added | |---|---|---| | $S_0$ | **Start** | Effects = initial state | | $S_6$ | Unstack(C, Table) | Holds C, clears Table | | $S_4$ | Unstack(B, C) | Holds B, clears C (i.e. B no longer on C) | | $S_2$ | Pickup(A) | Holds A | | $S_1$ | Putdown(A) | A on Table, A clear | | $S_3$ | Stack(B, A) | B on A | | $S_5$ | Stack(C, B) | C on B | | $S_\infty$ | **Finish** | Goals satisfied | The topological orderings (a valid linearization) are: $$S_0 \prec S_6 \prec S_4 \prec S_2 \prec S_1 \prec S_3 \prec S_5 \prec S_\infty$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice the parallelism available:** $S_6$, $S_4$, and $S_2$ are *not* directly ordered with each other once their individual preconditions are met. The partial order *only* forces: > - $S_0$ before everything > - $S_6$ before $S_5$ (Hold(C) needs to precede Stack(C, B)) > - $S_4$ before $S_3$ (Hold(B) precedes Stack(B, A)) > - $S_4$ before $S_5$ (clear(B) precedes Stack(C, B)) > - $S_2$ before $S_1$ (Hold(A) precedes Putdown(A)) > - $S_1$ before $S_3$ (clear(A) precedes Stack(B, A)) > - Everything before $S_\infty$ A real executor might run $S_6, S_4, S_2$ **in parallel** (with three robotic arms), demonstrating the partial-order advantage. ## 3.14 The Threat-Resolution Decision Summary | Causal Link | Threatening Step | Resolved By | Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | $S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(A)} S_1$ | $S_3$ (Stack(B,A) deletes Hold(A)) | Demotion: $S_1 \prec S_3$ | A put down before B stacked | | $S_0 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(C)} S_6$ | None | — | No step negates clear(C) between | | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(B)} S_5$ | None | — | Stack(B,A) doesn't delete clear(B) | | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(B)} S_3$ | None | — | $S_3$ is the consumer, not interleaving | The plan is now **complete and consistent** — POP has succeeded. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 POP Algorithm — Top-Level Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram captures the high-level functional architecture of a Partial Order Planner as a flow of refinement decisions. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Initialize Plan with S0 and Sinf] --> checkOpens{Any Open
Precondition?} checkOpens -- Yes --> pickOpen[Pick an Open Precondition Q
of a non-Finish step Sj] pickOpen --> chooseProvider{Choose Provider
Step Si} chooseProvider -- Existing step --> addLink1[Add Causal Link
Si -> Q -> Sj] chooseProvider -- New step --> addStep[Add New Step Si
to Plan A] addStep --> addLink1 addLink1 --> checkThreats{New Threats
Introduced?} checkThreats -- Yes --> resolve[Resolve by Promotion
or Demotion] checkThreats -- No --> checkOpens resolve --> cycleCheck{Cycle in
Ordering O?} cycleCheck -- Yes --> backtrack[Backtrack or
Try Alternate Resolution] cycleCheck -- No --> checkOpens backtrack --> checkOpens checkOpens -- No --> returnPlan[Return Complete
Consistent Plan] ``` ## 4.2 Causal-Link & Threat Architecture This block diagram shows how a single causal link is *protected* by threat detection and resolution. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PRODUCER["Producer Si"] EFF[Effect: Q] end subgraph CONSUMER["Consumer Sj"] PRE[Precondition: Q] end PRODUCER -- "causal link Si -> Q -> Sj" --> CONSUMER subgraph THREAT["Threatener Sk"] DEL[Delete Effect: not Q] end THREAT -. "threatens if Si precedes Sk precedes Sj" .-> LINK[Protected Causal Link] LINK --> RESOLVE{Resolution} RESOLVE -- Promotion --> PROM[Add Sk precedes Si] RESOLVE -- Demotion --> DEMO[Add Sj precedes Sk] RESOLVE -- Binding --> BIND[Constrain Variables
so not Q disappears] ``` ## 4.3 Blocks-World Plan Refinement — Step-by-Step View This diagram visualizes the *physical* causal structure of the 3-block example we solved in Section 3. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["S0 : Start
Initial State"] S6["S6 : Unstack(C,Table)
Add Hold(C)"] S4["S4 : Unstack(B,C)
Add Hold(B), clear(B)"] S2["S2 : Pickup(A)
Add Hold(A)"] S1["S1 : Putdown(A)
Add On(A,Table), clear(A)"] S3["S3 : Stack(B,A)
Add On(B,A)"] S5["S5 : Stack(C,B)
Add On(C,B)"] SINF["Sinf : Finish
Goals Achieved"] S0 --> S6 S0 --> S4 S0 --> S2 S6 --> S5 S4 --> S3 S4 --> S5 S2 --> S1 S1 --> S3 S3 --> SINF S5 --> SINF S1 --> SINF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is an *ordering constraint*. Notice that $S_6$, $S_4$, and $S_2$ are mutually unordered — a real planner/executor may schedule them in any order, even concurrently, because none of them's effects are required by the others. ## 4.4 POP vs. TOP — Architectural Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TOP["Total Order Planning"] T1[Step 1] --> T2[Step 2] --> T3[Step 3] --> T4[Step 4] end subgraph POP["Partial Order Planning"] P1[Step 1] --> P3[Step 3] P2[Step 2] --> P3 P2 --> P4[Step 4] P1 --> P4 end TOP -. "Single linear path" .- NOTE1[TOP forces premature ordering] POP -. "Minimal constraints only" .- NOTE2[POP defers ordering until forced] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q1. Define Partial Order Planning (POP). How does it differ from Total Order Planning? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Definition:** Partial Order Planning is a least-commitment planning technique in which a plan is represented as a partially ordered set of steps connected by ordering constraints, causal links, and binding constraints. A plan is formalized as the tuple $P = \langle A, O, L, B \rangle$. - **(1 mark)** **Least-commitment principle:** POP only introduces an ordering between two steps when it is necessary to protect a causal link or resolve a threat. - **(1 mark)** **Difference from TOP:** In Total Order Planning, all actions are linearly ordered, forcing premature decisions about execution order; POP keeps only the necessary orderings and allows the remaining steps to be parallelized or interleaved at execution time. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q2. What is a *threat* in a Partial Order Plan? Name the two standard techniques to resolve it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1.5 marks)** A **threat** occurs when a plan step $S_k$ lies between the producer $S_i$ and the consumer $S_j$ of a causal link $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$, and $S_k$ has the negation of the protected literal $Q$ (i.e., $\neg Q$) in its delete-list or effect. - **(1.5 marks)** The two resolution techniques are: - **Promotion** — adding the ordering $S_k \prec S_i$ (threatener placed before producer). - **Demotion** — adding the ordering $S_j \prec S_k$ (threatener placed after consumer). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the components of a Partial Order Plan with a suitable diagram. How does the *causal link* protect a precondition across the plan? **Model Answer (7 marks):** 1. **(2 marks) Plan tuple $P = \langle A, O, L, B \rangle$:** - $A$ — set of plan steps (actions, Start, Finish). - $O$ — set of ordering constraints, e.g., $S_i \prec S_j$. - $L$ — set of causal links $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$, indicating $S_i$ achieves $Q$ for $S_j$. - $B$ — set of binding constraints (unifications) on variables. 2. **(2 marks) Causal link protection mechanism:** A causal link $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ is a commitment by the planner that $Q$ will hold from the moment $S_i$ is executed until $S_j$ needs it. Any step $S_k$ in the interval $S_i \prec S_k \prec S_j$ whose effect negates $Q$ is a *threat* and must be resolved by promotion or demotion. 3. **(2 marks) Diagram:** A simple two-step causal link diagram with threatener and resolution arrows (as in Section 4.2 above). 4. **(1 mark) Significance:** This mechanism is what makes POP a **constraint-satisfaction** problem rather than a pure state-space search, allowing it to be more efficient. #### (b) **[7 marks]** Consider a robot navigation domain with the following actions and the initial/goal states given. Construct a Partial Order Plan and identify the threats and their resolutions. (Use a simple domain: robot at $R1$, key at $K1$, door between $R1$ and $R2$, goal is robot at $R2$.) **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Domain Actions:** - $\text{Move}(r, x, y)$: robot $r$ moves from $x$ to $y$. Pre: $\text{At}(r, x), \text{Adjacent}(x,y)$. Eff: $\text{At}(r, y), \neg \text{At}(r, x)$. - $\text{Pickup}(r, k, x)$: robot picks up key $k$ at $x$. Pre: $\text{At}(r, x), \text{AtKey}(k, x), \text{Free}(r)$. Eff: $\text{Holding}(r, k), \neg \text{AtKey}(k, x), \neg \text{Free}(r)$. - $\text{Open}(r, d)$: robot opens door $d$. Pre: $\text{At}(r, \text{loc}(d)), \text{Holding}(r, \text{key}(d))$. Eff: $\text{Opened}(d), \neg \text{Holding}(r, \text{key}(d)), \text{Free}(r)$. **Plan construction (stepwise):** | Step | Action | New Causal Link | |---|---|---| | $S_0$ | Start | Provides $\text{At}(\text{rob}, R1), \text{AtKey}(\text{key}, K1), \text{Adjacent}(R1, R2), \text{Free}(\text{rob})$ | | $S_1$ | Move(rob, R1, K1) | $S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{At}(\text{rob}, K1)} S_2$ | | $S_2$ | Pickup(rob, key, K1) | $S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Holding}(\text{rob}, \text{key})} S_3$ | | $S_3$ | Move(rob, K1, R1) | $S_3 \xrightarrow{\text{At}(\text{rob}, R1)} S_4$ | | $S_4$ | Open(rob, door) | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Opened}(\text{door})} S_5$ | | $S_5$ | Move(rob, R1, R2) | $S_5 \xrightarrow{\text{At}(\text{rob}, R2)} S_\infty$ | **Threats identified and resolved:** | Causal Link | Threatening Step | Resolution | |---|---|---| | $S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{At}(\text{rob}, K1)} S_2$ | None — no step between $S_1$ and $S_2$ | — | | $S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Holding}(\text{rob}, \text{key})} S_3$ | None (no step between $S_2$ and $S_3$) | — | | $S_3 \xrightarrow{\text{At}(\text{rob}, R1)} S_4$ | $S_5 = \text{Move(rob, R1, R2)}$ negates $\text{At}(\text{rob}, R1)$ after $S_4$ but before $S_\infty$ — but $S_5$ is the **consumer** of the related $\text{At}(\text{rob}, R2)$ link, not an interleaving threatener | N/A — no real threat | | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Opened}(\text{door})} S_5$ | None | — | **Final orderings:** $$S_0 \prec S_1 \prec S_2 \prec S_3 \prec S_4 \prec S_5 \prec S_\infty$$ **Justification of correctness (1 mark):** The plan is a totally ordered plan in this example, because the *initial* state and *goal* state in the robot navigation example create a chain dependency. In general, the POP algorithm would explore alternative steps (e.g., robot could open the door first, then move to key, then back) but those would be dead-ends. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* #### (a) **[7 marks]** Describe the **least-commitment principle** in AI planning. Show how POP applies this principle to (i) variable binding and (ii) action ordering, with examples. **Model Answer (7 marks):** 1. **(2 marks) Definition:** The least-commitment principle states that *a planner should not commit to a decision any earlier than is strictly necessary*. It defers both ordering and binding decisions until they are forced by precondition-achievement or threat-resolution. 2. **(2 marks) Variable binding example:** Suppose the goal is $\text{On}(x, y)$ and the operator $\text{Stack}(a, b)$ is selected as the achiever. The variable $x$ may remain unbound (or bound to a set of candidates) until a downstream step actually *uses* $x$ — at which point unification forces the binding. In contrast, a state-space planner (e.g., STRIPS forward search) immediately grounds every variable in the world state. 3. **(2 marks) Action ordering example:** If steps $S_2$ and $S_3$ both achieve independent preconditions of $S_4$, POP does NOT add $S_2 \prec S_3$ or $S_3 \prec S_2$ unless a threat forces it. The two steps can be scheduled at runtime in any order, or even in parallel. 4. **(1 mark) Benefit:** This deferment enables efficient re-planning in dynamic environments, because the planner doesn't have to undo premature decisions — only the necessary ones. #### (b) **[7 marks]** Consider the following situation and construct a partial order plan. *Initial state:* $\text{On}(B, A), \text{OnTable}(C), \text{clear}(B), \text{clear}(C)$. *Goal:* $\text{On}(A, B), \text{On}(C, A)$. Identify at least one threat and resolve it. **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Stepwise Plan Construction:** | Iteration | Open Precondition | Step Added | Causal Link | Orderings | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{On}(C, A)$ of $S_\infty$ | $S_1 = \text{Stack}(C, A)$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{On}(C,A)} S_\infty$ | $S_0 \prec S_1 \prec S_\infty$ | | 2 | $\text{Hold}(C)$ of $S_1$ | $S_2 = \text{Pickup}(C)$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(C)} S_1$ | $S_0 \prec S_2 \prec S_1$ | | 3 | $\text{On}(A, B)$ of $S_\infty$ | $S_3 = \text{Stack}(A, B)$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{\text{On}(A,B)} S_\infty$ | $S_0 \prec S_3 \prec S_\infty$ | | 4 | $\text{clear}(B)$ of $S_3$ | $S_4 = \text{Unstack}(B, A)$ | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(B)} S_3$ | $S_0 \prec S_4 \prec S_3$ | | 5 | $\text{Hold}(A)$ of $S_3$ | $S_5 = \text{Unstack}(B, A)$ *via re-use* of $S_4$ effect | $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(A)} S_3$ | (no new ordering) | **Threat Analysis:** - **Causal link $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}(A)} S_3$:** Threatener is $S_1 = \text{Stack}(C, A)$, which deletes $\text{Hold}(C)$ — *not* $\text{Hold}(A)$. **No threat.** - **Causal link $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(B)} S_3$:** Threatener is $S_1 = \text{Stack}(C, A)$ (which doesn't delete $\text{clear}(B)$). **No direct threat.** - **However**, consider the *ordering* $S_4 \prec S_2$. Since $S_4$ (Unstack(B, A)) must precede $S_2$ (Pickup(C))? Actually no — $S_2$ requires $\text{OnTable}(C) \land \text{clear}(C)$, both true initially. So $S_2$ is independent. - **Real threat introduced:** $S_3 = \text{Stack}(A, B)$ requires $\text{clear}(A)$. $S_4 = \text{Unstack}(B, A)$ adds $\text{clear}(A)$. **Causal link** $S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{clear}(A)} S_3$ is needed. If $S_1$ (Stack(C, A)) were ordered between $S_4$ and $S_3$, it would delete $\text{clear}(A)$. **Resolution via Demotion:** Add $S_3 \prec S_1$, ensuring Stack(A, B) happens *before* Stack(C, A). **Final ordering constraints:** $$S_0 \prec S_4 \prec \{S_2,\; S_5\} \prec S_3 \prec S_1 \prec S_\infty$$ Notice that $S_2$ and $S_5$ are *partially* unordered — they can occur in any order, demonstrating the partial-order benefit. **Verification (1 mark):** All preconditions are covered by causal links, no threats remain unprotected, the partial order is acyclic. **Plan is complete and consistent.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the Start and Finish steps** — both $S_0$ and $S_\infty$ must be included in $A$ from the very beginning. Many students draw the plan without them and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing promotion and demotion** — a *promotion* forces the threatener **before the producer**; a *demotion* forces the threatener **after the consumer**. Mixing these up costs full marks on the threat-resolution sub-question. > 3. **Not checking for cycles** — after adding a promotion or demotion ordering, you *must* verify the partial order is still acyclic. Adding $S_i \prec S_k$ when $S_k \prec S_i$ already exists causes a cycle, and the algorithm should backtrack, not declare success. > 4. **Treating a causal link's producer as a threat to its own consumer** — a step cannot threaten its own causal link (it is either the producer or the consumer, not an interleaving step). > 5. **Skipping the explanation of why a threat needs resolution** — simply writing "demote $S_3$" without saying *which causal link is protected* and *which literal is negated* costs at least 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > **A high-density, rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE.** - ✅ **Definition (must memorize):** POP is a least-commitment planner building plans as $\langle A, O, L, B \rangle$ — a *partially* ordered multiset of steps with only the necessary ordering constraints. - ✅ **Two special steps:** $S_0$ (Start, provides initial state) and $S_\infty$ (Finish, requires goal state). - ✅ **Open precondition:** A precondition of a step that no causal link currently achieves. POP's job is to eliminate all open preconditions. - ✅ **Causal link:** $S_i \xrightarrow{Q} S_j$ — an assertion that $S_i$ provides literal $Q$ for $S_j$. It is the *commitment* of POP. - ✅ **Threat condition (formula):** $\exists S_k$ with $S_i \prec S_k \prec S_j$ and $\text{Delete}(S_k) \ni \neg Q$ where $Q$ is the protected literal. - ✅ **Resolution 1 — Promotion:** Add $S_k \prec S_i$ (threatener before producer). - ✅ **Resolution 2 — Demotion:** Add $S_j \prec S_k$ (threatener after consumer). - ✅ **Resolution 3 — Binding:** Constrain variables so $\neg Q$ no longer unifies (e.g., $x \neq y$). - ✅ **Plan complete:** No open preconditions remain. - ✅ **Plan consistent:** Acyclic ordering + all causal links protected. - ✅ **Least-commitment principle:** Defer both orderings and bindings. - ✅ **Complexity:** POP is PSPACE-complete (propositional case), but the partial-order structure is far more space-efficient than total-order search. - ✅ **Real-world users:** NASA CASPER (spacecraft), SIPE (manufacturing), O-Plan (UK AI planning), IPP (German aerospace). - ✅ **Contrast with TOP:** TOP requires linear ordering of *all* steps; POP keeps the ordering graph sparse and only commits when forced. - ✅ **Contrast with state-space search:** State-space planners build a *sequence of world states*; POP builds a *constraint structure* over action instances. - ✅ **Key references for further reading:** *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (Russell & Norvig) — Chapter 11 on Planning; Weld's 1994 survey "An Introduction to Least-Commitment Planning"; Penberthy & Weld's UCPOP algorithm. - ✅ **Common KTU question stems:** "Define POP", "Explain threat and resolution", "Compare POP vs TOP", "Construct POP for the given domain", "Show the plan as a partial order diagram". > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "ABCD" for POP components:** > **A**ctions (steps) — **B**indings (variables) — **C**ausal links (protections) — **D**emotion-or-Promotion (threat resolution). > Visualize as: **A**cts **B**ind **C**onditions, **D**efend them.

topics.title: Planning Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Planning Graph** is a directed, leveled graph data structure introduced by **Avrim L. Blum and Merrick L. Furst (1997)** as the central construct of the **GraphPlan algorithm**. It is a compact, polynomial-sized encoding of a **STRIPS-style planning problem** that alternates between two types of levels:

1. **Fact Levels (S-levels / State Levels):** Contain all the propositions (ground literals) that could possibly be true at a given time step.
2. **Action Levels (A-levels):** Contain all the planning actions (including *persistence / no-op actions*) that could possibly be executed at that time step.

Every level is connected to the next by:
- **Precondition Edges (pre→):** From a fact at level $S_i$ to an action at level $A_i$ whose precondition it satisfies.
- **Add-Effect Edges (add→):** From an action at level $A_i$ to a fact at level $S_{i+1}$ it adds.
- **Delete-Effect Edges (del-):** From an action at level $A_i$ to a fact at level $S_{i+1}$ it deletes.
- **Maintenance Edges:** From a no-op action at level $A_i$ to the *same fact* at level $S_{i+1}$.

A special kind of binary constraint called a **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex)** is tracked both at fact levels and action levels, recording pairs that *cannot* be true (or cannot be executed) simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Planning Graphs form the backbone of *partial-order / constraint-based planning*. They are the data structure that converts a PSPACE-complete classical planning problem into a polynomial-size representation on which heuristic extraction (e.g., the **level-cost heuristic**, $h_{max}$, $h_{add}$) becomes tractable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are organizing a **wedding reception with 12 events** — seating guests, cutting the cake, playing music, serving dinner — all happening across an evening.

- A **Calendar (Fact Level $S_i$)** lists *everything that could conceivably be true at minute i* (e.g., "Guests are seated", "Cake is on table", "Lights are dimmed").
- A **Schedule of Activities (Action Level $A_i$)** lists *everything that could conceivably happen in the next minute* (e.g., "Caterer serves food", "DJ plays song", "Photographer clicks").
- A **Conflict Sheet (Mutex Relations)** tells you which two activities *cannot run in parallel* (e.g., "Serve food" and "Play loud music" are mutually exclusive near the dining hour).
- Each minute, you **expand** the calendar and the schedule by one more sheet, then look for a coherent dinner plan.

That rolling, expanding calendar+schedule+conflict sheet is exactly a **Planning Graph**. The first level where all goals appear *non-mutex* is the candidate plan horizon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric:** The size of a planning graph with proposition set $P$ and action set $A$ grows as $\mathcal{O}(\vert P \vert^2 + \vert A \vert^2)$ per level — polynomial, even though the underlying planning problem is PSPACE-complete in the worst case.

## 1.3 Why Planning Graphs Matter in AI for Robotics

A robotic agent operating in a warehouse, hospital, or planetary terrain must rapidly compute a sequence of discrete decisions. Planning graphs enable:

- **Polynomial-time reachability estimation** (is goal $G$ reachable by level $k$?).
- **Heuristic computation** for forward-search planners (FF, Fast Downward).
- **Constraint propagation** through mutex relations.
- **Anytime behavior** because the graph can be expanded until a plan emerges.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Planning Graph — Step-by-Step Construction

Let $\Pi = \langle P, A, I, G \rangle$ be a STRIPS planning problem where:
- $P$ = set of propositions (ground atoms),
- $A$ = set of ground actions,
- $I \subseteq P$ = initial state,
- $G \subseteq P$ = goal specification.

### Step 1 — Initialize Fact Level $S_0$

$$S_0 \;\leftarrow\; I \quad \text{(the initial state)}$$

No mutexes exist at $S_0$ (every proposition is "trivially co-possible").

### Step 2 — Initialize Action Level $A_0$

For every proposition $p \in S_0$, introduce a **no-op / persistence action** $noOp(p)$ whose precondition, add-list, and delete-list are all $\{p\}$. Every real action $a \in A$ whose $pre(a) \subseteq S_0$ is also added to $A_0$.

### Step 3 — Compute Mutexes at $A_0$ (Action Mutex)

Two actions $a_1, a_2$ at the same action level are marked **mutex** if **any** of these holds:

1. **Inconsistent Effects:** $add(a_1) \cap del(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ **OR** $add(a_2) \cap del(a_1) \neq \varnothing$.
2. **Interference:** $del(a_1) \cap add(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ **OR** $del(a_2) \cap add(a_1) \neq \varnothing$.
3. **Competing Needs:** There exists $p, q \in P$ such that $p \in pre(a_1)$, $q \in pre(a_2)$, and $\{p, q\}$ is a *fact mutex* at $S_0$.

### Step 4 — Expand to Fact Level $S_{i+1}$

$$S_{i+1} \;=\; S_i \;\cup\; \bigcup_{a \in A_i} add(a)$$

Each proposition $p \in S_{i+1}$ carries **maintenance links** from the corresponding $noOp(p)$ and **producer links** from any action that added it.

### Step 5 — Compute Mutexes at $S_{i+1}$ (Fact Mutex)

Two propositions $p, q \in S_{i+1}$ are **fact-mutex** if **every** action pair $(a_p, a_q)$ that produces $p$ and $q$ respectively is an *action-mutex*. Formally:

$$\forall\, a_p \in producers(p),\ \forall\, a_q \in producers(q):\ (a_p, a_q) \in mutex(A_i) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (p, q) \in mutex(S_{i+1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination Lemma (Blum & Furst):** A planning graph reaches a **fixed point** $S_\infty$ where $S_{i+1} = S_i$ *and* the mutex relation stabilizes. Beyond this level, no new facts or non-mutex pairs ever appear.

### Step 6 — Plan Extraction (Backward Search)

- Starting from the lowest level $k$ where every $g \in G$ is present in $S_k$ and **all pairs in $G$ are non-mutex**, perform a **backward-chaining regression search**.
- At each level, select a consistent (non-mutex) *subset* of actions whose effects cover the current subgoal and whose preconditions form the new subgoal.
- If extraction fails at level $k$, expand one more level and retry.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | Boundary / Unit Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plan horizon (GraphPlan) | $h_{GP} \;=\; \min\{\,k \mid G \subseteq S_k \;\wedge\; \neg mutex(G)\,\}$ | Smallest $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | Max-level heuristic | $h_{max}(s) \;=\; \max_{g \in G(s)} level(g)$ | Computed from graph expansion at $s$ |
| 3 | Additive heuristic | $h_{add}(s) \;=\; \sum_{g \in G(s)} level(g)$ | Sum of first-level costs |
| 4 | Fact-level update | $S_{i+1} = S_i \cup \bigcup_{a \in A_i} add(a)$ | Monotonic non-decreasing |
| 5 | Action-mutex (Competing Needs) | $\exists\, p \in pre(a_1),\ q \in pre(a_2):\ (p,q) \in mutex(S_i)$ | Necessary condition |
| 6 | Action-mutex (Inconsistent Effects) | $add(a_1) \cap del(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ | Sufficient condition |
| 7 | Fact-mutex propagation | $(p,q) \in mutex(S_{i+1}) \iff \forall (a_p, a_q):\ a_p \in prod(p),\ a_q \in prod(q) \Rightarrow mutex(a_p, a_q)$ | Logical equivalence |
| 8 | Graph size per level | $\mathcal{O}(\vert P \vert^2 + \vert A \vert^2)$ | Polynomial in problem size |
| 9 | Termination bound | $\vert P \vert$ fact levels (worst case) | $\leq$ number of propositions |
| 10 | No-op invariant | $noOp(p):\ pre=\{p\},\ add=\{p\},\ del=\varnothing$ | Maintains proposition persistence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In modern robotic task planners like **ROSPlan** and **PDDL4J**, the planning-graph-derived heuristics $h_{max}$ and $h_{add}$ dramatically prune the search space, reducing planning time from minutes to milliseconds on benchmark domains such as *Blocksworld*, *Logistics*, and *Hospitals*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The "Three-Block Stack" Domain

Consider a STRIPS problem with three blocks **A**, **B**, **C** and a table **T**.

**Goal:** $\{(on\ A\ B),\ (on\ B\ C)\}$

**Action schema (ground instances):**

1. $stack(X, Y):\ pre=\{holding(X),\ clear(Y)\},\ add=\{on(X,Y),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\},\ del=\{holding(X),\ clear(Y)\}$
2. $unstack(X, Y):\ pre=\{on(X,Y),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\},\ add=\{holding(X),\ clear(Y)\},\ del=\{on(X,Y),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\}$
3. $pickup(X):\ pre=\{ontable(X),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\},\ add=\{holding(X)\},\ del=\{ontable(X),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\}$
4. $putdown(X):\ pre=\{holding(X)\},\ add=\{ontable(X),\ clear(X),\ arm\_empty\},\ del=\{holding(X)\}$

**Initial state $I$:** $\{ontable(A),\ ontable(B),\ ontable(C),\ clear(A),\ clear(B),\ clear(C),\ arm\_empty\}$

### Derivation 3.1.1 — Constructing $S_0$ and $A_0$

$S_0 = \{ontable(A), ontable(B), ontable(C), clear(A), clear(B), clear(C), arm\_empty\}$

For $A_0$, we list all real actions whose preconditions are *subsets* of $S_0$ plus all no-ops:

$$A_0 = \big\{ pickup(A),\ pickup(B),\ pickup(C),\ putdown(A),\ putdown(B),\ putdown(C), \ldots, noOp(p)\ \forall\, p \in S_0 \big\}$$

Action-mutex checks at $A_0$:

- $pickup(A)$ and $pickup(B)$: Both need $arm\_empty$ — but $arm\_empty$ is a singleton in $S_0$ (no competing-need mutex yet since $S_0$ has no mutexes). However, their *effects* delete $clear(A)$ and $clear(B)$ respectively, while their preconditions require the same fact $arm\_empty$. We must still check $S_0$-mutexes: there are none at level 0. So technically not mutex at $A_0$ — both can be "considered executable" from $S_0$.

> This reflects the well-known GraphPlan property: at the very first level, the graph is overly optimistic. Mutexes tighten in later levels.

### Derivation 3.1.2 — Computing $S_1$

For every action in $A_0$, we take its add-list union with $S_0$:

$$S_1 = S_0 \;\cup\; \{holding(A),\ holding(B),\ holding(C),\ ontable(A),\ ontable(B),\ ontable(C),\ clear(A),\ clear(B),\ clear(C),\ arm\_empty\}$$

In our example, $S_1 = S_0 \cup \{holding(A), holding(B), holding(C)\}$ — the only *new* facts are the *holding* propositions.

### Derivation 3.1.3 — Computing Fact-Mutexes at $S_1$

Two facts $holding(A)$ and $holding(B)$ are mutex at $S_1$ if **every** action producing $holding(A)$ is mutex with **every** action producing $holding(B)$.

- Producer of $holding(A)$: $\ pickup(A)$ and $noOp(holding(A))$ (but $holding(A) \notin S_0$, so no no-op).
- Producer of $holding(B)$: $\ pickup(B)$ only.
- Check: Is $pickup(A)$ mutex with $pickup(B)$? Both need $arm\_empty$, which is non-mutex in $S_0$. Both delete different facts. So they are **not** mutex at $A_0$.

Therefore $(holding(A), holding(B)) \notin mutex(S_1)$. This illustrates a crucial subtlety: the planning graph *under-approximates* reachability at level 1 because mutexes are still tightening.

### Derivation 3.1.4 — The Termination Argument

For any finite STRIPS problem with $\vert P \vert = n$ propositions, define:

$$S_i \subseteq S_{i+1} \subseteq S_{i+2} \subseteq \ldots \subseteq P$$

Since $P$ is finite, the chain of subsets stabilizes within $n$ steps:

$$\exists\, k \leq n:\ S_{k+1} = S_k \quad \text{and} \quad mutex(S_{k+1}) = mutex(S_k)$$

This fixed point $S_k$ is denoted $S_\infty$ — the planning graph has fully leveled off, and no further expansion can produce new information.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mutex-Relation Taxonomy

| Mutex Type | Where It Lives | Trigger Condition | Example in Blocksworld |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Competing Needs** | Action level $A_i$ | Two actions need mutex preconditions | $stack(A,B)$ needs $holding(A)$; $pickup(C)$ needs $arm\_empty$ — if $arm\_empty$ is mutex with $holding(A)$, they conflict |
| **Inconsistent Effects** | Action level $A_i$ | One deletes what the other adds | $unstack(A,B)$ adds $holding(A)$; $stack(A,B)$ deletes $holding(A)$ |
| **Interference** | Action level $A_i$ | One deletes a precondition of the other | $putdown(A)$ deletes $holding(A)$ while $stack(A,B)$ needs $holding(A)$ |
| **Inconsistent Support** | Fact level $S_{i+1}$ | Every producer pair of two facts is mutex | $on(A,B)$ and $on(A,C)$ (A cannot be on two blocks) |

## 3.3 The Plan Extraction Algorithm — Worked Pseudocode Walkthrough

**Input:** Planning graph $PG$ with levels $0, 1, \ldots, k$, goal $G$.

**Algorithm:**

1. If $\exists g \in G$ with $g \notin S_k$: return failure (expand graph).
2. If $G$ contains a mutex pair: return failure (expand graph).
3. Choose a proposition $g \in G$ (e.g., the one with fewest available producers).
4. For each *non-mutex* action $a$ at level $k-1$ with $g \in add(a)$:
   - Recursively extract a plan for $pre(a) \cup (G \setminus \{g\})$ at level $k-1$.
   - If successful, return $a \cup$ recursive-subplan.
5. If no such action yields a subplan, mark $(k-1, g)$ as a **no-good** and backtrack.
6. If all choices exhausted: return failure (expand graph).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoization of No-Goods:** To avoid exponential re-exploration, GraphPlan memoizes *no-goods* — sets of goals that cannot be simultaneously achieved at a given level. This is the key polynomial-time elegance of the algorithm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Planning Graph Architecture for Three-Block Domain

```mermaid

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Plan Extraction Phase

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start: Graph Expanded to Level k]) --> chkGoal{All g in G present at Sk?}
    chkGoal -- No --> expand[Expand Graph by One Level]
    expand --> chkGoal
    chkGoal -- Yes --> chkMutex{Any mutex pair in G at Sk?}
    chkMutex -- Yes --> expand
    chkMutex -- No --> pickG[Pick goal g in G with fewest producers]
    pickG --> iterAct{For each non-mutex action a with g in add a at level k minus 1}
    iterAct -- Found --> recurse[Recursively solve pre a union G minus g at level k minus 1]
    recurse --> success{Subplan found?}
    success -- Yes --> returnPlan[Return plan: a union subplan]
    success -- No --> markNG[Mark level k minus 1, g as no good]
    markNG --> iterAct
    iterAct -- Exhausted --> expand
    returnPlan --> stop([Output Valid Plan])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GraphPlan in a Robotic Stack

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input["Input Layer"]
        PDDL[PDDL Domain File]
        Prob[Problem Instance]
    end

    subgraph Grounder["Grounding Layer"]
        Norm[Normalization]
        Inst[Action Instantiation]
    end

    subgraph GraphEngine["Planning Graph Engine"]
        FactLvl[Fact Level Builder]
        ActLvl[Action Level Builder]
        Mutex[Mutex Propagator]
        Fixed[Fixed Point Detector]
    end

    subgraph Extractor["Plan Extractor"]
        Back[Backward Search]
        NoGood[No-Good Memoizer]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output Layer"]
        Plan[Plan Sequence]
        Heur[h_max / h_add Heuristic]
    end

    PDDL --> Norm
    Prob --> Norm
    Norm --> Inst
    Inst --> FactLvl
    Inst --> ActLvl
    FactLvl --> Mutex
    ActLvl --> Mutex
    Mutex --> Fixed
    Fixed --> Back
    Fixed --> Heur
    Back --> NoGood
    NoGood --> Back
    Back --> Plan
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering note:** In a real ROS-based robotic deployment, the *Planning Graph Engine* and *Plan Extractor* are packaged as a C++ library (e.g., **OPTIC**, **POPF**) that the robot's task scheduler calls at each replanning cycle, typically every **100–500 ms** in dynamic environments.

---
