<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Planning Graph. List its two main types of levels.**

**Model Answer:**
A planning graph is a directed, leveled, polynomial-size data structure that encodes a STRIPS planning problem, alternating between **Fact Levels (S-levels)** containing all reachable propositions and **Action Levels (A-levels)** containing all possibly executable actions (including no-op/persistence actions). Edges encode preconditions, add-effects, and delete-effects, while **mutual exclusion (mutex)** relations track pairs of facts or actions that cannot co-exist at the same level.

*(Expected: 4–5 sentences, 1 small diagram of two-level graph optional.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a mutual exclusion (mutex) relation in a planning graph? State the two mutex conditions at the action level.**

**Model Answer:**
A mutex relation is a binary constraint indicating that two nodes at the same level **cannot co-occur** in any valid plan. At the **action level**, two actions $a_1$ and $a_2$ are mutex if any of:
1. **Inconsistent effects:** $add(a_1) \cap del(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ or $add(a_2) \cap del(a_1) \neq \varnothing$.
2. **Interference:** $del(a_1) \cap add(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ or $del(a_2) \cap add(a_1) \neq \varnothing$.
3. **Competing needs:** a precondition of $a_1$ and a precondition of $a_2$ are themselves fact-mutex at the previous fact level.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the structure of a planning graph with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **no-op (persistence) actions** and **mutual exclusion (mutex)** relations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **GraphPlan algorithm** for plan extraction. Show how the algorithm decides when to stop expanding the graph. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A planning graph consists of alternating **Fact Levels ($S_0, S_1, \ldots$)** and **Action Levels ($A_0, A_1, \ldots$)**.

- $S_0$ is the initial state.
- $A_i$ contains every ground action whose preconditions are in $S_i$, *plus* a no-op action for every proposition in $S_i$.
- $S_{i+1}$ is $S_i$ union all add-effects of actions in $A_i$.
- Mutex relations are recorded at each level: action-mutex at $A_i$, fact-mutex at $S_{i+1}$.

**Role of no-op actions:** They allow propositions to *persist* across time steps without change, ensuring that every fact in $S_i$ has a path forward to $S_{i+1}$. This is essential because the planning graph would otherwise "forget" facts not used by any action.

**Role of mutex relations:** They encode the *negative information* that two propositions cannot be true together (e.g., $holding(A)$ and $arm\_empty$ after picking up) or that two actions cannot be executed in parallel. This tightens the optimistic initial estimate of the graph.

*(Include a two-level diagram of the S₀→A₀→S₁→A₁→S₂ structure.)*

**(b)** The **GraphPlan algorithm** proceeds in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Graph Expansion:**
- Start with $S_0 = I$.
- Repeatedly add a new action level $A_i$ and fact level $S_{i+1}$.
- Compute mutex relations at each new level.
- Stop when $S_{i+1} = S_i$ and mutex relations stabilize (**fixed point reached**), or earlier if goals become reachable.

**Phase 2 — Plan Extraction (Backward Search):**
- Find the smallest level $k$ where $G \subseteq S_k$ and no two goals are mutually exclusive.
- Recursively select for each goal a non-mutex *producer action* at level $k-1$.
- The preconditions of chosen actions become the new subgoal at level $k-1$.
- On failure, mark no-goods and retry at level $k+1$.

**Stopping Criterion:**
The algorithm terminates when either (i) a valid plan is extracted, or (ii) the **graph reaches its fixed point** (leveled off) without all goals being non-mutex — in which case the planning problem is **unsolvable**.

**Worked Example (Three-Block Stack):** Already demonstrated in Section 3.1 of these notes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Define mutual exclusion in a planning graph. Explain the three conditions under which two actions are marked as mutex. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** How does the planning graph provide **heuristic estimates** for forward-state-space planners? Explain $h_{max}$ and $h_{add}$ with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(See Part A Question 2 for the mutex definition.)* The three conditions under which two actions $a_1$ and $a_2$ at the same action level are marked mutex are:

1. **Inconsistent Effects:** One action asserts the negation (or deletes) of what the other asserts. Formally, $add(a_1) \cap del(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ OR $add(a_2) \cap del(a_1) \neq \varnothing$.

2. **Interference:** One action deletes a precondition of the other. Formally, $del(a_1) \cap pre(a_2) \neq \varnothing$ OR $del(a_2) \cap pre(a_1) \neq \varnothing$. Equivalently expressed via add/delete of the other.

3. **Competing Needs:** There exist preconditions $p \in pre(a_1)$ and $q \in pre(a_2)$ such that $(p, q)$ is a fact-mutex at the previous level — i.e., $p$ and $q$ cannot both be true simultaneously, hence $a_1$ and $a_2$ cannot both execute.

**(b)** Planning graphs are the foundation of the most influential *domain-independent heuristics* in classical planning:

**$h_{max}$ (Max-Level Heuristic):**
For a state $s$ and goal set $G$, construct a planning graph from $s$ and record, for each proposition $p$, the **first level** at which $p$ appears:

$$level(p) = \min\{\,k \mid p \in S_k\,\}$$

Then define:

$$h_{max}(s) \;=\; \max_{g \in G(s)} level(g)$$

This is an **admissible** (never overestimates) heuristic — it ignores sub-goal interactions.

**$h_{add}$ (Additive / Sum-Level Heuristic):**
Same construction, but sum the level costs:

$$h_{add}(s) \;=\; \sum_{g \in G(s)} level(g)$$

This is **inadmissible** (may overestimate) but often much more informative, leading to dramatically faster search in domains like *Blocksworld* and *Logistics*.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose goal $G = \{on(A,B), on(B,C)\}$ and the planning graph shows:

$$level(on(A,B)) = 3, \quad level(on(B,C)) = 4$$

Then:
- $h_{max} = \max(3, 4) = 4$
- $h_{add} = 3 + 4 = 7$

If the true optimal plan length is **5 steps**, then $h_{max} = 4$ is admissible, while $h_{add} = 7$ overestimates — but guides the search much more aggressively.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently:
> - Confuse **action-mutex** with **fact-mutex** conditions (they are different! action-mutex has *three* rules, fact-mutex has only one).
> - Forget that **no-op actions** must be added at *every* action level (not just the first).
> - State that planning graphs *solve* planning — they are a *representation*; the extraction phase still requires search.
> - Confuse the **fixed-point termination** with "no plan exists" — the graph only levels off; if goals are still mutex, the problem is unsolvable.
> - Drop the **memoized no-goods** explanation in the extraction phase, losing 2–3 marks on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (must memorize):** A planning graph is a *polynomial-size, leveled, alternating fact-action representation* of a STRIPS planning problem.
- **Two level types:** Fact level ($S_i$) and Action level ($A_i$).
- **Three edge types:** Precondition edges, add-effect edges, delete-effect edges (plus maintenance edges for no-ops).
- **No-op (persistence) action:** $noOp(p):\ pre = add = \{p\},\ del = \varnothing$. Added for *every* proposition at every level.
- **Three action-mutex conditions:** Inconsistent effects, Interference, Competing needs.
- **One fact-mutex condition:** Inconsistent support — every producer pair of the two facts is action-mutex.
- **Termination:** Graph reaches a **fixed point** within at most $\vert P \vert$ fact levels (Blum–Furst theorem).
- **Plan extraction:** Backward search from the lowest level where goals are present *and non-mutex*; uses **no-good memoization**.
- **Heuristic extraction:** $h_{max}$ (admissible) and $h_{add}$ (inadmissible but informative) — derived from first-appearance levels of propositions.
- **Computational complexity:** Polynomial *per level*; total bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\vert P \vert^2 + \vert A \vert^2) \cdot \vert P \vert$.
- **Origin:** Blum & Furst, 1997 — *Artificial Intelligence* journal.
- **Applications:** Heuristic guidance in FF, Fast Downward, LAMA; ROSPlan integration for mobile robots; task planning in NASA autonomy frameworks (EUROPA-2).
- **Common confusions to avoid:** Planning graph ≠ search graph (state-space search tree); mutex at action level ≠ mutex at fact level; no-op is *not* optional.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning with Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning with Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 Module 2: Planning with Propositional Logic — Section 1: Core Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a *high-weightage* component of Module 2, bridging classical AI search (Module 1) with knowledge-based reasoning. It is a guaranteed question area in ESE Part A (3 marks) and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-part in Part B.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Planning with Propositional Logic** is a class of automated planning in Artificial Intelligence where the planning domain, initial state, goal state, and actions are all encoded using **propositional (Boolean) logic sentences**. A *plan* is then derived by logically inferring a sequence of actions that transforms the initial state into a goal state, typically by reducing planning to a **satisfiability (SAT) problem** or by forward/backward search over a logical representation.

The foundational framework is the **STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver)** representation, introduced by Fikes and Nilsson in **1971**, which formalizes planning as a deductive reasoning problem over world states.

Mathematically, a propositional planning problem is a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \langle P, S_0, S_G, A, \gamma \rangle
$$

where:
- $P$ is a finite set of **propositional variables** (ground atoms representing world facts)
- $S_0 \subseteq P$ is the **initial state** (a complete valuation over $P$)
- $S_G \subseteq P$ is the **partial goal specification** (a conjunction of propositional literals)
- $A$ is a finite set of **action schemas** (also called *operators*)
- $\gamma : A \times S \to S$ is the **state-transition function**

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-World Assumption (CWA):** In STRIPS-style planning, any proposition *not asserted true* in a state is assumed to be **false**. This is a critical assumption that makes the propositional encoding tractable.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Smart Recipe" 🥘

Imagine you are teaching a robot to make a cup of tea. Instead of writing code, you write logical sentences:

> *"IF the kettle is empty AND the tap is reachable, THEN the robot CAN fill the kettle."*
> *"After filling, the kettle BECOMES full, and the tap is no longer being used."*

This is **planning with propositional logic** — you describe the **world** with Boolean facts (kettle_full, water_boiled, cup_has_tea) and describe **actions** as logical rules that change those facts. The planner's job is to **logically deduce** a sequence of actions (a "recipe") that gets from the messy kitchen to a perfect cup of tea.

**Three ingredients you must always specify:**
1. 🍳 **Initial state** (the starting mess in the kitchen)
2. 🍵 **Goal state** (the desired cup of tea)
3. ⚙️ **Actions with preconditions & effects** (the recipe steps)

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition | KTU 2024 Significance |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **State** | A complete assignment of truth values to all propositions in $P$ | Universe of discourse |
| **Atom / Literal** | A propositional variable $p$ or its negation $\neg p$ | Atomic facts in the world |
| **Ground Action** | A fully instantiated action schema (no free variables) | Executable step in a plan |
| **Precondition** | A conjunction of literals that must hold to execute an action | `Pre(a)` |
| **Add List** | Literals that become true after the action | `Add(a)` — the *positive effects* |
| **Delete List** | Literals that become false after the action | `Del(a)` — the *negative effects* |
| **CWA** | Anything not asserted is assumed false | Default reasoning assumption |
| **Frame Problem** | How to express what *doesn't* change when an action occurs | Solved implicitly by STRIPS |

---

## 1.4 Why Propositional Logic? (The Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Application Domains:**
> - 🤖 **Robotic manipulation** (NASA's Deep Space 1 Remote Agent used propositional planning)
> - 🏭 **Factory automation & scheduling** (job-shop scheduling)
> - 🚗 **Autonomous vehicle behavior planning** (lane changes, intersection negotiation)
> - 🎮 **Video game AI** (strategy game opponents, NPC behavior trees)
> - 🧬 **Bioinformatics** (DNA sequencing assembly planning)

The advantage over plain search (Module 1) is **declarativity** — we describe *what* the world looks like and *what* actions do, rather than hard-coding a search graph. This allows the planner to **reason** about the consequences of its own actions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The STRIPS Representation — Anatomy of an Action

A **STRIPS action schema** has three logical components. For an action named $a$ with variables, the schema is written as:

$$
a: \text{name}(x_1, \ldots, x_k) \\
\quad \text{Pre: } \pi_1 \wedge \pi_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge \pi_m \\
\quad \text{Add: } \alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_p \\
\quad \text{Del: } \delta_1, \delta_2, \ldots, \delta_q
$$

**Operational interpretation:**
- **Precondition list ($\pi$):** A set of positive literals that *must all* be true in the current state for the action to be applicable.
- **Add list ($\alpha$):** The set of positive literals that become true *after* the action executes.
- **Delete list ($\delta$):** The set of positive literals that become false *after* the action executes.
- **STRIPS Assumption:** If a literal is *not* in the add or delete list, its truth value is *unchanged* (this implicitly solves the frame problem).

> [!NOTE]
> **The STRIPS Closed-World Side-Effect:** Because anything not mentioned in Add/Del is preserved, and anything not in $S_0$ is assumed false, STRIPS assumes the *world only changes in the ways explicitly described by actions*. This is a major limitation in dynamic environments but keeps the logic tractable.

---

## 2.2 Situation Calculus — The Logical Spine

Introduced by **John McCarthy** (1963), Situation Calculus represents *change* as a first-order logic over situations (world histories).

**Key predicates:**
- $\text{At}(p, s)$: proposition $p$ is true in situation $s$
- $\text{Result}(a, s)$: the situation resulting from performing action $a$ in $s$
- $\text{Holds}(p, s)$: an alternative formulation meaning "fluent $p$ is true in $s$"

**Two foundational axioms:**

**(a) Effect Axiom** (What an action *does*):
$$
\forall s.\, \text{At}(p, s) \land \text{Poss}(a, s) \land (p \in \text{Add}(a)) \rightarrow \text{At}(p, \text{Result}(a, s))
$$

**(b) Frame Axiom** (What an action *does not* change):
$$
\forall s.\, \text{At}(p, s) \land p \notin \text{Add}(a) \land p \notin \text{Del}(a) \land \text{Poss}(a, s) \rightarrow \text{At}(p, \text{Result}(a, s))
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The **Frame Problem** in pure Situation Calculus requires $O(|P| \cdot |A|)$ frame axioms — one for every (proposition, action) pair. This is the *representational frame problem* and motivated the *STRIPS efficiency shortcut*.

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula & Theorem Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation Used |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | STRIPS Planning Tuple | $\mathcal{P} = \langle P, S_0, S_G, A, \gamma \rangle$ | Domain spec |
| 2 | Action Applicability | $a$ applicable in $s$ iff $\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s$ | $\text{Applic}(a, s)$ |
| 3 | State Transition | $\gamma(a, s) = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$ | Successor state |
| 4 | Plan Validity | A plan $\pi = \langle a_1, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ is valid iff $S_G \subseteq \gamma^*(\pi, S_0)$ | $\models \pi$ |
| 5 | SATPlan Depth Bound | $\text{SAT}(T) = \text{CNF}(S_0 \cup \text{Pre} \cup \text{Eff} \cup \text{Frame}_T \cup \neg S_G)$ | Bounded satisfiability |
| 6 | GraphPlan: Fact Level $i$ | $\text{Fact}_0 = S_0$; $\text{Fact}_{i+1} = \text{Fact}_i \cup \text{Add}(\text{Act}_i)$ | Polynomial |
| 7 | GraphPlan: Action Level $i$ | $\text{Act}_i = \{a \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq \text{Fact}_i\}$ | Polynomial |
| 8 | Mutex (Action) | Two actions $a_1, a_2$ at level $i$ mutex if: (i) interference, (ii) competing needs, (iii) inconsistent effects | $a_1 \perp_i a_2$ |
| 9 | Mutex (Fact) | Two facts $f_1, f_2$ at level $i$ mutex if all action pairs producing them are mutex | $f_1 \perp_i f_2$ |
| 10 | Plan Length Lower Bound | $\text{LB}(\pi) = \min\{i \mid S_G \subseteq \text{Fact}_i \text{ and not all pairwise mutex}\}$ | GraphPlan termination |
| 11 | Frame Axiom Count | $\vert P \vert \times \vert A \vert$ explicit frame axioms (situation calculus) | $O(nm)$ blowup |
| 12 | STRIPS Efficiency | $O(1)$ implicit frame axioms per action | Tractability gain |

---

## 2.4 Algorithm 1: Progression (Forward) Search in STRIPS

**Why it works:** STRIPS's CWA makes successor states uniquely determined — there is no "unknown" — so we can apply standard uninformed/informed search from $S_0$ until $S_G$ is reached.

**Why it fails sometimes:** Progression suffers from the *irrelevant action problem* — many actions are applicable in any state, so the branching factor explodes (e.g., in blocks world with 10 blocks, $b \approx 20$).

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize search tree with root node $S_0$.
2. Repeat: pick a leaf node $s$ from the frontier.
3. If $S_G \subseteq s$, **return** the plan.
4. Compute the set of *relevant* actions: $R(s) = \{a \in A \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s\}$.
5. For each $a \in R(s)$, generate child $s' = \gamma(a, s)$.
6. Insert $s'$ (with plan label) into frontier.
7. If frontier empty, **return failure**.

---

## 2.5 Algorithm 2: Regression Search in STRIPS

Regression searches *backwards* from $S_G$. The regression of a goal $g$ over an action $a$ is:

$$
\text{Regress}(g, a) = (g \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)
$$

*provided that* $g \cap \text{Del}(a) = \emptyset$ (otherwise $a$ would *clobber* a goal literal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of Regression:** The branching factor is smaller because we only consider actions that achieve at least one unsatisfied goal literal. **Disadvantage:** States are no longer fully grounded — they become partial states, complicating CWA.

---

## 2.6 Algorithm 3: Planning as Satisfiability (SATPlan)

**Core idea:** Encode "does a plan of length $T$ exist?" as a propositional CNF formula. If the formula is **satisfiable**, the satisfying assignment *decodes* into the plan.

**Encoding ingredients** (using propositional variable $F^t$ meaning "fact $F$ is true at time $t$"):

1. **Initial state axioms:** $\bigwedge_{F \in S_0} F^0 \;\land\; \bigwedge_{F \notin S_0} \neg F^0$
2. **Precondition axioms:** For each action $a$ at time $t$: $a^t \rightarrow \bigwedge_{F \in \text{Pre}(a)} F^t$
3. **Effect axioms:** For each action $a$ and each $F \in \text{Add}(a)$: $a^t \rightarrow F^{t+1}$ (and analogously for delete).
4. **Frame axioms:** For each $F$ and $t$: $F^t \wedge F^{t+1} \rightarrow \bigvee_{a : F \in \text{Add}(a)} a^t$ (if $F$ is true at $t$ and $t+1$, some action must have *caused* it to remain true).
5. **Action exclusion:** At most one action per time step: $\bigwedge_{t} \bigwedge_{a \neq b} \neg(a^t \wedge b^t)$
6. **Goal axioms:** $\bigwedge_{F \in S_G} F^T$
7. **Negated goal at earlier steps:** $\bigwedge_{t < T} \neg(S_G \subseteq s^t)$ — used to *minimize* plan length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (SATPlan Completeness):** *A planning problem $\mathcal{P}$ has a plan of length $\le T$ if and only if the SATPlan encoding $\text{SAT}(T)$ is satisfiable.* This reduces planning — an PSPACE-complete problem in general — to NP-complete SAT, for which powerful solvers (DPLL, CDCL) exist.

---

## 2.7 Algorithm 4: Planning Graph (GraphPlan)

The **planning graph** is a polynomial-size, layered data structure introduced by Blum and Furst (1997) that **estimates** the distance to the goal and provides a structured search space.

**Structure (alternating layers):**
- Layer 0: Proposition (fact) layer = $S_0$
- Layer 1: Action layer = all actions whose preconditions are in fact layer 0
- Layer 2: Fact layer 1 = all Add-effects of layer 1 actions
- ... continues until **leveling off** (two consecutive fact layers become identical)
- **Persistence actions (no-ops):** for every fact $F$ not deleted, a no-op action preserves $F$ across layers.

**Mutex conditions (the heart of GraphPlan):**

1. **Inconsistent Effects:** $a_1$ adds $p$ and $a_2$ adds $\neg p$ (or vice versa) $\Rightarrow a_1 \perp a_2$.
2. **Interference:** $a_1$ deletes a precondition of $a_2$ (or vice versa) $\Rightarrow a_1 \perp a_2$.
3. **Competing Needs:** $a_1$ needs $p$ and $a_2$ needs $q$, where $p \perp q$ at the previous fact layer $\Rightarrow a_1 \perp a_2$.
4. **Inconsistent Support:** $f_1$ and $f_2$ are mutex at a fact layer if *every* action pair producing them is mutex (or one is unsupported).

> [!NOTE]
> **Termination Guarantee:** The planning graph always levels off in at most $2^{\vert P \vert}$ layers (since there are only $2^{\vert P \vert}$ possible fact subsets), but in practice it levels off in $O(\vert P \vert \cdot \vert A \vert)$ steps. This gives a polynomial approximation of plan length.

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Example

---

## 3.1 The Canonical Worked Example: Blocks World

We will use the **Blocks World** — the standard pedagogical example in KTU examinations — to derive every concept in full.

**Domain:**
- Three blocks: A, B, C
- One robot arm
- A table surface
- Propositions (the vocabulary $P$):
  - $\text{On}(x, y)$: block $x$ is on block $y$ (or table)
  - $\text{OnTable}(x)$: block $x$ is directly on the table
  - $\text{Clear}(x)$: nothing is on block $x$ (arm can grasp it)
  - $\text{ArmEmpty}$: robot arm is not holding anything
  - $\text{Holding}(x)$: arm is holding block $x$

**Problem Instance:**
- **Initial state $S_0$:**
  - $\text{On}(A, B)$, $\text{On}(B, C)$, $\text{OnTable}(C)$, $\text{Clear}(A)$, $\text{ArmEmpty}$
  - (By CWA, all other facts — e.g., $\text{On}(B, A)$, $\text{On}(C, B)$ — are false)
- **Goal $S_G$:**
  - $\text{On}(A, B)$, $\text{On}(C, A)$, $\text{OnTable}(B)$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Deriving the STRIPS Action Schemas

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Strategy:** We derive the *unstack* and *stack* schemas. Students are expected to derive the other two (*pickup* from table, *putdown* on table) by symmetry in exams.

### Action Schema 1: $\text{Unstack}(x, y)$ — Pick up $x$ from on top of $y$

**Derivation logic:** The arm must already be empty, $x$ must be clear (nothing on $x$), and $x$ must currently be on $y$. After unstacking, the arm holds $x$, $y$ becomes clear, and the relation $\text{On}(x, y)$ is *deleted* (no longer true).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unstack}(x, y): \quad & \text{Pre: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{Clear}(x) \wedge \text{On}(x, y) \\
& \text{Add: } \text{Holding}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(y) \\
& \text{Del: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{On}(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each literal:**
- `Pre: ArmEmpty` — hand is free to grasp
- `Pre: Clear(x)` — nothing sits on $x$ to obstruct the grasp
- `Pre: On(x, y)` — $x$ must currently be on $y$ to lift it
- `Add: Holding(x)` — after grasping, arm holds $x$
- `Add: Clear(y)` — once $x$ is removed, $y$ has nothing on it
- `Del: ArmEmpty` — arm is no longer free
- `Del: On(x, y)` — the relationship is broken

### Action Schema 2: $\text{Stack}(x, y)$ — Place $x$ on top of $y$

**Derivation logic:** The arm is already holding $x$, and $y$ must be clear. After stacking, $x$ is on $y$, $y$ is no longer clear, the arm becomes empty, and the holding relation is deleted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack}(x, y): \quad & \text{Pre: } \text{Holding}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(y) \\
& \text{Add: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{On}(x, y) \\
& \text{Del: } \text{Holding}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Action Schemas 3 & 4 (for completeness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pickup}(x): \quad & \text{Pre: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{OnTable}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(x) \\
& \text{Add: } \text{Holding}(x) \\
& \text{Del: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{OnTable}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Putdown}(x): \quad & \text{Pre: } \text{Holding}(x) \\
& \text{Add: } \text{ArmEmpty} \wedge \text{OnTable}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(x) \\
& \text{Del: } \text{Holding}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Hand-Solved Plan via Regression

We want a plan achieving $S_G = \{\text{On}(A, B), \text{On}(C, A), \text{OnTable}(B)\}$.

**Step 1: Achieve $\text{On}(C, A)$.** Looking at the Add lists, the only action that *adds* $\text{On}(C, A)$ is $\text{Stack}(C, A)$. Regression of $S_G$ over $\text{Stack}(C, A)$ requires $S_G \cap \text{Del}(\text{Stack}(C, A)) = \emptyset$ — yes. New sub-goal:

$$
G_1 = (S_G \setminus \{\text{On}(C,A)\}) \cup \text{Pre}(\text{Stack}(C, A)) = \{\text{On}(A,B), \text{OnTable}(B), \text{Holding}(C), \text{Clear}(A)\}
$$

**Step 2: Achieve $\text{Holding}(C)$.** Achieved by $\text{Unstack}(C, ?)$ or $\text{Pickup}(C)$. From $S_0$, $C$ has $B$ on it — wait, recheck: $S_0$ says $\text{On}(B, C)$, so $C$ is *not clear* in $S_0$. So we cannot directly unstack $C$. We must first **clear $C$**, which means moving $B$ off $C$.

This is exactly the kind of step that motivates **partial-order planning** — we don't know yet *where* to put $B$, only that it must be off $C$. We represent the open precondition $\text{Clear}(C)$ as a *flaw* to be resolved later.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-plan so far (partial):**
> $$\text{Stack}(C, A) \leftarrow \text{[achieve: Holding(C) and Clear(A)]} \leftarrow \text{Unstack(C, B)} \text{ or similar}$$

**Step 3: Continue resolution…** (The exam would require you to write the full plan: $\text{Unstack}(A, B)$, $\text{Putdown}(A)$, $\text{Unstack}(B, C)$, $\text{Stack}(B, ?)$, $\text{Pickup}(C)$, $\text{Stack}(C, A)$, $\text{Pickup}(B)$, $\text{Stack}(B, A)$ — a multi-step plan.)

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: SATPlan Encoding for $T = 2$ Time Steps

For the same problem, we want to know: *is there a 2-step plan?* If not, the solver increments $T$.

**Step A — Create propositional variables for each fact at each time:**
- $F^0, F^1, F^2$ for every fact $F$ (so $\vert P \vert \times 3$ variables).
- $a^t$ for every ground action $a$ and time $t \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step B — Initial state clauses (time 0):**
$$
\text{On}(A,B)^0 \wedge \text{On}(B,C)^0 \wedge \text{OnTable}(C)^0 \wedge \text{Clear}(A)^0 \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}^0
$$
and negations of all other facts at time 0.

**Step C — Goal state clauses (time $T=2$):**
$$
\text{On}(A,B)^2 \wedge \text{On}(C,A)^2 \wedge \text{OnTable}(B)^2
$$

**Step D — Precondition axiom (example for $\text{Unstack}(A,B)^0$):**
$$
\text{Unstack}(A,B)^0 \rightarrow \text{ArmEmpty}^0 \wedge \text{Clear}(A)^0 \wedge \text{On}(A,B)^0
$$

**Step E — Effect axiom (positive):**
$$
\text{Unstack}(A,B)^0 \rightarrow \text{Holding}(A)^1 \wedge \text{Clear}(B)^1
$$

**Step F — Effect axiom (negative):**
$$
\text{Unstack}(A,B)^0 \rightarrow \neg\text{ArmEmpty}^1 \wedge \neg\text{On}(A,B)^1
$$

**Step G — Frame axiom (example for $\text{On}(B,C)$ persisting):**
$$
\text{On}(B,C)^0 \wedge \text{On}(B,C)^1 \rightarrow \bigvee_{a : \text{On}(B,C) \in \text{Add}(a)} a^0
$$
i.e., if $\text{On}(B,C)$ is true at $t=0$ and $t=1$, *some* action that *adds* it must have fired at $t=0$.

**Step H — Action exclusion at each step:**
$$
\bigwedge_{a \neq b} \neg(a^t \wedge b^t) \quad \text{for } t = 0, 1
$$

**Step I — Conjunction:** Combine all clauses into one CNF formula. Hand it to a SAT solver. If **UNSAT** for $T=2$, increment $T$ and try $T=3, 4, \ldots$ until **SAT** is found.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works mathematically:** Resolution is *refutation-complete* for propositional logic. A SAT solver explores all possible truth assignments; the action exclusion axiom guarantees at most one action per step, and frame axioms prevent "magic" — every fact's presence must be *causally explained* by an action's effect.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Building a Planning Graph (GraphPlan)

For the blocks world with $S_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,C), \text{OnTable}(C), \text{Clear}(A), \text{ArmEmpty}\}$:

**Fact Layer 0 (F0):** Exactly the five true facts in $S_0$ (plus no-ops for each).

**Action Layer 1 (A1):** An action $a$ is at A1 iff $\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq F0$.
- $\text{Unstack}(A, B)$: $\text{Pre} = \{\text{ArmEmpty}, \text{Clear}(A), \text{On}(A,B)\}$ — *all in F0* ✓ → add
- $\text{Unstack}(B, C)$: needs $\text{Clear}(B)$, but $\text{Clear}(B) \notin F0$ ✗ → exclude
- $\text{Stack}$, $\text{Putdown}$: need $\text{Holding}(x)$, not in F0 ✗ → exclude
- $\text{Pickup}(A)$: needs $\text{OnTable}(A)$, not in F0 ✗ → exclude
- No-ops: one per fact in F0.

**Fact Layer 1 (F1):** $\text{Add}(\text{Unstack}(A,B)) \cup \text{Add}(\text{no-ops})$
- $\text{Holding}(A)$, $\text{Clear}(B)$, plus all of F0 (preserved by no-ops).
- *Mutex candidates at F1:* $\text{Holding}(A)$ is mutex with $\text{ArmEmpty}$ (deleted by unstack, persisted only via no-op — they are inconsistent effects). $\text{On}(A,B)$ and $\text{Clear}(B)$ are mutex (inconsistent support at A1).

**Action Layer 2 (A2):** Add actions whose preconditions are in F1 (and not all-mutex).
- $\text{Stack}(A, B)$: needs $\text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B)$ — both in F1 and *not* mutex at F1 ✓
- $\text{Putdown}(A)$: needs $\text{Holding}(A)$ ✓
- $\text{Unstack}(B, C)$: needs $\text{Clear}(B)$ ✓
- $\text{Pickup}(B)$: needs $\text{Clear}(B), \text{OnTable}(B), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — but $\text{ArmEmpty} \perp \text{Holding}(A)$ at F1, so it is mutex ✗
- ...

**Fact Layer 2 (F2):** = union of adds. We check: is $S_G \subseteq F2$? If yes *and* no two goal facts are pairwise mutex at F2, **a plan exists of length $\le 2$**. Otherwise, expand further.

---

## 3.6 The Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Search Direction | Branching Factor | Plan Optimality | Key Limitation | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Progression (Forward)** | $S_0 \to S_G$ | High (irrelevant actions) | Optimal with $A^*$ | State-space explosion | Medium |
| **Regression (Backward)** | $S_G \to S_0$ | Moderate | Optimal with $A^*$ | Partial-state complexity | Medium |
| **SATPlan** | Both (bounded) | Variable | Optimal for minimal $T$ | SAT encoding overhead | **High** |
| **GraphPlan** | Forward, leveled | Pruned by mutex | Optimal for leveled graph | Polynomial-time only | **High** |
| **Partial-Order Planning (POP)** | Least-commitment | Low | Not always optimal | Flaw-resolution search | **High** |
| **Situation Calculus (deductive)** | Forward (proof) | Low | Complete but slow | Frame axiom blowup | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU 2024:** Modern industrial planners (Fast Downward, LAMA) combine the *reachability heuristics* from GraphPlan with the *delete-relaxation* idea. The conceptual lineage from STRIPS → SATPlan → GraphPlan → Heuristic Search is the most testable arc in this module.

```mermaid
    Check -- "Pre(a) ⊄ s" --> Skip["Action Not Applicable"]
    Apply --> Compute["s' = s ∪ Add(a)"]
    Compute --> Subtract["s' = s' \\ Del(a)"]
    Subtract --> SPrime["New State s'"]
    SPrime --> Check2{"S_G ⊆ s' ?"}
    Check2 -- "Yes" --> Plan["✓ Plan Found"]
    Check2 -- "No" --> S0
```

```mermaid
    FP["Frame Problem:<br>What does NOT change<br>when an action fires?"]
    FP --> SC["Situation Calculus Solution:<br>Explicit frame axioms<br>O(|P| × |A|) clauses"]
    FP --> SS["STRIPS Solution:<br>Implicit frame:<br>anything not in Add/Del persists"]
    FP --> R["Reified Solution (Event Calculus):<br>Predicates Initiates(e, f, t),<br>Terminates(e, f, t)"]

    SC --> Lim1["Limitation: blowup for large domains"]
    SS --> Adv1["Advantage: O(1) per action,<br>polynomial planning"]
    R --> Adv2["Advantage: handles concurrent<br>and exogenous events"]

    style FP fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style SS fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: The Four Main Planning Paradigms (Comparative Block Architecture)
