# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two 3-mark questions per module, direct recall/short-application. Each answer should be ~80-120 words.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the STRIPS representation of a planning problem. List the three components of a STRIPS action schema with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **STRIPS planning problem** is a tuple $\mathcal{P} = \langle P, S_0, S_G, A, \gamma \rangle$ where $P$ is a finite set of propositional variables, $S_0$ is the initial state, $S_G$ is the goal (a conjunction of literals), $A$ is a finite set of action schemas, and $\gamma$ is the deterministic state-transition function.

A **STRIPS action schema** has three components:
1. **Precondition list (Pre):** Literals that must be true for the action to fire. *Example:* `Pickup(x)` requires `ArmEmpty ∧ OnTable(x) ∧ Clear(x)`.
2. **Add list (Add):** Literals made true after execution. *Example:* `Pickup(x)` adds `Holding(x)`.
3. **Delete list (Del):** Literals made false after execution. *Example:* `Pickup(x)` deletes `ArmEmpty, OnTable(x), Clear(x)`.

STRIPS uses the Closed-World Assumption: any literal not asserted true is false. The frame problem is handled implicitly — only listed add/delete effects change.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the Closed-World Assumption (CWA) in propositional planning? Why is it important for STRIPS-style planners?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Closed-World Assumption (CWA)** is the convention that any proposition *not* explicitly asserted true in a state is assumed to be **false**. In STRIPS, $S_0$ lists only the *true* facts; everything else is implicitly $\bot$.

**Importance for STRIPS-style planners:**
1. **Determinism:** The successor state $\gamma(a, s)$ is *uniquely* determined by the action's Add/Del lists, enabling forward search.
2. **Compactness:** Only positive facts need storage — no explicit negation lists.
3. **Tractability:** Reduces the state space from $2^{2^{|P|}}$ (all logical theories over $P$) to $2^{|P|}$ (all truth assignments over $P$).
4. **Frame efficiency:** Implicit frame axioms rely on CWA — anything not deleted is preserved as true.

CWA fails in *open-world* or *incomplete-information* settings, which is why modern extensions (e.g., contingent planning, POMDPs) abandon it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks with sub-parts of 7+7 marks. Students choose between two alternative questions on the same module. Both are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the planning as satisfiability (SATPlan) approach. Encode the following Blocks World instance into a propositional CNF for a plan of length $T = 2$, listing the clauses for: initial state, goal, precondition axiom (for one action), effect axiom (for one action), and frame axiom (for one persistent fact). [**7 marks**]

**Initial state:** $S_0 = \{\text{On}(A, B),\ \text{OnTable}(B),\ \text{OnTable}(C),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{Clear}(C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

**Goal:** $S_G = \{\text{On}(A, C),\ \text{OnTable}(B),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

**Available action:** $\text{Stack}(A, C)$ with $\text{Pre} = \{\text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(C)\}$, $\text{Add} = \{\text{On}(A,C), \text{ArmEmpty}\}$, $\text{Del} = \{\text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(C)\}$

---

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**SATPlan Idea:** Encode "does a plan of length $T$ exist?" as a propositional formula. Use variables $F^t$ (fact $F$ at time $t$) and $a^t$ (action $a$ at time $t$). A SAT solver returns either a satisfying assignment (= the plan) or UNSAT.

**Step 1 — Initial state clauses (time 0):**
$$
\text{On}(A,B)^0 \wedge \text{OnTable}(B)^0 \wedge \text{OnTable}(C)^0 \wedge \text{Clear}(A)^0 \wedge \text{Clear}(C)^0 \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}^0
$$
and negations of all other facts at $t=0$ (e.g., $\neg\text{Holding}(A)^0$).

**Step 2 — Goal clauses (time $T=2$):**
$$
\text{On}(A,C)^2 \wedge \text{OnTable}(B)^2 \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Precondition axiom for $\text{Stack}(A,C)^1$:**
$$
\text{Stack}(A,C)^1 \rightarrow \text{Holding}(A)^1 \wedge \text{Clear}(C)^1
$$

**Step 4 — Effect axiom (positive) for $\text{Stack}(A,C)^1$:**
$$
\text{Stack}(A,C)^1 \rightarrow \text{On}(A,C)^2 \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}^2
$$

**Effect axiom (negative):**
$$
\text{Stack}(A,C)^1 \rightarrow \neg\text{Holding}(A)^2 \wedge \neg\text{Clear}(C)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Frame axiom for $\text{On}(A,B)$ persisting from $t=1$ to $t=2$:**
$$
\text{On}(A,B)^1 \wedge \text{On}(A,B)^2 \rightarrow \bigvee_{a : \text{On}(A,B) \in \text{Add}(a)} a^1
$$
(If the only action adding $\text{On}(A,B)$ is $\text{Stack}(A,B)$, this becomes $\text{On}(A,B)^1 \wedge \text{On}(A,B)^2 \rightarrow \text{Stack}(A,B)^1$.)

**Step 6 — Action exclusion at each step:**
$$
\forall a \neq b:\ \neg(a^t \wedge b^t)
$$

**Step 7 — Conjunction:** Combine all clauses into a single CNF and feed to a SAT solver.

> **Marker tip:** Full marks require *both* positive and negative effect axioms, and at least one frame axiom demonstrated explicitly. *(7 marks distributed: 1 for concept, 1 for initial, 1 for goal, 1 for precondition, 1 for effect, 1 for frame, 1 for action-exclusion+conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Describe the GraphPlan algorithm. What are mutex relations? List and explain the three types of mutex between actions. Show the first two layers of the planning graph for the following: [**7 marks**]

**Initial state:** $S_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{OnTable}(B),\ \text{OnTable}(C),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

**Goal:** $S_G = \{\text{On}(A,C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

---

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**GraphPlan Algorithm (Blum & Furst, 1997):** Build a layered planning graph alternating *fact layers* and *action layers*, annotate with mutex relations, and stop when (i) all goal facts appear in a fact layer non-mutex, OR (ii) the graph levels off (two consecutive fact layers identical).

**Mutex relations** are binary relations between actions or facts at the same layer, indicating they *cannot both* be true simultaneously in any valid plan. They prune the search dramatically.

**Three types of action mutex (all at the same action layer $i$):**

1. **Inconsistent Effects:** Two actions $a_1$ and $a_2$ have opposite effects on the same proposition. *Example:* $a_1$ adds $p$ and $a_2$ adds $\neg p$ (or vice versa). They are mutex because both cannot be true in the successor state.

2. **Interference:** Action $a_1$ deletes a precondition of $a_2$, or vice versa. *Example:* $a_1$ deletes $q$ but $a_2$ requires $q$ as a precondition. They cannot fire in the same state since $a_2$'s need would be violated.

3. **Competing Needs:** Actions $a_1$ and $a_2$ need preconditions that are themselves mutex at the previous fact layer $i-1$. *Example:* $a_1$ needs $p$, $a_2$ needs $q$, and $p \perp q$ at fact layer $i-1$. They cannot both fire simultaneously.

**First two layers for the given problem:**

**Fact Layer 0 (F0):** $\text{On}(A,B), \text{Clear}(A), \text{ArmEmpty}, \text{OnTable}(B), \text{OnTable}(C), \text{Clear}(C)$ — all true in $S_0$ (plus no-ops for each).

**Action Layer 1 (A1):** Actions with preconditions $\subseteq F0$.
- $\text{Unstack}(A, B)$: needs $\text{ArmEmpty}, \text{Clear}(A), \text{On}(A,B)$ — all in F0 ✓
- $\text{Pickup}(B)$: needs $\text{ArmEmpty}, \text{OnTable}(B), \text{Clear}(B)$ — $\text{Clear}(B) \notin F0$ ✗
- $\text{Pickup}(C)$: needs $\text{ArmEmpty}, \text{OnTable}(C), \text{Clear}(C)$ — all in F0 ✓
- $\text{Stack}, \text{Putdown}$: need $\text{Holding}(x)$ — not in F0 ✗
- No-ops: one for every fact in F0.

**Fact Layer 1 (F1):** $\text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B), \text{Holding}(C), \text{Clear}(\text{Table})$, plus all F0 facts preserved by no-ops.

**Mutex at A1:** $\text{Unstack}(A,B)$ and $\text{Pickup}(C)$ are mutex via *interference* (Unstack deletes $\text{ArmEmpty}$, Pickup needs it).

**Goal check at F1:** $\text{On}(A,C) \notin F1$ yet — must extend to F2.

> **Marker tip:** Drawing the layered graph with *all* mutex relations earns full marks; missing mutex annotations costs 2 marks. *(7 marks: 1 for GraphPlan concept, 2 for mutex definitions, 1 for mutex examples, 2 for graph construction, 1 for goal-check inference.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the **frame problem** in classical AI planning. Explain how STRIPS handles it *implicitly* and discuss its limitations. [**7 marks**]

---

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Frame Problem:** The frame problem is the problem of representing — in a tractable way — *which things do not change* when an action is performed. A naive first-order logic theory (e.g., Situation Calculus) requires a **frame axiom for every (proposition, action) pair** stating that if the action fires, the proposition is unchanged:

$$
\forall s.\, \text{At}(p, s) \land p \notin \text{Add}(a) \land p \notin \text{Del}(a) \land \text{Poss}(a, s) \rightarrow \text{At}(p, \text{Result}(a, s))
$$

For $|P| = 100$ propositions and $|A| = 50$ actions, this requires **5000 frame axioms** — an $O(|P| \times |A|)$ representational blowup.

**How STRIPS handles it implicitly:**
- STRIPS action schemas list **only** the propositions in the Add and Delete lists.
- The semantics are: "if $F$ is not in $\text{Del}(a)$, then $F$ retains its truth value after $a$ fires."
- This is an **implicit** representation — no explicit frame axioms are written. The number of "frame facts" per action is $O(1)$.
- Combined with the **Closed-World Assumption**, STRIPS makes the successor state $\gamma(a, s)$ uniquely computable in polynomial time.

**Limitations of the STRIPS solution:**
1. **Rigid semantics:** Cannot easily express *conditional* persistence (e.g., "if the door is open, the room stays lit"). Requires extra action decompositions.
2. **No concurrent actions:** STRIPS assumes one action at a time; simultaneous actions break the implicit frame.
3. **No exogenous events:** If the world changes due to *external* causes (not modeled as actions), STRIPS cannot reason about it.
4. **Incomplete information:** CWA fails when some propositions are *unknown*; STRIPS then becomes unsound.
5. **Ramification problem:** Indirect effects of actions (e.g., moving a table also moves the vase on it) are not represented. Requires *causal extension* languages.

> **Marker tip:** To earn full marks, students must (1) define frame problem precisely, (2) show the $O(|P| \times |A|)$ blowup, (3) explain the *implicit* mechanism of Add/Del lists, and (4) list at least 3 limitations. *(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for STRIPS mechanism, 2 for limitations, 1 for engineering conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** What is **partial-order planning (POP)**? Explain the concept of *flaws* (open preconditions and threats) and the algorithms used to resolve them. Compare POP with total-order (linear) planning. [**7 marks**]

---

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Partial-Order Planning (POP):** Also called *least-commitment planning*, POP is a planning strategy where the planner builds a plan as a *partially ordered* set of steps. The order between non-interfering actions is *deliberately left unspecified* until forced by causal dependencies. This contrasts with *total-order* (linear) planning, which commits to a strict left-to-right sequence early.

**Plan representation:**
- A **plan** is a 4-tuple $\langle \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{B} \rangle$:
  - $\mathcal{A}$: set of plan steps (actions + Start + Finish)
  - $\mathcal{O}$: set of ordering constraints ($a_i \prec a_j$)
  - $\mathcal{L}$: set of causal links ($a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$ meaning "$a_i$ achieves $p$ for $a_j$")
  - $\mathcal{B}$: set of binding constraints (variable equalities)

**Flaws and their resolution:**

A *flaw* is a feature of a partial plan that must be resolved before the plan is executable. Two main types:

1. **Open Precondition (Unachieved Goal):** A precondition $p$ of step $a_j$ is not yet *causally linked* to any earlier step that asserts $p$.
   - **Resolution:** Find an existing step $a_i$ (or add a new step) that adds $p$, then add the causal link $a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$ and the ordering $a_i \prec a_j$.

2. **Threat (Causal Conflict):** A step $a_k$ exists that could *clobber* (delete) a proposition $p$ that is protected by a causal link $a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$, and $a_k$ is not provably ordered with respect to $a_i$ and $a_j$.
   - **Resolution (three options):**
     - **Demotion:** Order $a_k \prec a_i$ (force $a_k$ to fire before $a_i$)
     - **Promotion:** Order $a_j \prec a_k$ (force $a_k$ to fire after $a_j$)
     - **Separation:** Add binding constraints so $a_k$ cannot match the variables in the link

**Algorithm:** POP is typically implemented as a *flaw-selection* loop using *least commitment*:
1. Initialize plan with Start and Finish; link Start's effects to Finish's preconditions.
2. Pick a flaw (open precondition or threat).
3. Resolve it (if multiple options, branch / use heuristics).
4. Repeat until no flaws remain → valid plan.
5. Linearize the partial order arbitrarily to produce an executable sequence.

**Comparison with Total-Order (Linear) Planning:**

| Aspect | Total-Order Planning | Partial-Order Planning |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Search space size** | Enormous (commits to orderings early) | Smaller (delays commitments) |
| **Branching factor** | High (action sequences) | Lower (flaw-resolution choices) |
| **Backtracking cost** | High (must undo orderings) | Low (only resolves local flaws) |
| **Plan flexibility** | Fixed sequence | Can be re-linearized for execution context |
| **Optimality** | Optimal if $A^*$ is used | Not always optimal (flaw order matters) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simple | Higher (causal-link bookkeeping) |
| **KTU relevance** | Medium | **High** (frequently tested) |

> **Marker tip:** A common mistake is to confuse *causal links* with *ordering constraints*. They are distinct: a causal link says *why* an ordering exists; an ordering is a structural constraint. *(7 marks: 1 for POP definition, 2 for flaw types, 2 for resolution strategies, 2 for comparison table.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic — the FIVE deadly sins:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Closed-World Assumption (CWA):** Always state explicitly that STRIPS assumes CWA. Examiners dock 1 mark per omission.
> 2. **Confusing Add and Delete lists with Preconditions:** A precondition is what must be *true before*; Add is what becomes *true after*; Delete is what becomes *false after*. Mixing them up is the most common error.
> 3. **Omitting frame axioms in SATPlan encodings:** Students frequently write effect axioms but forget the frame axioms (the "everything that doesn't change must be causally explained" clauses). The encoding is **incomplete** without them — a -2 mark penalty.
> 4. **Drawing planning graphs without mutex annotations:** A planning graph is *not* a planning graph without mutex relations. Always annotate.
> 5. **Mixing up POP flaw types:** *Open preconditions* are resolved by adding causal links; *threats* are resolved by demotion/promotion/separation. Confusing them is a -1 mark penalty per question.
> 6. **Hand-waving on action exclusion in SATPlan:** The axiom $\bigwedge_{a \neq b} \neg(a^t \wedge b^t)$ is *mandatory* — without it, multiple actions could fire simultaneously, which is invalid classical planning.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- [x] **STRIPS** = $\langle P, S_0, S_G, A, \gamma \rangle$; actions have **Pre, Add, Del**.
- [x] **CWA:** anything not in $S_0$ is **false**; makes the state space $2^{|P|}$.
- [x] **Implicit frame assumption** = STRIPS solves the frame problem in $O(1)$ per action.
- [x] **Situation Calculus** = explicit $O(|P| \times |A|)$ frame axioms; McCarthy 1963.
- [x] **Forward (progression) search** = $S_0 \to S_G$; suffers from irrelevant actions.
- [x] **Backward (regression) search** = $S_G \to S_0$; uses $\text{Regress}(g, a) = (g \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$.
- [x] **SATPlan** = encode plan-of-length-$T$ as CNF; **5 axiom types**: initial, goal, precondition, effect, frame + action-exclusion.
- [x] **SATPlan Theorem:** $\mathcal{P}$ has a plan of length $\le T$ **iff** $\text{SAT}(T)$ is satisfiable.
- [x] **GraphPlan** = layered fact/action graph with **mutex annotations**; terminates in $O(|P| \cdot |A|)$.
- [x] **Three action mutexes:** *Inconsistent Effects*, *Interference*, *Competing Needs*.
- [x] **Fact mutexes** = *Inconsistent Support* (all action pairs producing them are mutex).
- [x] **Partial-Order Planning** = least-commitment, builds plan via **flaw resolution** (open preconditions + threats).
- [x] **Threat resolution:** *Demote, Promote, or Separate* — never clobber a causal link.
- [x] **Ramification problem** = STRIPS does NOT represent indirect effects (e.g., moving table moves vase).
- [x] **Qualification problem** = STRIPS assumes all preconditions are *necessary and sufficient* (no "doors can be stuck").
- [x] **PDDL** (Planning Domain Definition Language, 1998) is the modern standard derived from STRIPS, adding typing, durative actions, and preferences.
- [x] **Industrial planners** (Fast Downward, LAMA) combine GraphPlan-style reachability with delete-relaxation heuristics.

> **🎓 Closing Note:** Planning with Propositional Logic is the *theoretical foundation* of all modern task planning in robotics. Mastering STRIPS encoding, SATPlan axiom construction, and GraphPlan mutex analysis will secure you full marks on Module 2 — these are the "Kerning Questions" of KTU AI-Robotics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Acting in Real World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning and Acting in Real World

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Planning and Acting in the Real World** refers to the branch of Automated Planning in Artificial Intelligence (AI) that addresses the complexities, uncertainties, and constraints encountered when a rational agent (typically an autonomous robot) must transition from a deliberative *plan* (a sequence of actions) to physical *execution* in an environment that is **incomplete, nondeterministic, dynamic, and resource-bounded**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> In classical planning, we assume a fully observable, deterministic, static, and discrete environment. *Real-world planning* relaxes these assumptions to handle time, resources, hierarchical tasks, sensor uncertainty, and stochastic outcomes — bridging the **sense → plan → act** loop that every autonomous robot must perform.

The core formal statement can be expressed as:

$$\Pi = \langle S, S_0, A, T, G, C, R, \tau \rangle$$

Where:
- $S$ = Set of world states
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = Initial states
- $A$ = Set of actions
- $T(s, a, s')$ = Transition model (often stochastic: $P(s' \mid s,a)$)
- $G$ = Goal states
- $C(s, a, s')$ = Action cost function
- $R$ = Set of bounded resources (time, energy, fuel)
- $\tau$ = Temporal/duration constraints

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Delivery Robot in a Busy Office

Imagine a **food-delivery robot** in a multi-floor office building. Before it acts, the planner must answer:

1. *Which elevator to take?* (Resource management — elevators are shared)
2. *What if a hallway is blocked?* (Contingency / conditional planning)
3. *Can I make a delivery within 20 minutes?* (Time-constrained scheduling)
4. *Should I replan if the customer's desk is empty?* (Execution monitoring)

This robot cannot rely on a single precomputed plan. It must **plan, act, observe, and replan** continuously — exactly what *Planning and Acting in the Real World* is about.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** The classical planner gives you a *recipe*. Real-world planning is the **chef** who adjusts the recipe when the oven breaks, the timer expires, or ingredients run out.

## 1.3 Why Classical Planning Fails in the Real World

| Assumption (Classical) | Real-World Violation | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Fully observable | Sensors are noisy, occluded | Partial information planning |
| Deterministic | Motors slip, objects shift | Stochastic / nondeterministic |
| Static | Humans, other robots move | Dynamic / adversarial |
| Discrete states | Continuous time, speed, angle | Time-constrained planning |
| Unlimited resources | Battery, fuel, memory | Resource-bounded planning |
| Single agent | Multiple robots/people | Multi-agent coordination |

## 1.4 The Sense–Plan–Act Loop

The real-world agent continuously cycles through:

$$\text{Sense (Observe)} \longrightarrow \text{Plan (Deliberate)} \longrightarrow \text{Act (Execute)} \longrightarrow \text{Monitor (Re-observe)}$$

This is the **executive architecture** that all real robotic AI must implement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Real-World Planning Extensions

### A. Time, Schedules, and Resources (Temporal Planning)
Classical STRIPS ignores *when* an action runs. Real-world planners use **temporal logic** where each action has:
- **Duration** $\Delta(a)$
- **Start time** $s(a)$ and **End time** $e(a) = s(a) + \Delta(a)$
- **Resource consumption** $r_i(a)$ for each resource $i$

**Key constraint** (Simple Temporal Network — STP):
$$s(a_j) - s(a_i) \geq \Delta(a_i) + c_{ij}$$
for any precedence constraint $a_i \prec a_j$, where $c_{ij}$ is the minimum lag.

### B. Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) Planning
Instead of primitive actions, the agent reasons about **tasks** at multiple abstraction levels.

- **Primitive tasks** = executable actions
- **Non-primitive (compound) tasks** = decomposed via *methods* into subtasks
- HTN planners search for a **decomposition tree** that refines a high-level task into a valid executable plan.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** HTN is like a project manager decomposing "Launch Product" → "Design" → "Code" → "Test" → "Deploy" — each level gets more specific until reaching executable units.

A *method* is a 4-tuple:
$$m = \langle \text{CompoundTask}, \text{Preconditions}, \text{Subtasks}, \text{Constraints} \rangle$$

### C. Planning with Nondeterminism
The world is modelled as a **conditional outcome**:
$$\text{Outcome}(a) = \{(s_1, p_1), (s_2, p_2), \ldots, (s_k, p_k)\}$$
with $\sum_{i} p_i = 1$.

The agent must compute a **contingent plan** (conditional plan) — a tree where each branch corresponds to an observable outcome.

### D. Conditional Planning (Contingency Planning)
Plans are represented as decision trees:
$$\text{Conditional-Plan}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{action } a & \text{if } s \models \text{condition} \\ \text{plan}_2 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### E. Execution Monitoring and Replanning
A *monitor* checks the world state $s_{\text{actual}}$ against the *expected* state $s_{\text{predicted}}$ at every step:

- **Action monitoring:** Verify the action's preconditions still hold.
- **Plan monitoring:** Check if the *rest of the plan* is still valid.
- **Goal monitoring:** Detect if the goal is already achieved (or impossible).

If discrepancy detected → **replan** from current state.

### F. Continuous Planning
There is no clear "plan-then-execute" boundary. The agent **always has a plan**, always executes the first unfinished action, and **re-plans opportunistically** whenever new information arrives or conditions change. This is the architecture used in NASA's **Remote Agent** and modern autonomous systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Notation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temporal STP constraint | $s(a_j) - s(a_i) \geq \Delta(a_i) + c_{ij}$ | Precedence with delay | seconds (s) |
| Resource cumulative bound | $\sum_{a \in \text{active}} r_i(a) \leq R_i^{\max}$ | Resource capacity | Wh, ml, kg |
| Stochastic transition | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Probability of outcome | dimensionless [0,1] |
| Expected cost (MDP-style) | $E[C] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \cdot C(s,a,s')$ | Mean cost per action | cost units |
| HTN decomposition | $t \xrightarrow{m} \langle t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k \rangle$ | Method application | — |
| Conditional plan node | $\pi(s) = a \text{ if } \phi(s)$ | Branching action | — |
| Replan trigger | $\|s_{\text{actual}} - s_{\text{predicted}}\| > \epsilon$ | Threshold violation | state-metric |
| Total makespan | $T_{\text{makespan}} = \max_a e(a)$ | Plan finish time | s |
| Slack time | $\text{slack} = T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{makespan}}$ | Buffer available | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse:**
> - **Conditional planning** = offline, plans for all contingencies in advance.
> - **Replanning** = online, reacts to failures during execution.
> - **Continuous planning** = always-on, no plan/execute boundary.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use temporal + continuous planning for routing under dynamic traffic.
- **Mars rovers (NASA):** Use HTN + execution monitoring to schedule daily activities with limited power and bandwidth.
- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva):** Use multi-agent planning for path coordination.
- **Drone swarms:** Combine conditional planning with stochastic models for search-and-rescue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Expected Cost of a Conditional Plan

Let us rigorously derive how an agent evaluates a *stochastic* plan in a nondeterministic domain.

### Setup
A robot must move from room $A$ to room $C$ via door $D$. Action $\text{Move}(A,C)$ has two outcomes:
- $0.7$ probability: arrives in $C$ (success)
- $0.3$ probability: stays in $A$ (failure — door jammed)

Each attempt costs $C_{\text{try}} = 1$ energy unit. The robot can retry up to $n$ times.

### Step 1 — Model the cost of one attempt
$$C_1 = C_{\text{try}} + 0.7 \cdot C_{\text{success}} + 0.3 \cdot C_{\text{failure}}$$

### Step 2 — Model success and failure recursively
- $C_{\text{success}} = 0$ (goal reached, no further cost)
- $C_{\text{failure}} = 1 \cdot C_1$ (must try again)

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= C_{\text{try}} + 0.7 \cdot 0 + 0.3 \cdot (1 \cdot C_1) \\
C_1 &= 1 + 0 + 0.3 \cdot C_1 \\
C_1 - 0.3 \cdot C_1 &= 1 \\
0.7 \cdot C_1 &= 1 \\
C_1 &= \frac{1}{0.7} = \frac{10}{7} \approx 1.4286
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ retries
After $n$ attempts, the cumulative success probability is:
$$P_{\text{success}}(n) = 1 - (1 - 0.7)^n = 1 - 0.3^n$$

The expected number of attempts is the geometric mean:
$$E[N] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.7} = \frac{10}{7}$$

### Step 4 — Total expected cost
$$E[\text{Cost}_{\text{total}}] = E[N] \cdot C_{\text{try}} = \frac{10}{7} \cdot 1 = \frac{10}{7} \approx 1.43 \text{ energy units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A stochastic plan has a cost *higher* than the deterministic equivalent because the agent must budget for retries. KTU examiners love asking students to compute $E[N]$ for geometric retry scenarios.

## 3.2 Derivation: Simple Temporal Network (STP) Consistency Check

Given three activities $a, b, c$ with constraints:
- $b$ starts after $a$ ends: $s(b) \geq e(a)$
- $c$ starts after $b$ ends: $s(c) \geq e(b)$
- Hard deadline: $e(c) \leq T_{\max}$
- Durations: $\Delta(a) = 5$, $\Delta(b) = 3$, $\Delta(c) = 4$ seconds

### Step 1 — Express each constraint
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C1:} \quad & s(b) - s(a) \geq 5 \\
\text{C2:} \quad & s(c) - s(b) \geq 3 \\
\text{C3:} \quad & s(c) - s(a) \leq 12 \quad (\text{since } T_{\max} = 12)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Build the distance graph $G = (V, E)$ with weight
- $V = \{a, b, c\}$
- Edge $a \to b$ with weight $-5$ (from C1, reversed)
- Edge $b \to c$ with weight $-3$ (from C2, reversed)
- Edge $a \to c$ with weight $12$ (from C3)

### Step 3 — Run Bellman–Ford / Floyd–Warshall to detect negative cycles
The transitive path $a \to b \to c$ gives weight $-5 + -3 = -8$.
The direct constraint says $s(c) - s(a) \leq 12$, i.e., edge $a \to c$ has weight $12$.

The condition for consistency is that the **shortest path** $\leq$ **longest path** for every pair:
$$\text{shortest}(a,c) \leq \text{longest}(a,c)$$
$$-8 \leq 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ Consistent}$$

### Step 4 — Compute earliest/latest start times
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{earliest}}(a) &= 0 \\
s_{\text{earliest}}(b) &= 0 + 5 = 5 \\
s_{\text{earliest}}(c) &= 5 + 3 = 8 \\
\text{Slack} &= T_{\max} - e(c) = 12 - 12 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students treat $s(c) - s(a) \leq 12$ as $-12$ in the distance graph. Remember: shortest-path problem requires the **upper-bound** edge weight to be **positive** in the transformed graph (reversed inequalities).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | When to Use | Key Strength | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classical STRIPS | Toy / fully known world | Provably optimal | Brittle in reality |
| Temporal Planning (STP) | Hard deadlines, scheduling | Respects real time | Needs accurate durations |
| HTN Planning | Domain has natural hierarchy | Human-readable, expert-friendly | Requires hand-crafted methods |
| Conditional Planning | Known sensor uncertainty | Plan for contingencies upfront | Plan tree explosion |
| Replanning | Surprise failures | Reacts online | May oscillate between plans |
| Continuous Planning | Dynamic world with streaming info | Always valid current plan | Computationally intense |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sense–Plan–Act–Monitor Loop (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Sensor Input\nRaw Observations] --> nodeP[Perception Module\nFeature Extraction]
    nodeP --> nodeW[World State Estimator\nBelief State Update]
    nodeW --> nodeM{Monitor\nDiscrepancy greater than epsilon?}
    nodeM -- No --> nodeA[Execute Next Action\nFrom Current Plan]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Effectors\nMotors / Wheels / Grippers]
    nodeE --> nodeENV[Physical World]
    nodeENV --> nodeS
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeRP[Replanner\nGenerate New Plan]
    nodeRP --> nodeK[Knowledge Base\nGoals and Constraints]
    nodeK --> nodeRP
    nodeRP --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 HTN Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Compound Task\nDeliver Package to Room 305]
    nodeM1[Method 1\nUse Elevator Route]
    nodeM2[Method 2\nUse Staircase Route]
    nodeRoot --> nodeM1
    nodeRoot --> nodeM2
    nodeM1 --> nodeS1[Primitive\nGo to Elevator Lobby]
    nodeM1 --> nodeS2[Primitive\nCall Elevator]
    nodeM1 --> nodeS3[Primitive\nEnter Elevator]
    nodeM1 --> nodeS4[Primitive\nSelect Floor 3]
    nodeM1 --> nodeS5[Primitive\nNavigate to Room 305]
    nodeM2 --> nodeT1[Primitive\nGo to Staircase A]
    nodeM2 --> nodeT2[Primitive\nClimb 3 Flights]
    nodeM2 --> nodeT3[Primitive\nNavigate to Room 305]
```

## 4.3 Conditional Plan as Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[State s0\nPickup Box]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA1[Action\nOpen Gripper]
    nodeA1 --> nodeC1{Object\nGrasped?}
    nodeC1 -- Yes --> nodeA2[Action\nLift and Move]
    nodeA2 --> nodeG[Goal Achieved]
    nodeC1 -- No --> nodeA3[Action\nReposition Gripper]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA1
```

## 4.4 Temporal Plan as Gantt-like Schedule

```mermaid
gantt
    title Robot Schedule with Temporal Constraints
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Sensing
    Scan Environment    :a1, 0, 3
    section Planning
    Generate Path       :a2, after a1, 2
    section Acting
    Move to Pickup      :a3, after a2, 5
    Pickup Object       :a4, after a3, 2
    Move to Dropoff     :a5, after a4, 6
    Release Object      :a6, after a5, 1
```

## 4.5 Continuous vs. Classical Planning Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Classical[Classical Planning]
        nodeC1[Plan Complete Plan] --> nodeC2[Execute Whole Plan]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Stop]
    end
    subgraph Continuous[Continuous Planning]
        nodeP1[Have Current Plan] --> nodeP2[Execute First Action]
        nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Observe World]
        nodeP3 --> nodeP4{Need to Replan?}
        nodeP4 -- Yes --> nodeP5[Generate New Plan]
        nodeP5 --> nodeP1
        nodeP4 -- No --> nodeP1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between classical planning and planning in the real world. List any two real-world assumptions violated by classical planners.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Classical planning assumes a **fully observable, deterministic, static, finite, and discrete** environment with a **single agent** and **unlimited resources**. Real-world planning relaxes these:
1. **Partial observability** — sensors are noisy and limited in range.
2. **Nondeterminism** — actions can have stochastic outcomes (e.g., a gripper slip).
*(Any two valid violations get 1.5 marks each.)*
The key difference is that real-world planning incorporates **time, resources, uncertainty, and replanning**, which classical STRIPS does not handle. *(0.5 mark for the summary.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a Hierarchical Task Network (HTN)? How does a *method* differ from a *primitive task*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **HTN** is a planning approach where high-level compound tasks are recursively decomposed into subtasks using *methods* until only executable primitive actions remain. *(1 mark)*
- A **primitive task** is directly executable by the agent (e.g., "Move to room B"). *(1 mark)*
- A **method** is a *decomposition rule* specifying how a compound task is reduced to subtasks under given preconditions (e.g., "Deliver to floor 3 → use elevator OR staircase"). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *conditional planning* with *replanning* — the former plans offline for all contingencies, the latter triggers online after failure.
> 2. In temporal constraints, remember the **direction** of the inequality: $s(b) - s(a) \geq \Delta$ is a *lower bound*, not an upper bound.
> 3. HTN plans are **decomposed**, not searched like STRIPS — wrong method-name usage costs 2 marks.
> 4. Stochastic expected-cost derivations: students often forget to **subtract** $p \cdot C_1$ from both sides, getting the geometric sum wrong.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a real-world planning agent. Discuss the role of the execution monitor and replanner. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A robot attempts to grasp an object. The action "Grasp" succeeds with probability 0.8 and fails (object drops) with probability 0.2. Each attempt costs 2 energy units. Compute the expected energy required to succeed at least once. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The real-world planning agent is built on a **sense–plan–act** loop with continuous monitoring:

1. **Perception Module (1 mark):** Processes raw sensor data (LiDAR, cameras, IMU) into a symbolic belief state $B$.
2. **Knowledge Base (1 mark):** Stores domain rules, world model, action schema, goals $G$, and constraints.
3. **Planner (1.5 marks):** Generates a plan $\pi$ using search/HTN/MDP techniques.
4. **Execution Monitor (1.5 marks):** At every step, compares $s_{\text{predicted}}$ with $s_{\text{observed}}$. Triggers replanning if discrepancy $\geq \epsilon$. Performs action, plan, and goal monitoring.
5. **Replanner (1 mark):** Generates a new plan from the current belief state, reusing prior plan where possible.
6. **Effectors (1 mark):** Execute the chosen primitive action in the physical world.

**Role of monitor:** Detects failures early, prevents cascading errors, and ensures the robot stays on-task.

**Role of replanner:** Maintains plan validity, adapts to new obstacles, and recovers from dead-ends.

*(Reference diagram: see SECTION 4.1.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Model the geometric retry scenario.
Let $p = 0.8$, $q = 0.2$, $C_{\text{try}} = 2$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write recursive cost equation.
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= C_{\text{try}} + q \cdot C_1 \\
C_1 &= 2 + 0.2 \cdot C_1 \\
0.8 \cdot C_1 &= 2 \\
C_1 &= \frac{2}{0.8} = 2.5 \text{ energy units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Verify with geometric series.
Expected number of attempts $E[N] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.8} = 1.25$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute total expected energy.
$$
E[\text{Energy}] = E[N] \cdot C_{\text{try}} = 1.25 \times 2 = 2.5 \text{ energy units}
$$

**Answer:** The robot needs on average **2.5 energy units** to succeed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper — Alternative]

**(a)** *Explain Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) planning in detail. How does it differ from STRIPS-style planning? Show a small example of a method decomposition. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Consider a robot with three tasks: $T_1$ (duration 4 s), $T_2$ (duration 3 s), $T_3$ (duration 2 s). Constraints: $T_2$ must start after $T_1$ ends; $T_3$ must start after $T_2$ ends; the entire plan must finish within 10 s. Using a Simple Temporal Network (STP), determine if the schedule is feasible and compute the slack. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**HTN vs. STRIPS (3 marks):**
- STRIPS searches for a sequence of primitive actions achieving a goal state.
- HTN searches for a **decomposition tree** of a given high-level task using *methods*.

**HTN Components (2 marks):**
- **Compound task** — abstract, not directly executable.
- **Primitive task** — directly executable.
- **Method** — decomposition rule: preconditions + subtask ordering.
- **Plan** — fully decomposed tree of primitive tasks.

**Example (2 marks):**
Task: `CleanRoom`
- Method 1: `CleanRoom → SweepFloor ∧ DustFurniture`
  - Precondition: `hasBroom ∧ hasDuster`
- Method 2: `CleanRoom → UseVacuumRobot`
  - Precondition: `vacuumAvailable`

If precondition `hasBroom = true` and `vacuumAvailable = false`, the planner picks Method 1 and decomposes further:
`SweepFloor → MoveToCenter ∧ ExecuteSweepMotion ∧ ReturnToDock`
`DustFurniture → PickDuster ∧ WipeSurfaces ∧ PlaceDuster`

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the constraints.
$$
\begin{aligned}
s(T_2) - s(T_1) &\geq 4 \\
s(T_3) - s(T_2) &\geq 3 \\
s(T_3) + 2 &\leq 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s(T_3) \leq 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Build distance graph with edges (negated lower bounds).
- $T_1 \to T_2$: weight $-4$
- $T_2 \to T_3$: weight $-3$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute earliest times.
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{earliest}}(T_1) &= 0,\; e(T_1) = 4 \\
s_{\text{earliest}}(T_2) &= 4,\; e(T_2) = 7 \\
s_{\text{earliest}}(T_3) &= 7,\; e(T_3) = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Check deadline.
$e(T_3) = 9 \leq 10$ ✓ **Feasible.**

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute slack.
$$
\text{slack} = T_{\max} - e(T_3) = 10 - 9 = 1 \text{ second}
$$

**Answer:** The schedule is **feasible** with **1 second of slack**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- ☐ Classical STRIPS assumes **fully observable, deterministic, static, finite** worlds.
- ☐ Real-world planning adds **time, resources, uncertainty, hierarchy, multiple agents**.
- ☐ A **plan** is a sequence/tree of actions; a **schedule** is a plan with assigned start times.
- ☐ **HTN** = compound + primitive tasks + methods; search is over decompositions, not goal states.
- ☐ **Conditional plan** = offline branching; **replan** = online reaction; **continuous plan** = always-on loop.
- ☐ Temporal constraints use **STPs** with inequalities of the form $s_j - s_i \geq \Delta_{ij}$.
- ☐ **Bellman–Ford** on the distance graph detects infeasibility (negative cycle).
- ☐ **Expected cost** of a stochastic retry follows a geometric sum: $E[C] = C_{\text{try}} / p$.
- ☐ **Execution monitor** checks (i) action preconditions, (ii) plan validity, (iii) goal status.
- ☐ **Resources** are *consumable* (battery) or *reusable* (CPU); always sum across *active* actions.
- ☐ **Continuous planning** (NASA Remote Agent) eliminates the plan/act boundary.
- ☐ Common KTU keywords: *contingent plan, partial-information planning, deadline, makespan, slack, method decomposition, belief state, monitor, replan trigger.*
- ☐ Always draw the **sense-plan-act** loop for 14-mark architecture questions — diagrams score 2–3 free marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uncertainty, Probabilistic Reasoning, and Dynamic Bayesian Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Uncertainty, Probabilistic Reasoning, and Dynamic Bayesian Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Uncertainty* in AI refers to the situation in which an intelligent agent cannot definitively determine the true state of the world, the outcome of an action, or the value of a variable due to incomplete knowledge, noisy sensors, stochastic environments, or adversarial factors. It is mathematically quantified using probability theory, where beliefs about propositions are expressed as real numbers in the closed interval **[0, 1]**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Foggy Self-Driving Car

Imagine a self-driving robot navigating through a foggy Kerala morning. The road is clear, but the camera sees only **blurry shapes**. Is that a pedestrian or a lamp post? Should it brake or continue? The robot cannot *know for certain*, so it must **reason about degrees of belief**.

> *"There is no certainty, only probability."*
> — A foundational principle in modern probabilistic AI.

This is exactly the kind of problem robots face every microsecond: sensors are noisy, the world is partially observable, and actuators are imperfect. Probability gives the robot a *calibrated* way to make rational decisions under this fog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Probability and not Logic?**
> Classical (Boolean) logic answers: **TRUE** or **FALSE** (1 or 0). Real robots rarely have such clean answers. Probability theory provides a **continuous, mathematically rigorous framework** for representing graded beliefs, combining evidence, and updating knowledge as new sensor data arrives.

## 1.2 Probabilistic Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Probabilistic Reasoning* is the branch of AI that uses the laws of probability (especially **Bayes' theorem**) to represent and manipulate uncertain knowledge, perform inference over a set of random variables, and update beliefs given new evidence. The agent's knowledge is encoded as a **joint probability distribution (JPD)** over relevant variables, from which all other queries are derived.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Diagnosis Robot

Think of a robot doctor. A patient says, *"I have a fever."* The robot thinks:
- Fever is *caused* by many diseases (flu, dengue, COVID).
- Some diseases are *more prevalent* right now (prior probability).
- A blood test *updates* the diagnosis (likelihood).

Probabilistic reasoning is the mathematical machinery that lets the robot **combine prior medical knowledge with new test results** to compute a calibrated diagnosis. This is **Bayesian updating**.

## 1.3 Dynamic Bayesian Networks (DBNs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN)* is a Bayesian network that represents a **temporal stochastic process** — a sequence of random variables indexed by time. It compactly encodes the **transition model** $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ and the **sensor model** $P(E_t \mid X_t)$ by assuming that the process is **Markovian** (future depends only on the present, not the past) and **stationary** (the model parameters do not change over time).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Robot's Memory Tape

Imagine a robot's brain as a **tape recording**. At every time step $t$, a *hidden* state $X_t$ (e.g., the robot's true position) generates *observations* $E_t$ (e.g., noisy GPS readings). The next state $X_{t+1}$ depends only on $X_t$ (Markov property) and on the robot's control action $U_t$. A DBN is the **graphical blueprint** of this tape — showing how the world evolves and how sensors report it.

## 1.4 Key Probabilistic Vocabulary

| Term | Mathematical Notation | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Random Variable | $X$ | A variable whose value is uncertain (e.g., `Position`) |
| Probability | $P(X=x)$ | Degree of belief that $X$ equals $x$ |
| Joint Distribution | $P(X, Y)$ | Probability over two variables simultaneously |
| Conditional Probability | $P(X \mid Y)$ | Probability of $X$ **given** that $Y$ is true |
| Prior | $P(X)$ | Belief **before** seeing evidence |
| Posterior | $P(X \mid E)$ | Belief **after** seeing evidence $E$ |
| Likelihood | $P(E \mid X)$ | How probable the evidence is **if** $X$ is true |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Axioms of Probability (Kolmogorov, 1933):**
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $0 \le P(X) \le 1$
> 2. **Normalization:** $P(\text{True}) = 1$
> 3. **Additivity:** $P(A \lor B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \land B)$

These three rules are the **only** mathematical rules needed to derive every formula in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundation — Bayes' Theorem

Bayes' theorem is the single most important equation in probabilistic AI. It allows an agent to **invert** a causal, generative model $P(\text{cause} \to \text{effect})$ into a diagnostic reasoning model $P(\text{effect} \to \text{cause})$.

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

| Symbol | Name | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $P(H \mid E)$ | **Posterior** | Belief in hypothesis $H$ after seeing evidence $E$ |
| $P(E \mid H)$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of observing $E$ if $H$ is true |
| $P(H)$ | **Prior** | Initial belief in $H$ before evidence |
| $P(E)$ | **Marginal** (Evidence) | Total probability of $E$ across all hypotheses |

### Why It Works — The Logic Behind the Formula

1. **Joint probability** can be written two ways:
   - $P(H, E) = P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E)$
   - $P(H, E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$

2. Equating the two right-hand sides and solving for $P(H \mid E)$ yields Bayes' theorem.

3. The denominator $P(E)$ is just a **normalizing constant** that ensures the posterior sums to 1. It is computed via the **law of total probability**:
$$P(E) = \sum_{i} P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)$$

### Real-World Utility in Robotics

- **Robot Localization:** $P(\text{position} \mid \text{sensor reading})$
- **Object Recognition:** $P(\text{object class} \mid \text{image features})$
- **Fault Diagnosis:** $P(\text{motor failure} \mid \text{vibration data})$
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Entire system is Bayesian
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical decision support

## 2.2 Bayesian Networks (Static)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *Bayesian Network* (BN) is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** in which each node represents a random variable, and each directed edge $X \to Y$ encodes a conditional dependence (a direct probabilistic influence). Each node has a **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** $P(X \mid \text{Parents}(X))$.

### Core Structural Properties

- **Chain Rule Factorization:** The full joint distribution over $n$ variables is factored as a product of local CPTs:
$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

- **Conditional Independence:** A node is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. This is the **local Markov property**.

- **D-Separation:** A graphical test for determining whether two sets of nodes $X$ and $Y$ are independent given evidence $Z$.

### Three Canonical Connection Patterns

| Pattern | Diagram | Independence Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Causal Chain** | $A \to B \to C$ | $A \perp C \mid B$ (observing $B$ blocks the path) |
| **Common Cause** | $A \leftarrow B \to C$ | $A \perp C \mid B$ (observing $B$ blocks the path) |
| **Common Effect (V-structure)** | $A \to C \leftarrow B$ | $A \not\perp C$ in general, but $A \perp C \mid C$ (observing $C$ **opens** the path) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Explaining Away" Effect:** In a V-structure ($A \to C \leftarrow B$), if you observe that $C$ occurred, then learning $A$ changes your belief about $B$, even though $A$ and $B$ are a priori independent. This counterintuitive phenomenon is critical in diagnostic reasoning.

## 2.3 Dynamic Bayesian Networks (Temporal Extension)

A DBN extends a static BN by **unrolling** it across time. It has two key assumptions:

1. **First-Order Markov Assumption:**
$$P(X_t \mid X_{0:t-1}) = P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$$
*The future is independent of the past given the present.*

2. **Sensor Markov Assumption:**
$$P(E_t \mid X_{0:t}, E_{0:t-1}) = P(E_t \mid X_t)$$
*Sensor readings depend only on the current state.*

3. **Stationarity:** $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ and $P(E_t \mid X_t)$ are the same for all $t$.

### DBN Topology (Two-Slice Bayesian Network, 2TBN)

A DBN is fully specified by:
- **Prior:** $P(X_0)$ at time $t=0$
- **2TBN:** $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ and $P(E_t \mid X_t)$ linking two adjacent time slices

When you **unroll** this over $T$ time steps, the joint distribution is:
$$P(X_{0:T}, E_{0:T}) = P(X_0) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ P(X_t \mid X_{t-1}) \cdot P(E_t \mid X_t) \Big]$$

## 2.4 The Three Canonical Inference Tasks in DBNs

Given observations $e_{1:t}$ up to time $t$, an agent must compute beliefs about the hidden state $X_t$.

| Task | Formula | Real-World Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Filtering** (Online) | $P(X_t \mid e_{1:t})$ | *"Where is the robot **now**?"* |
| **Prediction** | $P(X_{t+k} \mid e_{1:t}), \, k > 0$ | *"Where will the robot be in 5 seconds?"* |
| **Smoothing** (Offline) | $P(X_k \mid e_{1:t}), \, 0 \le k < t$ | *"Where **was** the robot at time $k$?"* |
| **Most Likely Explanation (MLE)** | $\arg\max_{x_{1:t}} P(x_{1:t} \mid e_{1:t})$ | *"What **trajectory** best explains the sensor data?"* — solved by the Viterbi algorithm |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU question on this module will use one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Diagnostic reasoning |
| 2 | Total Probability | $P(E) = \sum_i P(E \mid H_i) P(H_i)$ | Normalizing constant |
| 3 | Chain Rule | $P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \prod_i P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ | Joint distribution from BN |
| 4 | DBN Joint | $P(X_{0:T}, E_{0:T}) = P(X_0) \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(X_t \mid X_{t-1}) P(E_t \mid X_t)$ | Unrolled DBN |
| 5 | Markov Assumption | $P(X_t \mid X_{0:t-1}) = P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ | Temporal simplification |
| 6 | Filtering Recursion | $P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t+1}) = \alpha \cdot P(e_{t+1} \mid X_{t+1}) \sum_{x_t} P(X_{t+1} \mid x_t) P(x_t \mid e_{1:t})$ | Recursive online inference |
| 7 | Kalman Filter Update | $\mu_{t+1} = A \mu_t + B u_t$ | Linear-Gaussian prediction |
| 8 | Kalman Measurement | $K_t = \Sigma_t C^T (C \Sigma_t C^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 9 | HMM Forward | $\alpha_t(j) = P(e_t \mid j) \sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}$ | Discrete-state DBN |
| 10 | Viterbi Recursion | $V_{t}(j) = \max_i \big[ V_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \big] \cdot P(e_t \mid j)$ | Most-likely path |

**Notation Key:** $\alpha$ = normalizing constant, $A$ = state transition matrix, $B$ = control matrix, $C$ = observation matrix, $R$ = sensor noise covariance, $\Sigma$ = state covariance, $K$ = Kalman gain, $a_{ij}$ = transition probability from state $i$ to $j$, $\alpha_t(j)$ = forward message at time $t$ in state $j$, $V_t(j)$ = max log-probability of any path ending in state $j$ at time $t$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from the Product Rule

**Starting Point — The Product Rule (Conjunction Symmetry):**
For any two propositions $A$ and $B$:
$$P(A \land B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$
$$P(A \land B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 1 — Equate the two expressions** (since the LHS is the same):
$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the posterior** $P(A \mid B)$:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $A$ with hypothesis $H$ and $B$ with evidence $E$:**
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Handle the denominator** using the law of total probability over mutually exclusive hypotheses $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n$:
$$P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **only derivation** of Bayes' theorem. KTU examiners love asking students to re-derive it from the product rule, so memorize these four steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the BN Chain Rule Factorization

**Goal:** Show that a Bayesian network over variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ ordered according to the topological order of the DAG factorizes as a product of local CPTs.

**Step 1 — Begin with the general chain rule of probability** (a tautology, valid for any ordering):
$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3 \mid X_1, X_2) \cdots P(X_n \mid X_1, \dots, X_{n-1})$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the shorthand $X_{1:i-1}$ for all predecessors of $X_i$:**
$$P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_{1:i-1})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Bayesian network's local Markov property** — each node is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. So the conditioning set $X_{1:i-1}$ can be **reduced** to just $\text{Parents}(X_i)$:
$$P(X_i \mid X_{1:i-1}) = P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** to obtain the BN factorization:
$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i)) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This factorization is the entire point of a Bayesian network.** Instead of storing $2^n - 1$ probabilities for $n$ binary variables, you store only the CPTs, which are exponentially smaller for sparse graphs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Filtering Recursion

**Goal:** Derive a recursive formula for $P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t+1})$ in terms of $P(X_t \mid e_{1:t})$.

**Step 1 — Condition on the previous hidden state** $X_t$:
$$P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t+1}) = P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t}, e_{t+1})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sensor Markov assumption** ($e_{t+1}$ depends only on $X_{t+1}$):
$$P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t}, e_{t+1}) = P(e_{t+1} \mid X_{t+1}, e_{1:t}) \cdot P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t})$$

Since $e_{t+1}$ depends only on $X_{t+1}$:
$$= P(e_{t+1} \mid X_{t+1}) \cdot P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t})$$

**Step 3 — Marginalize over the unknown previous state** $X_t$:
$$P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t}) = \sum_{x_t} P(X_{t+1} \mid x_t, e_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid e_{1:t})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Markov assumption** for the transition ($X_{t+1}$ depends only on $X_t$, not on $e_{1:t}$):
$$P(X_{t+1} \mid x_t, e_{1:t}) = P(X_{t+1} \mid x_t)$$

**Step 5 — Combine everything and add a normalizing constant $\alpha$:**
$$P(X_{t+1} \mid e_{1:t+1}) = \alpha \cdot P(e_{t+1} \mid X_{t+1}) \cdot \sum_{x_t} P(X_{t+1} \mid x_t) \cdot P(x_t \mid e_{1:t}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this equation aloud:** "To predict the new state from the old one, first **propagate** the old belief through the transition model, then **weight** it by how well the new evidence matches each possible state, then **normalize**."

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Robot Localization with Bayes' Filter

**Problem Setup (A KTU-style scenario):**
A warehouse robot moves on a 1D circular track with **3 positions** (1, 2, 3). At each step it either stays put or moves clockwise with equal probability.
- **Prior:** $P(X_0 = 1) = P(X_0 = 2) = P(X_0 = 3) = 1/3$
- **Sensor:** When the robot is at position $i$, it reports $i$ with probability **0.8** and one of the other two positions with probability **0.1** each.

**Step 1 — Initial Belief Vector:**
$$bel_0 = \langle 1/3, \; 1/3, \; 1/3 \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Robot moves (Transition Step).** Transition matrix $T$ (row = from, column = to):
$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\overline{bel}_1 = bel_0 \cdot T = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \Big\langle \tfrac{1}{3}(0.5) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.0) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.5), \;\tfrac{1}{3}(0.5) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.5) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.0), \;\tfrac{1}{3}(0.0) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.5) + \tfrac{1}{3}(0.5) \Big\rangle$$

$$= \langle 1/3, \; 1/3, \; 1/3 \rangle$$

(The prior was uniform, so the transition doesn't change it.)

**Step 3 — Sensor reports position 2 (Measurement Update).** The likelihood vector is:
$$L_2 = \langle P(e_1=2 \mid X=1), \; P(e_1=2 \mid X=2), \; P(e_1=2 \mid X=3) \rangle = \langle 0.1, \; 0.8, \; 0.1 \rangle$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and normalize:**
$$bel_1 = \alpha \cdot \overline{bel}_1 \odot L_2 = \alpha \cdot \langle 1/30, \; 8/30, \; 1/30 \rangle$$

Sum: $1/30 + 8/30 + 1/30 = 10/30 = 1/3$.
So $\alpha = 3$:
$$bel_1 = \langle 3/30, \; 24/30, \; 3/30 \rangle = \langle 0.1, \; 0.8, \; 0.1 \rangle$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The robot's belief has correctly updated to put **80% probability** on being at position 2. The Bayes filter has worked.

**Step 5 — Verify the recursive structure.** This matches the formula:
$$P(X_1 \mid e_1) = \alpha \cdot P(e_1 \mid X_1) \cdot \sum_{x_0} P(X_1 \mid x_0) \cdot P(x_0)$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Filtering Algorithms

| Algorithm | State Space | Model Type | Time per Step | Memory | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Exact Bayes Filter** | Discrete | Discrete | $O(\vert X\vert^2 \vert E\vert)$ | $O(\vert X\vert)$ | Small state tracking (toy examples) |
| **Kalman Filter** | Continuous (linear-Gaussian) | Linear dynamics | $O(d^3)$ | $O(d^2)$ | Robot odometry, drone navigation |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Continuous | Linearized nonlinear | $O(d^3)$ | $O(d^2)$ | GPS-INS fusion, SLAM |
| **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo)** | Continuous / hybrid | Arbitrary | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Localization under heavy noise |
| **Grid / Histogram Filter** | Continuous (discretized) | Arbitrary | $O(\vert X\vert)$ | $O(\vert X\vert)$ | 2D robot localization |
| **HMM (Viterbi / Forward-Backward)** | Discrete | Discrete DBN | $O(\vert X\vert^2 T)$ | $O(\vert X\vert)$ | Speech, gesture, fault sequence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a DBN in disguise.** It is a DBN with a single discrete hidden state variable and a single discrete evidence variable per time step. All the filtering, smoothing, and Viterbi algorithms apply to HMMs as special cases.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — From Static BN to DBN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Static Bayesian Network] --> B[Add Time Index t]
    B --> C[2-Slice Template 2TBN]
    C --> D[Unroll Across T Steps]
    D --> E[Dynamic Bayesian Network]
    E --> F[Filtering]
    E --> G[Prediction]
    E --> H[Smoothing]
    E --> I[Most Likely Explanation]
    F --> J[Online Robot Localization]
    G --> K[Trajectory Forecasting]
    H --> L[Post-hoc Sensor Calibration]
    I --> M[Best Path via Viterbi]
```

## 4.2 Generic Dynamic Bayesian Network — Two-Slice Template

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xt1["Xt minus 1 (hidden state)"] --> Xt["Xt (hidden state)"]
    Xt --> Xtp1["Xt plus 1 (hidden state)"]
    Xt --> Et["Et (observation)"]
    Xt1 --> Et1["Et minus 1 (observation)"]
    Ut["Ut (control action)"] --> Xt
    style Xt fill:#FFE4B5
    style Xt1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Xtp1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Et fill:#B0E0E6
    style Et1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style Ut fill:#90EE90
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The orange nodes ($X_{t-1}, X_t, X_{t+1}$) are the **hidden state** chain (Markov property).
> - The blue nodes ($E_{t-1}, E_t$) are the **observations** (sensor model).
> - The green node ($U_t$) is the **control input** (action taken by the robot).
> - The arcs $X_{t-1} \to X_t$ and $U_t \to X_t$ form the **transition model** $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1}, U_t)$.
> - The arc $X_t \to E_t$ is the **sensor model** $P(E_t \mid X_t)$.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Bayes' Filter Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input["Prior Belief bel_t minus 1"] --> Predict
    subgraph Predict["PREDICTION STEP (Transition)"]
        direction LR
        T1["Multiply by transition model P(Xt | Xt minus 1)"] --> T2["Sum over previous state"]
        T2 --> T3["Result: predicted belief bar-bel_t"]
    end
    T3 --> Update
    subgraph Update["UPDATE STEP (Measurement)"]
        direction LR
        U1["Receive new evidence et"] --> U2["Compute likelihood P(et | Xt)"]
        U2 --> U3["Element-wise multiply: bar-bel_t times likelihood"]
        U3 --> U4["Normalize so sum equals 1"]
        U4 --> U5["Result: posterior belief bel_t"]
    end
    U5 --> Output["Posterior Belief bel_t"]
    style Predict fill:#FFF8DC
    style Update fill:#E0FFFF
```

## 4.4 Three Canonical BN Topologies — D-Separation Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Chain["Causal Chain: A to B to C"]
        CA["A"] --> CB["B"] --> CC["C"]
    end
    subgraph Fork["Common Cause: A to B to C"]
        FA["A"] --> FB["B"]
        FC["C"] --> FB
    end
    subgraph V["V-Structure: A to B to C (Common Effect)"]
        VA["A"] --> VB["B"]
        VC["C"] --> VB
    end
    style Chain fill:#F0FFF0
    style Fork fill:#FFF0F5
    style V fill:#FFFACD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D-separation rule at a glance:**
> - In **Chain** and **Fork** topologies, conditioning on the middle node **blocks** the path → independence.
> - In **V-structure**, conditioning on the middle node **opens** the path → dependence ("explaining away").
