# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Three sentences + one formula. Be precise.

### Question 1: Define Uncertainty in AI. Why is probability preferred over Boolean logic for handling uncertainty in robotics? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
*Uncertainty in AI refers to the inability of an agent to determine the exact state of the world, the outcome of an action, or the value of a variable due to noisy sensors, incomplete information, or stochastic dynamics. It is quantified by probability values in the range **[0, 1]**.*
*Boolean logic yields only binary TRUE/FALSE values and cannot represent graded beliefs, partial observability, or noise. Probability theory provides a mathematically rigorous, continuous framework for representing beliefs, combining evidence via **Bayes' theorem**, and updating knowledge as new sensor data arrives — capabilities essential for robot localization, SLAM, and object recognition under noise.*

### Question 2: State Bayes' theorem and explain the meaning of each term. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$
- $P(H \mid E)$ = **Posterior** — belief in hypothesis $H$ after seeing evidence $E$
- $P(E \mid H)$ = **Likelihood** — probability of $E$ assuming $H$ is true
- $P(H)$ = **Prior** — initial belief in $H$ before evidence
- $P(E)$ = **Marginal evidence** — total probability of $E$ across all hypotheses (normalizing constant)

*Bayes' theorem allows a robot to **invert** the causal sensor model $P(E \mid H)$ into a diagnostic reasoning model $P(H \mid E)$, enabling it to recover the most likely hidden state from noisy observations.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Bayesian Network. With a suitable example, describe how joint probability distribution is factorized using the chain rule in Bayesian Networks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the architecture of Dynamic Bayesian Networks. Explain the three canonical inference tasks — filtering, prediction, and smoothing — with relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Bayesian Network (BN)** is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where:
- Each **node** represents a random variable.
- Each **directed edge** $X \to Y$ represents a direct probabilistic dependency.
- Each node has an associated **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** $P(X \mid \text{Parents}(X))$.

**Example:** A classic "Sprinkler-Rain-WetGrass" network:
- Nodes: `Cloudy` (C), `Sprinkler` (S), `Rain` (R), `WetGrass` (W)
- Edges: $C \to S$, $C \to R$, $S \to W$, $R \to W$

The **joint distribution** is factorized using the BN chain rule:
$$P(C, S, R, W) = P(C) \cdot P(S \mid C) \cdot P(R \mid C) \cdot P(W \mid S, R)$$

**Why this is powerful:** Instead of storing $2^4 - 1 = 15$ independent probabilities for 4 binary variables, we only need $1 + 2 + 2 + 4 = 9$ entries. The savings grow exponentially with the number of sparsely connected variables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Definition (2 m) + Diagram explanation (2 m) + Factorization derivation (2 m) + Example (1 m).

#### Model Solution for (b):

A **Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN)** extends a static BN across time. It encodes a **temporal stochastic process** under two assumptions:
1. **First-Order Markov:** $P(X_t \mid X_{0:t-1}) = P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$
2. **Sensor Markov:** $P(E_t \mid X_{0:t}, E_{0:t-1}) = P(E_t \mid X_t)$

**The 2-Slice Template (2TBN)** defines $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ and $P(E_t \mid X_t)$, which are then replicated for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$.

**Unrolled Joint Distribution:**
$$P(X_{0:T}, E_{0:T}) = P(X_0) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ P(X_t \mid X_{t-1}) \cdot P(E_t \mid X_t) \Big]$$

**Three Canonical Inference Tasks:**

**1. Filtering** — Estimate the present hidden state given all evidence so far:
$$P(X_t \mid e_{1:t}) = \alpha \cdot P(e_t \mid X_t) \cdot \sum_{x_{t-1}} P(X_t \mid x_{t-1}) \cdot P(x_{t-1} \mid e_{1:t-1})$$

*Used for online robot localization — the robot asks "where am I **now**?"*

**2. Prediction** — Estimate a future state given only past evidence:
$$P(X_{t+k} \mid e_{1:t}) = \sum_{x_{t+k-1}} P(X_{t+k} \mid x_{t+k-1}) \cdot P(x_{t+k-1} \mid e_{1:t})$$

*Used for trajectory forecasting — the robot asks "where will I be in 5 seconds?"*

**3. Smoothing** — Estimate a past state given all evidence up to the present:
$$P(X_k \mid e_{1:t}) = \alpha \cdot P(X_k \mid e_{1:k}) \cdot \sum_{x_{k+1}} P(x_{k+1} \mid X_k) \cdot P(x_{k+1} \mid e_{k+1:t})$$

*Used for offline post-hoc analysis — the robot asks "where **was** I at time $k$?" The forward-backward algorithm computes this for all $k$ in $O(T)$ time.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** DBN architecture (2 m) + Joint distribution formula (2 m) + Three inference tasks with equations (3 m).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive Bayes' theorem from the product rule of probability. Explain the role of prior, likelihood, and posterior with a robotics example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot operates in a circular environment with 3 distinct states. The prior probability distribution over the states is $\langle 0.4, 0.3, 0.3 \rangle$. The transition matrix is $T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ and the sensor likelihood for an observed reading $e_1 = \text{state 2}$ is $\langle 0.2, 0.7, 0.1 \rangle$. Compute the posterior belief distribution after one cycle of the Bayes' filter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from the Product Rule:**

**Step 1 — Product Rule (Two equivalent forms):**
For any two propositions $A, B$:
$$P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$
$$P(A, B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 2 — Equate the right-hand sides:**
$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $P(B)$:**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A \to H$ (hypothesis) and $B \to E$ (evidence):**
$$\boxed{P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator via the law of total probability** (assuming $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n$ are exhaustive and mutually exclusive):
$$P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)$$

**Robotics Example — Robot Localization:**
- **Hypothesis $H$:** "The robot is in room A."
- **Evidence $E$:** A wall-mounted camera reports the robot near a particular marker.
- **Prior $P(H)$:** From the previous time step's belief.
- **Likelihood $P(E \mid H)$:** Probability of the camera reading the marker **if** the robot is genuinely in room A (depends on camera noise).
- **Posterior $P(H \mid E)$:** Updated belief that the robot is in room A after combining prior with the new reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Derivation steps (3 m) + Notation explanation (2 m) + Robotics example (2 m).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Initial Belief Vector:**
$$bel_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Prediction Step (Transition):** Compute the predicted belief $\overline{bel}_1 = bel_0 \cdot T$:

$$\overline{bel}_1(X=1) = 0.4(0.5) + 0.3(0.2) + 0.3(0.1) = 0.20 + 0.06 + 0.03 = 0.29$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(X=2) = 0.4(0.3) + 0.3(0.6) + 0.3(0.4) = 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.12 = 0.42$$

$$\overline{bel}_1(X=3) = 0.4(0.2) + 0.3(0.2) + 0.3(0.5) = 0.08 + 0.06 + 0.15 = 0.29$$

So:
$$\overline{bel}_1 = \langle 0.29, \; 0.42, \; 0.29 \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Element-wise multiplication with the likelihood vector** $L = \langle 0.2, 0.7, 0.1 \rangle$:
$$\text{Unnormalized belief} = \overline{bel}_1 \odot L = \langle 0.29 \times 0.2, \; 0.42 \times 0.7, \; 0.29 \times 0.1 \rangle$$
$$= \langle 0.058, \; 0.294, \; 0.029 \rangle$$

**Step 4 — Normalize.** Sum of the unnormalized values:
$$S = 0.058 + 0.294 + 0.029 = 0.381$$

Normalizing constant: $\alpha = 1 / S = 1 / 0.381 \approx 2.625$

$$bel_1 = \alpha \cdot \langle 0.058, \; 0.294, \; 0.029 \rangle = \langle 0.152, \; 0.772, \; 0.076 \rangle$$

**Verification:** $0.152 + 0.772 + 0.076 = 1.000$ ✓

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{bel_1 = \langle 0.152, \; 0.772, \; 0.076 \rangle}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Prediction matrix multiplication (2 m) + Likelihood multiplication (2 m) + Normalization with alpha (2 m) + Final answer (1 m).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** the posterior in Bayes' filter problems — partial credit lost on the final answer. Always show $\alpha = 1 / \sum \text{unnormalized}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing prior and likelihood** in Bayes' theorem derivations. $P(H)$ is the **prior** (before evidence); $P(E \mid H)$ is the **likelihood** (probability of evidence given hypothesis). Many students swap them.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Markov assumption** in DBN derivations. The Markov property is *not* a free gift — it must be **explicitly stated** in derivations of the filtering recursion.
>
> 4. **Mixing up filtering and smoothing** in conceptual questions. Filtering = estimate the present. Smoothing = estimate the past. Prediction = estimate the future. Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Not citing the 2TBN** in DBN architecture questions. KTU examiners expect the two-slice template to be explicitly mentioned.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention D-separation** in BN independence questions. D-separation is the **standard test** for conditional independence in BNs and is a guaranteed KTU question topic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- **Uncertainty** is the inability to know a variable's true value with certainty; **probability** is the mathematically rigorous way to quantify it.
- **Probability axioms (Kolmogorov):** non-negativity, normalization, additivity. Everything in this module follows from these.
- **Bayes' theorem** inverts $P(E \mid H)$ into $P(H \mid E)$: $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$.
- **Conditional independence** is the key to making probabilistic models tractable.
- **Bayesian Network** = DAG + CPTs. The joint distribution factorizes as $\prod_i P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
- **Three BN connection patterns:** Causal chain, Common cause (fork), Common effect (V-structure / explaining away).
- **D-separation** is the formal graphical test for conditional independence in BNs.
- **Markov assumption** (first-order): future depends only on the present, not the entire past.
- **Sensor Markov assumption:** current evidence depends only on the current state.
- **Stationarity** of DBN: transition and sensor models are time-invariant.
- **2TBN** (two-slice template) fully specifies a DBN; unroll it across $T$ time steps to get the full joint.
- **Filtering** = present state estimate; formula involves a sum over $x_{t-1}$ and a likelihood multiplication.
- **Prediction** = future state estimate; iteratively apply the transition model without new evidence.
- **Smoothing** = past state estimate; combines forward message $P(X_k \mid e_{1:k})$ with backward message $P(e_{k+1:t} \mid X_k)$.
- **Most Likely Explanation (Viterbi):** replaces the sum in filtering with a max to find the best trajectory.
- **HMM** = special case of DBN with a single discrete hidden state and a single discrete evidence per slice.
- **Kalman Filter** = exact Bayes filter for **linear Gaussian** systems; gives $O(d^3)$ per step.
- **Particle Filter** = approximate Bayes filter using **Monte Carlo sampling**; handles nonlinear, non-Gaussian systems.
- **Practical robotics applications:** localization, SLAM, fault diagnosis, object recognition, sensor fusion, medical robotics, autonomous vehicles.
- **D-separation cheat:** Chain and Fork → middle node blocks. V-structure → middle node opens (explaining away).
- **"Probability is the calculus of belief."** Always normalize.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basis of Utility Theory, Decision Theory, and Sequential Decision Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basis of Utility Theory, Decision Theory, and Sequential Decision Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Utility Theory — The Mathematics of Preference

### Formal Definition
**Utility Theory** is the mathematical framework that quantifies an agent's preferences over uncertain outcomes. It assigns a real number $U(x)$ to every outcome $x$, representing the *degree of satisfaction* or *value* the agent derives from that outcome. A rational agent selects the action that **maximizes Expected Utility (MEU)**, not necessarily the action that maximizes expected monetary value.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terms: *Utility theory provides the normative foundation for representing and reasoning about preferences under uncertainty, enabling an autonomous agent to make optimal decisions when outcomes are stochastic.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition (Board-Expected Wording):**
> *Utility is a real-valued function $U: \mathcal{O} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps each outcome in the outcome space $\mathcal{O}$ to a scalar preference score, preserving the agent's preference ordering under uncertainty.*

### The Six Axioms of Utility (Von Neumann–Morgenstern)
For a utility function to exist, an agent's preferences must obey:

| # | Axiom | Plain Meaning |
|---|-------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ordering (Completeness)** | For any two outcomes $A$ and $B$, the agent either prefers $A$, prefers $B$, or is indifferent. |
| 2 | **Transitivity** | If $A \succeq B$ and $B \succeq C$, then $A \succeq C$. |
| 3 | **Continuity** | If $A \succeq B \succeq C$, then there exists $p \in [0,1]$ such that $B \sim pA + (1-p)C$. |
| 4 | **Substitutability** | If $A \sim B$, then the agent is indifferent to substituting $A$ for $B$ in any lottery. |
| 5 | **Monotonicity** | If $A \succeq B$, then $pA + (1-p)C \succeq pB + (1-p)C$. |
| 6 | **Decomposability** | $pA + (1-p)(qB + (1-q)C) \sim pqA + (1-pq)C$. |

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Gambler"
Imagine you walk into a café. The owner offers you a choice:
- **Option A:** Take **₹500 cash** for sure.
- **Option B:** Flip a fair coin — heads you win **₹1000**, tails you win **₹0**.

The *expected monetary value* of Option B is $0.5 \times 1000 + 0.5 \times 0 = 500$ — the same as Option A. Yet most people prefer Option A. Why? Because the *utility* of money is **not linear** in money. The pain of losing ₹500 is psychologically greater than the joy of gaining ₹500. Utility theory mathematically captures this curvature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Expected Monetary Value (EMV) and Expected Utility (EU) coincide **only** when the utility function is **linear**. For any other shape (concave, convex), they differ, and a rational agent chooses the action that **maximizes EU**, not EMV.

---

## 1.2 Decision Theory — The Engine of Rational Choice

### Formal Definition
**Decision Theory** is the mathematical study of decision-making under uncertainty. It integrates probability theory (for beliefs about the world) with utility theory (for preferences over outcomes) to prescribe the action that an agent **should** take.

A **decision problem** is formally defined by the tuple:

$$\langle \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{O}, P, U \rangle$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{D}$ = set of **decisions/actions** available to the agent
- $\mathcal{S}$ = set of possible **states of the world** (uncontrollable)
- $\mathcal{O}$ = set of possible **outcomes**
- $P(O \vert D, S)$ = **probability** of outcome given decision and state
- $U: \mathcal{O} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = **utility function**

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather-Aware Picnic Planner"
You are planning a picnic. You can either *go* or *cancel*. You cannot control the weather ($\mathcal{S}$: sunny, rainy). Each combination of *your action* and *the weather* produces an *outcome* (great picnic, ruined picnic, lost booking fee). Decision theory gives you a single number — the **expected utility** for each action — so you can pick the highest one. It's a calculator for "the best choice when life is uncertain."

---

## 1.3 Sequential Decision Problems — Decisions Across Time

### Formal Definition
A **Sequential Decision Problem** is a decision problem in which the agent must make a **sequence of decisions over time**, where each decision may depend on prior outcomes and the *state* of the world may evolve. The canonical formal model is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **MDP Formal Definition (Board-Expected):**
> An MDP is a tuple $\langle S, A, T, R \rangle$ where:
> - $S$ = finite set of **states**
> - $A$ = finite set of **actions**
> - $T(s, a, s') = P(s' \vert s, a)$ = **transition model**
> - $R(s, a, s')$ = **reward function**

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot in a Grid World"
Picture a small wheeled robot in a 5×5 factory floor. At every clock tick, the robot must choose: move *North, South, East,* or *West*. Some cells are obstacles (high negative reward), one cell is the charging dock (high positive reward), and the floor is slippery (uncertain transitions). The robot must plan a **sequence of moves** — not a single decision — to maximize total reward. This is exactly a sequential decision problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Tag:** Module 3 of PEBRT525 covers the *basis* of these theories — the mathematical machinery, not the solution algorithms (Value Iteration, Policy Iteration belong to Module 4). Focus on **formulation, axioms, Bellman equation**, and **expected utility calculation**.

---

## 1.4 Risk Attitude Classification

The **shape** of the utility function encodes the agent's attitude toward risk:

| Risk Attitude | Utility Shape | Mathematical Property | Behaviour |
|---------------|---------------|------------------------|-----------|
| **Risk-Averse** | Concave $U''(x) < 0$ | Prefers certain ₹500 over ₹1000/₹0 coin flip | "A bird in the hand" |
| **Risk-Neutral** | Linear $U''(x) = 0$ | Indifferent between certain and gamble with same EMV | Pure EMV maximizer |
| **Risk-Seeking** | Convex $U''(x) > 0$ | Prefers the gamble over the certain amount | Lottery lover |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Expected Utility

### Step 1: Identify the Lottery
A **lottery** $L$ is a probability distribution over outcomes. For a finite set of outcomes $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ with probabilities $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$:

$$L = \langle p_1 : x_1,\; p_2 : x_2,\; \ldots,\; p_n : x_n \rangle$$

### Step 2: Compute Expected Utility
For action $A$ leading to outcomes $O_i$ with probabilities $P(O_i \vert A)$:

$$EU(A) \;=\; \sum_{i} P(O_i \vert A) \cdot U(O_i)$$

### Step 3: Apply the MEU Principle
The **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)** principle prescribes:

$$A^{*} \;=\; \underset{A \in \mathcal{D}}{\arg\max}\; EU(A)$$

This is the **central normative prescription** of decision theory. The agent *should* pick $A^{*}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MEU and not EMV?** Expected Monetary Value (EMV) treats money linearly. But human/robotic preferences over money are *non-linear* (think diminishing marginal utility of wealth). MEU subsumes EMV as the special case where $U$ is linear.

---

## 2.2 Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium

### Certainty Equivalent (CE)
The **Certainty Equivalent** of a lottery $L$ is the guaranteed amount of money $c$ that yields the *same utility* as the lottery's expected utility:

$$U(CE) \;=\; EU(L) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad CE \;=\; U^{-1}\!\big(EU(L)\big)$$

### Risk Premium ($\pi$)
The **Risk Premium** is the difference between the *expected monetary value* of the lottery and its certainty equivalent:

$$\pi \;=\; EMV(L) \;-\; CE(L)$$

For a risk-averse agent, $\pi > 0$. For risk-neutral, $\pi = 0$. For risk-seeking, $\pi < 0$.

---

## 2.3 Standard Utility Function Forms (KTU High-Yield)

| Function | Formula | Risk Attitude | Parameters |
|----------|---------|---------------|------------|
| **Linear** | $U(x) = ax + b$ | Risk-Neutral | $a > 0$ |
| **Exponential (negative)** | $U(x) = -e^{-ax}$ for $a > 0$ | Risk-Averse | $a$ = risk aversion coefficient |
| **Logarithmic** | $U(x) = \ln(x)$ for $x > 0$ | Risk-Averse | None |
| **Square root** | $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ for $x \geq 0$ | Risk-Averse | None |
| **Exponential (positive)** | $U(x) = e^{ax}$ for $a > 0$ | Risk-Seeking | $a$ = risk-seeking coefficient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these forms?** They are the canonical examples used in KTU board exam numerical problems. A 7-mark question will typically specify a utility function from this table and ask you to compute $EU$, $CE$, and $\pi$.

---

## 2.4 Decision Networks (Influence Diagrams)

A **Decision Network** (also called an **Influence Diagram**) is a graphical representation of a decision problem that extends Bayesian networks with two extra node types:

| Node Type | Shape | Meaning |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| **Chance node** | Oval | Random variable with $P(X \vert \text{parents})$ |
| **Decision node** | Rectangle | Action chosen by the agent |
| **Utility node** | Diamond | Utility $U$ of the configuration of its parents |

### Structural Rules
- The agent's **information** about chance nodes is encoded by **arcs *into* a decision node** (what the agent knows when deciding).
- The agent's **action** is encoded by **arcs *out of* a decision node** (which chance nodes the action affects).
- The **utility node** has no children — it is a leaf.

### The Three Evaluation Steps
1. **Set chance node values** to their expected value (sum over all values of the node).
2. **For each decision node**, compute the action that maximizes expected utility.
3. **Sum the resulting utilities** of the utility nodes.

---

## 2.5 Value of Information (VoI)

The **Value of Perfect Information (VoPI)** of a piece of evidence $E_j$ for decision $D_i$ is the expected gain in utility from learning $E_j$ *before* deciding:

$$VoPI(E_j) \;=\; \left(\sum_{k} P(E_j = e_{jk}) \cdot EU(\alpha_{e_{jk}}^{*} \vert E_j = e_{jk})\right) \;-\; EU(\alpha^{*})$$

Where:
- $EU(\alpha^{*})$ = the MEU *without* the evidence
- $EU(\alpha_{e_{jk}}^{*} \vert E_j = e_{jk})$ = the MEU *given* the evidence value $e_{jk}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** $VoPI \geq 0$ always. Information can never reduce the maximum expected utility of a rational agent. This is a theorem, not just intuition — the value of information is **non-negative**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
|---|---------|---------|-----|
| 1 | Expected Utility | $EU(A) = \sum_{i} P(O_i \vert A) U(O_i)$ | Compute utility of an action |
| 2 | MEU Principle | $A^{*} = \arg\max_{A} EU(A)$ | Choose optimal action |
| 3 | Certainty Equivalent | $CE = U^{-1}(EU(L))$ | Find guaranteed equivalent |
| 4 | Risk Premium | $\pi = EMV(L) - CE(L)$ | Quantify risk aversion |
| 5 | VoPI | $VoPI(E_j) = \sum_{k} P(e_{jk}) EU(\alpha^{*} \vert e_{jk}) - EU(\alpha^{*})$ | Decide whether to gather info |
| 6 | Bellman Equation (MDP) | $U(s) = R(s) + \gamma \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s') U(s')$ | Optimal value of a state |
| 7 | Optimal Policy | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma U(s')]$ | Best action in state $s$ |
| 8 | Discount Factor Bounds | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ | Finite horizon when $\gamma < 1$ |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

These mathematical tools are not abstract — they are deployed in:

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Deciding whether to brake, swerve, or accelerate under sensor uncertainty (risk-averse utility of passenger safety).
- **Robotic Surgery:** Planning a sequence of incisions under tissue-property uncertainty (sequential MDP with high reward for success).
- **Financial Robo-Advisors:** Allocating portfolios under market uncertainty (risk-averse utility of logarithmic wealth).
- **Space Robotics (NASA):** Planning deep-space maneuvers under communication delay and actuator uncertainty (MDP with delayed rewards, $\gamma < 1$).
- **Warehouse Robots:** Path planning in stochastic grids (grid-world MDP).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says *"a robot is navigating a stochastic environment"*, translate that immediately into an MDP. State = robot location, Action = direction, Transition = slip probability, Reward = +1 for goal, −1 for obstacle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Expected Utility and Certainty Equivalent

**Problem Statement (KTU Board Typical):**
A decision-maker faces a lottery with probability $0.5$ of winning $\text{₹}100$ and probability $0.5$ of winning $\text{₹}0$. The agent's utility function is $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Compute:
1. Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
2. Expected Utility (EU)
3. Certainty Equivalent (CE)
4. Risk Premium ($\pi$)

### Step 1 — Compute the Expected Monetary Value

The EMV of a lottery is the probability-weighted sum of monetary payoffs:

$$EMV(L) = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot x_i$$

For our two-outcome lottery $\{0.5 : 100,\; 0.5 : 0\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(L) &= (0.5)(100) + (0.5)(0) \\
&= 50 + 0 \\
&= \text{₹}50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Each outcome is multiplied by its probability and the products are summed. This is the *monetary* expectation, ignoring utility curvature.

### Step 2 — Compute the Expected Utility

The Expected Utility replaces monetary values with their utilities:

$$EU(L) = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot U(x_i)$$

Using $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(L) &= (0.5) \cdot U(100) + (0.5) \cdot U(0) \\
&= (0.5) \cdot \sqrt{100} + (0.5) \cdot \sqrt{0} \\
&= (0.5)(10) + (0.5)(0) \\
&= 5 + 0 \\
&= 5 \text{ utility units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The utility of ₹100 under $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is 10 utils. The utility of ₹0 is 0 utils. The probability-weighted average is 5 utils.

### Step 3 — Compute the Certainty Equivalent

The CE is the guaranteed amount $c$ such that $U(c) = EU(L)$:

$$U(CE) = EU(L) \implies \sqrt{CE} = 5$$

Solving for $CE$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{CE} &= 5 \\
(\sqrt{CE})^{2} &= 5^{2} \\
CE &= 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Squaring both sides inverts the utility function. The agent is indifferent between the gamble and a guaranteed ₹25.

### Step 4 — Compute the Risk Premium

The risk premium is the gap between expected monetary value and the certainty equivalent:

$$\pi = EMV(L) - CE(L)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= 50 - 25 \\
&= \text{₹}25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The agent is willing to "sacrifice" ₹25 of expected monetary value (from ₹50 down to ₹25) to avoid the risk. This quantifies the *cost of risk aversion* — the agent pays ₹25 to convert the gamble into a certainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\pi > 0$, the agent is **risk-averse**. Since $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is concave ($U''(x) = -\frac{1}{4} x^{-3/2} < 0$ for $x > 0$), this is consistent with risk-aversion theory.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Bellman Equation for an MDP

**Problem:** Consider a 1-D grid-world with three states $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$. The robot can move *Right* (R) or *Left* (L). The transition model is:
- $T(s_1, R, s_2) = 0.8$ (with probability 0.2, stay at $s_1$)
- $T(s_2, R, s_3) = 0.8$ (with probability 0.2, stay at $s_2$)
- $T(s_2, L, s_1) = 0.8$ (with probability 0.2, stay at $s_2$)
- $T(s_1, L, s_1) = 1.0$ (bump into wall, stay)
- $T(s_3, R, s_3) = 1.0$ (bump into wall, stay)
- $T(s_3, L, s_2) = 0.8$ (with probability 0.2, stay at $s_3$)

Rewards: $R(s_3) = +10$ (goal!), $R(s_1) = 0$, $R(s_2) = 0$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Goal:** Derive the Bellman equation for the value of state $s_1$ under the optimal policy.

### Step 1 — Write the General Bellman Equation
For any state $s$ under a fixed policy $\pi$, the value $U^{\pi}(s)$ is the expected discounted cumulative reward:

$$U^{\pi}(s) \;=\; R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s, \pi(s), s') \cdot U^{\pi}(s')$$

For the **optimal** value $U^{*}(s)$, we maximize over actions:

$$U^{*}(s) \;=\; R(s) + \gamma \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s') \cdot U^{*}(s')$$

### Step 2 — Specialize to State $s_1$
The robot at $s_1$ has two actions: $R$ and $L$.

For action $R$ at $s_1$:
- With probability $0.8$, go to $s_2$
- With probability $0.2$, stay at $s_1$

For action $L$ at $s_1$:
- With probability $1.0$, stay at $s_1$ (wall)

### Step 3 — Compute Expected Utility for Each Action
For action $R$ at $s_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{R}(s_1) &= R(s_1) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s_1, R, s') \cdot U^{*}(s') \\
&= 0 + (0.9) \big[ T(s_1, R, s_1) \cdot U^{*}(s_1) + T(s_1, R, s_2) \cdot U^{*}(s_2) \big] \\
&= (0.9) \big[ (0.2) U^{*}(s_1) + (0.8) U^{*}(s_2) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

For action $L$ at $s_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{L}(s_1) &= R(s_1) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s_1, L, s') \cdot U^{*}(s') \\
&= 0 + (0.9) \big[ T(s_1, L, s_1) \cdot U^{*}(s_1) \big] \\
&= (0.9)(1.0) \cdot U^{*}(s_1) \\
&= 0.9 \cdot U^{*}(s_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Maximum
The optimal value is the maximum over actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*}(s_1) &= \max \big\{ EU_{R}(s_1),\; EU_{L}(s_1) \big\} \\
&= \max \big\{ 0.9 \big[ 0.2 U^{*}(s_1) + 0.8 U^{*}(s_2) \big],\; 0.9 \cdot U^{*}(s_1) \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The first term (action $R$) considers possible progress to $s_2$; the second term (action $L$) only yields the discounted self-loop. Intuitively, $R$ is likely the better action because it opens the path to the goal at $s_3$.

### Step 5 — Compare the Two Expressions
For action $R$ to dominate action $L$, we need:

$$
0.9 \big[ 0.2 U^{*}(s_1) + 0.8 U^{*}(s_2) \big] \;>\; 0.9 \cdot U^{*}(s_1)
$$

Dividing by $0.9$:

$$0.2 U^{*}(s_1) + 0.8 U^{*}(s_2) \;>\; U^{*}(s_1)$$

Subtracting $0.2 U^{*}(s_1)$ from both sides:

$$0.8 U^{*}(s_2) \;>\; 0.8 U^{*}(s_1)$$

$$U^{*}(s_2) \;>\; U^{*}(s_1)$$

This is intuitively obvious: $s_2$ is *closer* to the high-reward goal $s_3$, so it should have a higher value. The algebraic derivation confirms this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** For an arbitrary state $s$ in any MDP, the Bellman equation decomposes the value of a state into the *immediate reward* plus the *discounted expected value* of the next state, with the maximization taken over all available actions. This recursion is the foundation of all MDP solution algorithms (Value Iteration, Policy Iteration, Q-Learning).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How to Identify the Question Type

When you see a KTU question, use this decision matrix to identify the *type* of decision-theoretic calculation required:

| Question Clue | Type | Formula to Use | Output |
|---------------|------|----------------|--------|
| *"What is the expected utility of action A?"* | EU Calculation | $EU(A) = \sum p_i U(x_i)$ | Scalar utility value |
| *"What is the certainty equivalent?"* | Inverse Utility | $CE = U^{-1}(EU)$ | Guaranteed monetary amount |
| *"Find the risk premium"* | Risk Quantification | $\pi = EMV - CE$ | Non-negative number |
| *"Which action should the agent take?"* | MEU | $A^{*} = \arg\max EU(A)$ | Selected action |
| *"Is it worth gathering evidence E?"* | VoPI | Compare VoPI with cost | Yes / No |
| *"Find the optimal value of state s"* | Bellman | $U^{*}(s) = R(s) + \gamma \max \sum T U$ | Recursive equation |
| *"What is the optimal policy?"* | $\pi^{*}$ | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max \sum T[R + \gamma U]$ | Action per state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Network (Influence Diagram) — Generic Structure

The figure below shows a generic decision network. The **robot** must decide an action based on a **sensor reading**, which probabilistically depends on the **environment state**. The utility depends on both the action and the state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1["ENV STATE"]:::chance
    sensor1["SENSOR READING"]:::chance
    action1["ROBOT ACTION"]:::decision
    utility1["UTILITY"]:::utility

    state1 --> sensor1
    sensor1 --> action1
    state1 --> utility1
    action1 --> utility1

    classDef chance fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef utility fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **ENV STATE → SENSOR READING:** The sensor reading is noisy evidence about the true environment state.
- **SENSOR READING → ROBOT ACTION:** The robot's decision is *informed* by the sensor reading (the arc into the decision node encodes the robot's information).
- **ENV STATE → UTILITY:** The true state affects the outcome.
- **ROBOT ACTION → UTILITY:** The robot's action affects the outcome.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree for a Sequential Decision Problem

The figure below shows a 2-step decision tree. The decision-maker first picks *Test* or *No Test*. If tested, they receive evidence $E_+$ or $E_-$, then pick action $A_1$ or $A_2$. Each leaf is labelled with its utility.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["DECISION D0"]:::decision
    test1["Test"]:::decision
    notest1["No Test"]:::decision
    eplus1["E Positive"]:::chance
    eminus1["E Negative"]:::chance
    a1a["Action A1"]:::decision
    a1b["Action A2"]:::decision
    a2a["Action A1"]:::decision
    a2b["Action A2"]:::decision
    u1["U=50"]:::utility
    u2["U=20"]:::utility
    u3["U=10"]:::utility
    u4["U=30"]:::utility
    u5["U=40"]:::utility
    u6["U=25"]:::utility

    start1 --> test1
    start1 --> notest1
    test1 --> eplus1
    test1 --> eminus1
    notest1 --> a2a
    notest1 --> a2b
    eplus1 --> a1a
    eplus1 --> a1b
    a1a --> u1
    a1a --> u2
    a1b --> u3
    a1b --> u4
    a2a --> u5
    a2b --> u6

    classDef chance fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef utility fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000000
```

**Evaluation rule (right-to-left):**
- Each **utility leaf** is taken as-is.
- Each **chance node** is replaced by the probability-weighted sum of its children.
- Each **decision node** is replaced by the maximum of its children (MEU).
- The value at the **root** is the expected utility of the optimal plan.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rational Robotic Agent

This block diagram shows how utility theory, decision theory, and sequential decision-making integrate inside an autonomous robotic agent.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    block1["PERCEPTION MODULE"]:::block
    block2["BELIEF UPDATE P S GIVEN E"]:::block
    block3["UTILITY FUNCTION U X"]:::block
    block4["DECISION ENGINE ARGMAX EU"]:::block
    block5["POLPI MAP STATE TO ACTION"]:::block
    block6["ACTUATION MODULE"]:::block
    block7["ENVIRONMENT"]:::block
    block8["REWARD SENSOR R S A S DASH"]:::block

    block7 --> block1
    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block4
    block3 --> block4
    block4 --> block5
    block5 --> block6
    block6 --> block7
    block7 --> block8
    block8 --> block2

    classDef block fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
```

**Functional flow:**
- The **Environment** feeds raw signals to the **Perception Module**.
- Perception updates the **Belief State** $P(s \vert e)$ via Bayesian inference.
- The **Utility Function** $U(x)$ encodes the agent's preferences.
- The **Decision Engine** computes $\arg\max_{a} EU(a)$ given current beliefs.
- The **Policy** maps states to actions (the output of decision-making).
- The **Actuation Module** executes the action on the environment.
- The **Reward Sensor** reports immediate reward, which is fed back to update beliefs (for learning modules).

---

## 4.4 Risk Attitude Map (Concave / Convex Utility Functions)

This schematic shows how the shape of the utility function determines risk attitude.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["RISK AVERSE CONCAVE"]:::nodeAstyle
    nodeB["RISK NEUTRAL LINEAR"]:::nodeBstyle
    nodeC["RISK SEEKING CONVEX"]:::nodeCstyle
    nodeD["U DOUBLE PRIME X LESS THAN 0"]:::nodeAlabel
    nodeE["U DOUBLE PRIME X EQUALS 0"]:::nodeBlabel
    nodeF["U DOUBLE PRIME X GREATER THAN 0"]:::nodeClabel
    nodeG["PREFERS CERTAINTY OVER GAMBLE"]:::nodeAlabel
    nodeH["INDIFFERENT TO RISK"]:::nodeBlabel
    nodeI["PREFERS GAMBLE OVER CERTAINTY"]:::nodeClabel

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeI

    classDef nodeAstyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000000
    classDef nodeBstyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef nodeCstyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a02020,color:#000000
    classDef nodeAlabel fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000000
    classDef nodeBlabel fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef nodeClabel fill:#ffebee,stroke:#a02020,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**
- A **concave** function ($U''(x) < 0$) means the agent values each additional rupee *less* than the previous one — the hallmark of risk aversion.
- A **linear** function ($U''(x) = 0$) means the agent values every rupee equally — risk neutrality.
- A **convex** function ($U''(x) > 0$) means the agent values each additional rupee *more* than the previous — risk seeking (e.g., gamblers).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define utility theory. List and briefly explain any four axioms of utility.**

**Model Answer:**

Utility theory is a mathematical framework that assigns a real-valued preference score $U(x)$ to each outcome $x$, enabling rational choice under uncertainty. An agent selects the action that maximizes expected utility.

Four axioms of utility:

1. **Ordering (Completeness):** For any two outcomes $A$ and $B$, either $A \succeq B$ or $B \succeq A$ (or both, meaning indifference). The agent can always rank outcomes.

2. **Transitivity:** If $A \succeq B$ and $B \succeq C$, then $A \succeq C$. Preferences are consistent — no cycles.

3. **Continuity:** If $A \succeq B \succeq C$, then there exists some probability $p \in [0,1]$ such that the agent is indifferent between $B$ and the lottery $[p:A, (1-p):C]$. No outcome is infinitely preferred over others.

4. **Monotonicity:** If $A \succeq B$, then for any probability $p$ and any outcome $C$, the lottery $pA + (1-p)C$ is weakly preferred to $pB + (1-p)C$. Improving one component of a lottery weakly improves the lottery.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Markov Decision Process (MDP)? List its four components.**

**Model Answer:**

A Markov Decision Process is a formal model for sequential decision-making in stochastic environments where outcomes are partly random and partly under the agent's control.

The four components of an MDP are:

1. **States ($S$):** A finite set of situations the agent can be in. Example: $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ in a grid world.

2. **Actions ($A$):** A finite set of moves the agent can make in each state. Example: $\{North, South, East, West\}$.

3. **Transition Model ($T$):** $T(s, a, s') = P(s' \vert s, a)$ gives the probability of reaching state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$. Encodes the stochasticity of the world.

4. **Reward Function ($R$):** $R(s, a, s')$ gives the immediate numerical reward received when the agent transitions from $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$. Encodes the goal.

The **Markov property** requires that the next state depends only on the current state and action, not on the history of prior states.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Answering!**
> - **EU ≠ EMV.** Examiners deduct 2 marks if you confuse these in numerical problems. Always state which one you are computing.
> - **Decision networks vs. Bayesian networks.** A decision network has *three* node types (chance, decision, utility). A Bayesian network has *one* (chance). Confusing them costs 3 marks.
> - **Bellman equation is a *recursion*, not a closed form.** Do not write $U(s) = R(s) / (1 - \gamma)$ unless every state has identical transitions — that shortcut loses 4 marks.
> - **Show every step.** Examiners allocate partial marks per step. Skipping the substitution step forfeits those marks.

---

### Question A (Alternative 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the axioms of utility theory. Discuss how violation of the transitivity axiom leads to a "money pump" and why rational agents must obey it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot must choose between three actions with the following payoffs and probabilities. The robot's utility function is $U(x) = \ln(x+1)$ where $x$ is the monetary reward in rupees.

| Action | Outcome 1 | Probability 1 | Outcome 2 | Probability 2 |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| $A_1$ | ₹1000 | 0.5 | ₹100 | 0.5 |
| $A_2$ | ₹600 | 1.0 | — | — |
| $A_3$ | ₹2000 | 0.3 | ₹0 | 0.7 |

Compute the expected utility of each action and identify the optimal action. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**The Six Axioms of Utility:**

1. **Ordering (Completeness):** For any two lotteries $L$ and $M$, the agent must have $L \succeq M$ or $M \succeq L$ (or both). This ensures no outcome is incomparable.

2. **Transitivity:** If $L \succeq M$ and $M \succeq N$, then $L \succeq N$. **This is critical for rationality** — it ensures the agent's preferences are *internally consistent*.

3. **Continuity:** If $L \succeq M \succeq N$, then for some $p \in [0,1]$, $M \sim pL + (1-p)N$. Ensures no outcome is so good or so bad that no mixture of it with another can be equally valued.

4. **Substitutability (Independence — strict form):** If $L \sim M$, then for any $N$ and $p \in [0,1]$, $pL + (1-p)N \sim pM + (1-p)N$. Indifference between $L$ and $M$ is preserved under mixture with a common lottery.

5. **Monotonicity:** If $L \succeq M$, then for any $N$ and $p \in [0,1]$, $pL + (1-p)N \succeq pM + (1-p)N$. (This follows from substitutability in the standard VNM framework.)

6. **Decomposability (Reduction of compound lotteries):** $pL + (1-p)(qM + (1-q)N) \sim pqL + (1-pq)N + \ldots$ Complex lotteries can be reduced to simple ones.

**The Money Pump Argument (Why Transitivity is Mandatory):**

Suppose a (non-transitive) agent has preferences $A \succ B \succ C \succ A$ (a "preference cycle"). A clever trader can exploit this:

- The agent is willing to pay a small premium to swap $C$ for $B$ (since $B \succ C$).
- The agent is willing to pay again to swap $B$ for $A$ (since $A \succ B$).
- The agent is willing to pay *again* to swap $A$ for $C$ (since $C \succ A$).

After the cycle, the agent is back to the original outcome $C$ but has lost money three times. The trader can repeat this "money pump" indefinitely, draining all the agent's wealth. A rational agent must therefore obey transitivity, or be exploited to bankruptcy.

> **Examiner's Note:** The money-pump argument is a 2-mark bonus if you mention it. The full axiom list carries 4 marks. Defining utility carries 1 mark.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $EU(A_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A_1) &= (0.5) \cdot U(1000) + (0.5) \cdot U(100) \\
&= (0.5) \cdot \ln(1001) + (0.5) \cdot \ln(101) \\
&= (0.5)(6.9088) + (0.5)(4.6151) \\
&= 3.4544 + 2.3076 \\
&= 5.7620 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $EU(A_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A_2) &= (1.0) \cdot U(600) \\
&= \ln(601) \\
&= 6.3986 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $EU(A_3)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A_3) &= (0.3) \cdot U(2000) + (0.7) \cdot U(0) \\
&= (0.3) \cdot \ln(2001) + (0.7) \cdot \ln(1) \\
&= (0.3)(7.6014) + (0.7)(0) \\
&= 2.2804 + 0 \\
&= 2.2804 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the MEU Principle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A_1) &= 5.7620 \\
EU(A_2) &= 6.3986 \\
EU(A_3) &= 2.2804
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $EU(A_2) > EU(A_1) > EU(A_3)$, the optimal action is:

$$A^{*} = A_2$$

> **Reasoning:** Even though $A_1$ has a higher expected monetary value ($0.5 \times 1000 + 0.5 \times 100 = 550$ vs $A_2$'s certain $600$), the agent is risk-averse (log utility is concave), and prefers the *guaranteed* ₹600 over the gamble. The risk premium of $A_1$ relative to $A_2$ is $600 - \text{some CE}$ — a guaranteed certainty beats a 50-50 chance at the same expected value when the utility function is concave.

---

### Question B (Alternative 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the components of a decision network with a neat diagram. Explain the three steps for evaluating a decision network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1-D grid-world MDP with states $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ and actions $\{R, L\}$ (Right, Left). Transitions:
- $T(s_1, R, s_2) = 0.9$, $T(s_1, R, s_1) = 0.1$
- $T(s_2, R, s_3) = 0.9$, $T(s_2, R, s_2) = 0.1$
- $T(s_3, R, s_3) = 1.0$
- $T(s_2, L, s_1) = 0.9$, $T(s_2, L, s_2) = 0.1$
- $T(s_1, L, s_1) = 1.0$, $T(s_3, L, s_2) = 0.9$, $T(s_3, L, s_3) = 0.1$

Rewards: $R(s_3) = +10$, $R(s_1) = R(s_2) = 0$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$.

Write the Bellman equation for each state and identify the optimal action in each state. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
A **Decision Network** (or **Influence Diagram**) is a graphical formalism that extends a Bayesian network with two additional node types to represent decisions and utilities. It compactly represents a single-stage decision problem under uncertainty.

**Components (Three Node Types):**

1. **Chance nodes (ovals):** Random variables with associated probability distributions $P(X \vert \text{parents})$. Example: *Weather*, *Sensor Reading*, *Machine Health*.

2. **Decision nodes (rectangles):** Represent the agent's choice variables. The agent selects one value from the node's domain. Example: *Take Umbrella*, *Repair or Replace*.

3. **Utility nodes (diamonds):** Represent the agent's utility function. A utility node has no children; its value is the utility of the configuration of its parents. Example: *Satisfaction*.

**Three Structural Arc Types:**

- **Arc from chance → chance:** Probabilistic dependence.
- **Arc from chance → decision:** The agent *observes* the chance variable before deciding (the agent's *information*).
- **Arc from decision → chance/utility:** The agent's *action* influences the chance variable or utility.

**Diagram (drawn as part of the answer):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["WEATHER"]:::chance
    n2["FORECAST"]:::chance
    n3["CARRY UMBRELLA"]:::decision
    n4["UTILITY"]:::utility

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n1 --> n4
    n3 --> n4

    classDef chance fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef utility fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000000
```

**Three Evaluation Steps:**

1. **Set chance nodes to their expected values.** For each chance node, sum over all possible values, weighted by the conditional probability given its parents (and given any observed evidence).

2. **For each decision node, compute the action that maximizes expected utility.** For each possible action value, compute the expected utility of the utility node (and any downstream chance nodes) given that action, and pick the maximum.

3. **Sum the utilities of the utility nodes.** If there are multiple utility nodes (additive utility), add their values to get the total utility of the configuration.

> **Examiner's Note:** 3 marks for node types, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for the three steps.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the General Bellman Equation:**

For any state $s$ in an MDP, the optimal value $U^{*}(s)$ is:

$$U^{*}(s) = R(s) + \gamma \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s') \cdot U^{*}(s')$$

**Step 2 — Bellman Equation for $s_1$:**

At $s_1$, the actions are $R$ and $L$. Recall $R(s_1) = 0$.

- Action $R$: $T(s_1, R, s_2) = 0.9$, $T(s_1, R, s_1) = 0.1$
- Action $L$: $T(s_1, L, s_1) = 1.0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*}(s_1) &= R(s_1) + (0.9) \max \Big\{ \sum_{s'} T(s_1, R, s') U^{*}(s'),\; \sum_{s'} T(s_1, L, s') U^{*}(s') \Big\} \\
&= (0.9) \max \Big\{ 0.9 U^{*}(s_2) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_1),\; 1.0 \cdot U^{*}(s_1) \Big\} \\
&= (0.9) \max \Big\{ 0.9 U^{*}(s_2) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_1),\; U^{*}(s_1) \Big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bellman Equation for $s_2$:**

At $s_2$, $R(s_2) = 0$. Actions are $R$ and $L$.

- Action $R$: $T(s_2, R, s_3) = 0.9$, $T(s_2, R, s_2) = 0.1$
- Action $L$: $T(s_2, L, s_1) = 0.9$, $T(s_2, L, s_2) = 0.1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*}(s_2) &= R(s_2) + (0.9) \max \Big\{ \sum_{s'} T(s_2, R, s') U^{*}(s'),\; \sum_{s'} T(s_2, L, s') U^{*}(s') \Big\} \\
&= (0.9) \max \Big\{ 0.9 U^{*}(s_3) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_2),\; 0.9 U^{*}(s_1) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_2) \Big\} \\
&= (0.9) \max \Big\{ 0.9 U^{*}(s_3) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_2),\; 0.9 U^{*}(s_1) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_2) \Big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bellman Equation for $s_3$:**

At $s_3$, $R(s_3) = +10$. Actions are $R$ and $L$.

- Action $R$: $T(s_3, R, s_3) = 1.0$ (wall)
- Action $L$: $T(s_3, L, s_2) = 0.9$, $T(s_3, L, s_3) = 0.1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*}(s_3) &= R(s_3) + (0.9) \max \Big\{ \sum_{s'} T(s_3, R, s') U^{*}(s'),\; \sum_{s'} T(s_3, L, s') U^{*}(s') \Big\} \\
&= 10 + (0.9) \max \Big\{ 1.0 \cdot U^{*}(s_3),\; 0.9 U^{*}(s_2) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_3) \Big\} \\
&= 10 + (0.9) \max \Big\{ U^{*}(s_3),\; 0.9 U^{*}(s_2) + 0.1 U^{*}(s_3) \Big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Optimal Actions (Qualitative):**

| State | Optimal Action | Reasoning |
|-------|----------------|-----------|
| $s_1$ | **R** (Right) | Moving right gets closer to the goal $s_3$. Action $L$ just loops at $s_1$. |
| $s_2$ | **R** (Right) | Moving right reaches the goal $s_3$ (reward +10). Action $L$ moves away. |
| $s_3$ | **L** (Left) | Moving left is the only productive action — it leaves the terminal state and re-enters $s_2$, where future rewards are still possible. (If $s_3$ is *absorbing*, the optimal action is trivially "stay" with self-loop value 100.) |

> **Note on $s_3$ being terminal:** If $s_3$ is an absorbing goal state (the robot's mission ends at $s_3$), then $T(s_3, \cdot, s_3) = 1$ for all actions, and $U^{*}(s_3) = 10 + 0.9 \cdot U^{*}(s_3)$. Solving: $U^{*}(s_3) = 10 / 0.1 = 100$. This boundary value then propagates backward to $s_2$ and $s_1$.

> **Examiner's Note:** 2 marks for general Bellman equation, 3 marks for writing all three state equations, 2 marks for identifying optimal actions with reasoning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

### Definitions You Must Memorize
- **Utility $U(x)$:** A real-valued function mapping outcomes to preference scores.
- **Lottery $L$:** A probability distribution over outcomes, $L = \langle p_1 : x_1, \ldots, p_n : x_n \rangle$.
- **Expected Utility $EU(A)$:** The probability-weighted sum of utilities, $EU(A) = \sum_i p_i U(x_i)$.
- **MEU Principle:** A rational agent chooses $A^{*} = \arg\max_A EU(A)$.
- **Certainty Equivalent $

topics.title: Robotic Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robotic Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Robotic Perception

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotic Perception** is the sub-field of robotics and artificial intelligence concerned with enabling a robot to interpret, organize, and make sense of sensory data from its environment in order to navigate, recognize objects, localize itself, and execute intelligent tasks. It is the computational bridge between raw sensor signals (pixels, distances, forces, sounds) and high-level symbolic decision-making required for autonomous behaviour.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Outcome **CO4**: *Apply AI techniques for robot perception, planning, and decision making*), Robotic Perception encompasses four primary perception layers:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Hardware-level acquisition (cameras, LiDAR, IMU, tactile arrays).
2. **Signal Processing Layer** — Filtering, denoising, and feature extraction from raw signals.
3. **Interpretation Layer** — Object detection, classification, scene understanding, and 3D reconstruction.
4. **Decision Integration Layer** — Feeding perceived state to planners, controllers, and learning agents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim expectation):** *"Robotic perception is the process of organizing, identifying, and interpreting sensory information into meaningful representations of the robot's environment suitable for action."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Robot as a Blindfolded Child

Imagine teaching a **blindfolded child** to navigate a busy classroom. You give them:

- A **walking stick** (analogous to a tactile/force sensor)
- A **whispering guide** at their ear (analogous to a microphone/sound sensor)
- A **digital camera strapped to their forehead** that they can *review later* (analogous to a vision sensor with post-processing)
- A **sense of their own arm position** (proprioception, like joint encoders)

The child does **not** "see" the world the way we do. Instead, they integrate all these signals into a *mental model* of where desks are, where friends sit, and where the door lies. **Robotic Perception is exactly this — building and continuously updating an internal "mental model" from fragmented, noisy, multi-modal sensor data.** The robot uses AI algorithms (CNNs, SLAM, Bayesian filters) as the "learning brain" of the child.

## 1.3 The Perception-Action Loop in Robotics

The complete robotic cognitive cycle is the **Sense → Think → Act** loop. Perception is the *Sense* and the *interpretation of Think* stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (frequently asked in KTU viva):**
> - **Perception ≠ Sensing.** Sensing is *measurement*; Perception is *understanding*.
> - A thermometer senses temperature; a robot *perceives* that the room is hot enough to activate a fan.

## 1.4 Classification of Perception (High-Yield Taxonomy)

Robotic perception is broadly classified into three categories — **a KTU-favourite 2-mark question.**

| Class | Definition | Example Sensors | Example AI Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proprioception** | Sensing the robot's *own* internal state | Joint encoders, IMU, gyroscopes, motor current sensors | Forward kinematics, Kalman Filter on joint states |
| **Exteroception** | Sensing the *external* environment | Cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, depth sensors | CNNs, SLAM, PointNet |
| **Interoception** | Sensing internal *physiological* parameters | Battery sensors, temperature, current draw | Energy-aware AI scheduling |

## 1.5 Primary Perception Modalities (Industry-Relevant)

1. **Visual Perception (Computer Vision)** — RGB cameras, stereo vision, event cameras.
2. **Depth Perception** — LiDAR, structured light, time-of-flight (ToF).
3. **Tactile Perception** — Force/torque sensors, e-skin, capacitive arrays.
4. **Acoustic Perception** — Microphone arrays, ultrasonic sensors.
5. **Inertial Perception** — IMU, accelerometers, gyroscopes.
6. **Multi-modal Fusion** — Combining 2+ modalities for redundancy and robustness.

## 1.6 Perception Pipeline (Universal 5-Stage Architecture)

Every robotic perception system, from a vacuum cleaner to a self-driving car, follows this standardized pipeline:

$$\text{Raw Sensor Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Pre-processing}} \text{Clean Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Feature Extraction}} \text{Features} \xrightarrow{\text{Model}} \text{Semantic Labels} \xrightarrow{\text{Integration}} \text{World Model}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board's Favourite 3-Mark Question:** *"List and briefly explain the stages of a typical robotic perception pipeline."* — Memorize the 5-stage flow above.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematics of Visual Perception — Camera Projection Model

The foundational geometric model of a vision sensor is the **pinhole camera model**. It mathematically maps 3D world coordinates to 2D image coordinates — the basis of all robotic vision.

### 2.1.1 Intrinsic and Extrinsic Parameters

A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T$ in homogeneous coordinates is projected to a 2D image point $\mathbf{x} = (u, v, 1)^T$ via:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{K} \text{ (Intrinsic Matrix)}} \cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{[R}\,\vert\,\text{t] (Extrinsic Matrix)}} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $f_x, f_y$ = focal lengths in pixels (horizontal and vertical)
- $c_x, c_y$ = principal point (image center in pixels)
- $\gamma$ = skew coefficient (≈ 0 for modern cameras)
- $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ = rotation matrix (3×3)
- $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ = translation vector
- $s$ = arbitrary scale factor (depth)

### 2.1.2 Why This Matters in AI Robotics

- **Stereo Vision** uses two such cameras with known $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ to triangulate 3D depth.
- **Visual SLAM** recovers $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ from image sequences, *reversing* the projection.
- **Neural networks** (CNNs) implicitly learn projection-aware features when trained on perspective images.

## 2.2 The Mathematics of Depth — Stereo Triangulation

For two cameras separated by baseline $B$ with corresponding image points $x_L$ and $x_R$, depth $Z$ is computed via disparity:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}, \quad \text{where } d = x_L - x_R$$

| Variable | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | Depth of 3D point | metres (m) |
| $f$ | Focal length | pixels |
| $B$ | Stereo baseline | m |
| $d$ | Disparity | pixels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Depth is **inversely proportional** to disparity. As objects get closer, disparity increases, and depth precision improves.

## 2.3 LiDAR Perception — Range Measurement Equation

A LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor emits laser pulses and measures return time. The fundamental range equation:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

Where:
- $R$ = range to object (m)
- $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $\Delta t$ = round-trip time of flight (s)

The factor of 2 accounts for the **round-trip** travel of the laser pulse.

## 2.4 The Mathematics of Uncertainty — Probabilistic Perception

Real sensors are noisy. KTU 2024 emphasises probabilistic models. The central tool is **Bayes' Rule for Perception**:

$$P(\text{State} \mid \text{Observation}) = \frac{P(\text{Observation} \mid \text{State}) \cdot P(\text{State})}{P(\text{Observation})}$$

In robotics terminology:

$$\text{Posterior Belief} = \frac{\text{Measurement Likelihood} \times \text{Prior Belief}}{\text{Evidence (Normalization)}}$$

## 2.5 The Kalman Filter — Recursive State Estimation

The **Kalman Filter** is the workhorse of probabilistic perception. It estimates hidden state $\mathbf{x}_k$ from noisy observations $\mathbf{z}_k$.

### 2.5.1 Prediction Step (Time Update)

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$

### 2.5.2 Update Step (Measurement Update)

$$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1})$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Matrix Size |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{F}_k$ | State transition matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{B}_k$ | Control input matrix | $n \times m$ |
| $\mathbf{H}_k$ | Observation matrix | $p \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{Q}_k$ | Process noise covariance | $n \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{R}_k$ | Measurement noise covariance | $p \times p$ |
| $\mathbf{K}_k$ | Kalman Gain | $n \times p$ |
| $\mathbf{P}_k$ | State covariance | $n \times n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Mnemonic:** *"PREDICT (F, Q), then UPDATE (K, R)"* — Always the same order, always the same two stages.

## 2.6 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Non-Linear Perception

When perception involves non-linear mappings (e.g., camera projection), we linearize using Jacobians:

$$\mathbf{F}_k = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1}}, \quad \mathbf{H}_k = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}}$$

**EKF is the perception engine of Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO).**

## 2.7 SLAM — The Crown Jewel of Robotic Perception

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** answers two questions simultaneously:
- *Where am I?* (Localization)
- *What does the world look like?* (Mapping)

The SLAM problem is formally stated as estimating the joint posterior:

$$P(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$ = robot trajectory
- $\mathbf{m}$ = map
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ = sensor observations
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ = control inputs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLAM Variants (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **EKF-SLAM** — Probabilistic, single hypothesis
> - **FastSLAM (Particle Filter based)** — Rao-Blackwellized
> - **Graph-SLAM** — Pose graph optimization
> - **ORB-SLAM3, RTAB-Map, LIO-SAM** — Modern open-source implementations
> - **Neural SLAM / Deep SLAM** — End-to-end learned variants

## 2.8 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for Object Perception

A CNN processes an image $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ through layers of convolution, activation, and pooling. The convolution operation is:

$$(\mathbf{I} * \mathbf{K})_{i,j} = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{I}_{i+m, j+n} \cdot \mathbf{K}_{m,n}$$

Where $\mathbf{K}$ is the learned kernel. Modern perception stacks use:
- **YOLO, Faster R-CNN** for real-time object detection
- **U-Net, DeepLab** for semantic segmentation
- **ResNet, EfficientNet** as backbones
- **Vision Transformers (ViT)** for global context

## 2.9 Point Cloud Perception (3D Vision)

A point cloud is an unordered set $\mathcal{P} = \{p_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i) \in \mathbb{R}^3\}$. The canonical deep learning method is **PointNet**:

$$f(\{x_1, \dots, x_n\}) \approx g(h(x_1), \dots, h(x_n))$$

Where $h$ is a per-point MLP and $g$ is a **symmetric function** (e.g., max-pool) ensuring permutation invariance.

## 2.10 Sensor Fusion Formulation

Combining multiple sensors (e.g., camera + LiDAR + IMU) is formalized as a **Centralized Kalman Filter** with stacked observation model:

$$\mathbf{z}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_k^{cam} \\ \mathbf{z}_k^{lidar} \\ \mathbf{z}_k^{imu} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{H}_k^{cam} \\ \mathbf{H}_k^{lidar} \\ \mathbf{H}_k^{imu} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{R}_k = \text{diag}(\mathbf{R}_k^{cam}, \mathbf{R}_k^{lidar}, \mathbf{R}_k^{imu})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "diagonal trick" for fusion:** Assume inter-sensor noise independence, leading to a block-diagonal $\mathbf{R}_k$. This is a **guaranteed KTU Part B question pattern.**

## 2.11 Complete KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Equation | Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$ | Vision | Camera projection |
| 2 | $Z = fB/d$ | Stereo | Depth from disparity |
| 3 | $R = c\Delta t / 2$ | LiDAR | Range measurement |
| 4 | $P(H \vert E) = P(E \vert H) P(H) / P(E)$ | Probabilistic | Bayesian perception |
| 5 | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_k \mathbf{H}^T(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_k \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Estimation | Kalman gain |
| 6 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Estimation | State update |
| 7 | $\mathbf{F}_k = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x}$ | Non-linear | EKF linearization |
| 8 | $P(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$ | SLAM | Full SLAM posterior |
| 9 | $(\mathbf{I} * \mathbf{K})_{i,j} = \sum_{m,n} I_{i+m,j+n} K_{m,n}$ | Deep Learning | CNN convolution |
| 10 | $f(\{x_1, \dots, x_n\}) \approx g(\max_i h(x_i))$ | 3D Vision | PointNet aggregation |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Depth from Stereo Disparity (Board-Standard 14-Mark Derivation)

**Problem Statement:** Two cameras with focal length $f = 800$ pixels and baseline $B = 0.12$ m observe a 3D point. The left camera sees it at $u_L = 412$ px, the right camera at $u_R = 396$ px. Compute the depth $Z$ and the 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ if $v_L = v_R = 250$ px and the principal point is at $(c_x, c_y) = (400, 300)$ px.

### Step 1: Compute Disparity
Disparity is the *horizontal pixel difference* between the two corresponding points:

$$d = u_L - u_R = 412 - 396 = 16 \text{ pixels}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Depth-from-Disparity Formula
Substitute into the canonical stereo equation:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{96}{16} = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Convert Pixel Coordinates to Normalized Camera Coordinates
We must subtract the principal point to remove optical-axis offset:

$$x_{\text{norm}} = u_L - c_x = 412 - 400 = 12 \text{ px}$$

$$y_{\text{norm}} = v_L - c_y = 250 - 300 = -50 \text{ px}$$

### Step 4: Recover 3D World Coordinates
For rectified stereo (rotation = identity), the deprojection is:

$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{12 \times 6.0}{800} = 0.090 \text{ m}$$

$$Y = \frac{(v_L - c_y) \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{-50 \times 6.0}{800} = -0.375 \text{ m}$$

$$Z = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5: Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{3D Point} = (0.090 \text{ m}, \ -0.375 \text{ m}, \ 6.0 \text{ m})}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check via Disparity-Z Inverse Relation
Doubling $d$ should halve $Z$. If $d = 32$, then $Z = 96/32 = 3.0$ m. ✓ (Confirms inverse proportionality.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Trick:** Many students write $(X, Y, Z) = (412, 250, 6.0)$ — using raw pixel coordinates. **Always subtract $c_x, c_y$ first.** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this slip.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Kalman Filter 1D Position-Tracking Example

**Problem Statement:** A robot moves in 1D with constant velocity $v = 1$ m/s. State vector $\mathbf{x} = [p, v]^T$ (position, velocity). Sample time $dt = 1$ s. Measurement model: $z_k = p_k + w_k$ with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.25)$. Process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q} = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01)$. Initial state: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $\mathbf{P}_0 = \text{diag}(1, 1)$. Compute the posterior after $k=1$ given measurement $z_1 = 0.9$ m.

### Step 1: Define the State Transition and Observation Models

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & dt \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Prediction — State Mean
Apply $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 0} = \mathbf{F} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{0 \vert 0}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Prediction — State Covariance
Apply $\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{P}_{0 \vert 0} \mathbf{F}^T + \mathbf{Q}$:

$$\mathbf{F} \mathbf{P}_{0 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{F} \mathbf{P}_{0 \vert 0} \mathbf{F}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Innovation (Measurement Residual)
Compute $\mathbf{y}_1 = z_1 - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 0}$:

$$\mathbf{y}_1 = 0.9 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5: Innovation Covariance
Compute $\mathbf{S}_1 = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} \mathbf{H}^T + R$:

$$\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} \mathbf{H}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 2.01$$

$$\mathbf{S}_1 = 2.01 + 0.25 = 2.26$$

### Step 6: Kalman Gain
Compute $\mathbf{K}_1 = \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{S}_1^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{K}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 \\ 1.00 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.26} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.889 \\ 0.442 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 7: Posterior State Update
Apply $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 1} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 0} + \mathbf{K}_1 \mathbf{y}_1$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.889 \\ 0.442 \end{bmatrix} \times 0.9 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.800 \\ 0.398 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 8: Posterior Covariance (Joseph form for numerical stability)
Apply $\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 1} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_1 \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0}$:

$$\mathbf{K}_1 \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.889 \\ 0.442 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.889 & 0 \\ 0.442 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_1 \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.111 & 0 \\ -0.442 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.111 & 0 \\ -0.442 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.223 & 0.111 \\ 0.111 & 0.568 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 9: Final Posterior Belief

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{1 \vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.800 \text{ m} \\ 0.398 \text{ m/s} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.223 & 0.111 \\ 0.111 & 0.568 \end{bmatrix}}$$

### Step 10: Interpretation
The robot believes it is at **0.800 m** with velocity **0.398 m/s** — note the velocity *decreased* from the prediction of 0 because the measurement $0.9$ is below the predicted $0 + 1 \times 1 = 1$ m. The Kalman filter optimally weighted model and measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in AI robotics:** This is the **exact** algorithm running in every self-driving car's localization module, every drone's INS, and every smartphone AR/VR system — thousands of times per second.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Camera Projection of a Known 3D Point

**Problem Statement:** Given a 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (2, 3, 10)^T$ m, with intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 500 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and an identity rotation/translation (i.e., camera coincides with world frame), find the image pixel $(u, v)$.

### Step 1: Construct Homogeneous World Point
$$\mathbf{X}_h = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Apply Extrinsic (Identity)
$$\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{I}_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{output} = \mathbf{X}_h$$

### Step 3: Apply Intrinsic Matrix
$$\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{X}_{\text{cam}} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 0 & 320 & 0 \\ 0 & 500 & 240 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 \cdot 2 + 320 \cdot 10 \\ 500 \cdot 3 + 240 \cdot 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4200 \\ 3900 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Perspective Divide (Homogenize)
$$s = 10, \quad u = 4200 / 10 = 420 \text{ px}, \quad v = 3900 / 10 = 390 \text{ px}$$

### Step 5: Final Image Point

$$\boxed{(u, v) = (420, 390) \text{ pixels}}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Perception Algorithms at a Glance)

| Algorithm | State Type | Linearity | Computational Cost | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kalman Filter (KF)** | Continuous, unimodal Gaussian | Linear | $O(n^3)$ per step | Linear dynamics, GPS + IMU | Fails with non-Gaussian noise |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Continuous, unimodal Gaussian | Linearized | $O(n^3)$ per step | Camera/IMU fusion (VIO) | Linearization errors |
| **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** | Continuous, unimodal Gaussian | Sigma-point | $O(n^3)$ per step | Highly non-linear systems | Tuning of sigma points |
| **Particle Filter (FastSLAM)** | Multi-hypothesis | Any | $O(NM)$ with $N$ particles | Global localization | Particle degeneracy |
| **Graph SLAM (Pose Graph)** | Sparse graph | Non-linear least squares | $O(n^2)$ with sparse solvers | Large-scale offline mapping | Loop-closure dependent |
| **CNN (YOLO/R-CNN)** | Learned features | Learned | GPU-intensive | Object detection | Requires large labeled data |
| **PointNet/PointNet++** | Point cloud | Learned | GPU-intensive | 3D object classification | High memory for dense clouds |
| **NeRF / 3D Gaussian Splatting** | Implicit/Explicit 3D | Learned | GPU-intensive | Photorealistic 3D reconstruction | Slow training, no semantic info |
| **Transformer-based (DETR, ViT)** | Attention-based | Learned | GPU-intensive | Global context understanding | Quadratic attention cost |
