# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between proprioception and exteroception in robotic perception. Give one example sensor for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioception | Exteroception |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sensing the robot's *own* internal state | Sensing the *external* environment |
| **What is measured** | Joint angles, velocity, battery, motor current | Objects, distances, light, sound |
| **Example sensor** | Rotary encoder on motor shaft | RGB camera, LiDAR |
| **AI technique** | Forward/inverse kinematics, EKF on joint state | Object detection CNN, SLAM |
| **Use case** | Know if arm is at 45° or 90° | Know if a person is in front of robot |

> Proprioception = "How am *I* doing?"; Exteroception = "What's *out there*?" — both feed the same world model.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List the five stages of a generic robotic perception pipeline.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Sensing / Data Acquisition** — Raw data capture (camera, LiDAR, IMU).
2. **Pre-processing** — Denoising, calibration, normalization.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Edges, keypoints, descriptors (SIFT, ORB).
4. **Perception / Interpretation** — Object detection, classification, SLAM.
5. **World Model Integration** — Updating belief state, occupancy grid, semantic map for downstream planning.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Full Solutions)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the pinhole camera projection model with the intrinsic and extrinsic parameter matrices. Derive the equation mapping a 3D world point to a 2D image point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two cameras with focal length $f = 600$ pixels, baseline $B = 0.10$ m, and principal point $(c_x, c_y) = (320, 240)$ px observe a 3D point with image coordinates $(u_L, v_L) = (380, 260)$ and $(u_R, v_R) = (368, 260)$. Compute the depth and 3D world coordinates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The **pinhole camera model** idealizes the camera as a point aperture that projects 3D light rays onto a 2D image plane. The mapping is:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \cdot [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{K}$ is the **intrinsic matrix** (describes the camera's internal optics)
- $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **extrinsic matrix** (describes camera's pose in the world)

The intrinsic matrix:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $f_x, f_y$ are focal lengths in pixel units, $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point, and $\gamma$ is the axis skew (≈ 0 in modern cameras).

The extrinsic matrix transforms world coordinates to camera coordinates:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $\mathbf{R}$ is a 3×3 rotation matrix and $\mathbf{t}$ is a 3×1 translation vector.

The final 2D image point is recovered via perspective division by the depth $Z_c$:

$$u = \frac{f_x X_c + c_x Z_c}{Z_c}, \quad v = \frac{f_y Y_c + c_y Z_c}{Z_c}$$

> This model underpins stereo vision, structure-from-motion, visual odometry, and AR/VR.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1: Compute disparity.**

$$d = u_L - u_R = 380 - 368 = 12 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2: Compute depth using the stereo formula.**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{600 \times 0.10}{12} = \frac{60}{12} = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute normalized pixel coordinates.**

$$x_n = u_L - c_x = 380 - 320 = 60 \text{ px}$$

$$y_n = v_L - c_y = 260 - 240 = 20 \text{ px}$$

**Step 4: Recover 3D world coordinates.**

$$X = \frac{x_n \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{60 \times 5.0}{600} = 0.50 \text{ m}$$

$$Y = \frac{y_n \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{20 \times 5.0}{600} = 0.167 \text{ m}$$

$$Z = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{3D Point} = (0.50 \text{ m}, \ 0.167 \text{ m}, \ 5.0 \text{ m})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a)** What is SLAM? Explain the EKF-SLAM algorithm with its prediction and update steps. State the SLAM posterior distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1D Kalman filter tracks a robot's position. State vector is $\mathbf{x} = [p]^T$, with state transition $\mathbf{F} = [1]$, process noise $\mathbf{Q} = 0.1$, initial $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $\mathbf{P}_0 = 1$, and measurement $z_1 = 1.2$ with observation model $\mathbf{H} = [1]$ and measurement noise $R = 0.5$. Compute the posterior after one update. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Definition of SLAM:**
SLAM (**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping**) is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within it. It is the **core perceptual challenge** of autonomous mobile robots.

**SLAM Posterior Distribution (Full SLAM formulation):**

$$P(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$ = entire robot trajectory from time 1 to $t$
- $\mathbf{m}$ = map (set of landmark positions)
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ = history of sensor observations
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ = history of control inputs

**EKF-SLAM Algorithm — Prediction Step:**
The robot moves according to control $\mathbf{u}_k$. The predicted state mean and covariance are:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$

**EKF-SLAM Algorithm — Update Step:**
On receiving observation $\mathbf{z}_k$ of landmark $j$:

$$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_j^T (\mathbf{H}_j \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_j^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}))$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_j) \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}$$

The measurement function $h(\mathbf{x})$ is non-linear (range and bearing), so its Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_j = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ is used.

> **Limitation:** EKF-SLAM scales as $O(n^2)$ in map size, making it unsuitable for very large maps.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1: Prediction — State Mean.**

$$\hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = \mathbf{F} \hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} = 1 \times 0 = 0$$

**Step 2: Prediction — State Covariance.**

$$\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{P}_{0 \vert 0} \mathbf{F}^T + \mathbf{Q} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

**Step 3: Innovation (Measurement Residual).**

$$y_1 = z_1 - \mathbf{H} \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = 1.2 - 1 \times 0 = 1.2$$

**Step 4: Innovation Covariance.**

$$S_1 = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} \mathbf{H}^T + R = 1 \times 1.1 \times 1 + 0.5 = 1.6$$

**Step 5: Kalman Gain.**

$$K_1 = \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} \mathbf{H}^T S_1^{-1} = 1.1 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{1.6} = 0.6875$$

**Step 6: Posterior State Update.**

$$\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} = \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} + K_1 y_1 = 0 + 0.6875 \times 1.2 = 0.825$$

**Step 7: Posterior Covariance Update.**

$$\mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 1} = (1 - K_1 \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 0} = (1 - 0.6875) \times 1.1 = 0.34375$$

**Step 8: Final Posterior Belief.**

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} = 0.825, \quad \mathbf{P}_{1 \vert 1} = 0.34375}$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot's position belief is 0.825 (a weighted average between prediction 0 and measurement 1.2), and uncertainty dropped from 1.1 → 0.34375 after incorporating the measurement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the perspective divide** in the camera model — dividing by $Z_c$ is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing KF update order** — always Predict → Innovation → Gain → Update → Covariance update.
> 3. **Using raw pixel coordinates in stereo math** — must subtract $c_x, c_y$ first (costs 1–2 marks).
> 4. **Mixing $u_L - u_R$ direction** — convention is $d = u_L - u_R > 0$ for objects in front.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the LiDAR range equation $R = c\Delta t / 2$.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer — KTU deducts ½ mark for unit-less numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- **Robotic Perception** is the *interpretation* of sensor data into meaningful world representations — **not** mere sensing.
- **Three perception classes:** Proprioception (self), Exteroception (environment), Interoception (internal state).
- **Perception Pipeline (5 stages):** Sense → Pre-process → Extract Features → Interpret → Update World Model.
- **Pinhole Camera Model:** $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}$ — memorize both matrices and the perspective divide.
- **Stereo Depth Formula:** $Z = fB/d$ — depth is *inversely* proportional to disparity.
- **LiDAR Range:** $R = c \Delta t / 2$ — the factor of 2 is the round-trip.
- **Bayes' Rule:** $P(\text{State} \mid \text{Obs}) = P(\text{Obs} \mid \text{State}) P(\text{State}) / P(\text{Obs})$.
- **Kalman Filter Order:** Predict (mean, covariance) → Innovation → Kalman Gain → Update (state, covariance).
- **EKF:** Used when observation/dynamics are non-linear; uses Jacobians $\mathbf{F} = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{H} = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$.
- **SLAM Posterior:** $P(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$ — the joint posterior over trajectory and map.
- **SLAM Variants:** EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM (Particle Filter), Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3, Neural SLAM.
- **CNN for Perception:** Convolution, ReLU, Pooling, Fully Connected, Softmax — YOLO/R-CNN for detection, U-Net for segmentation.
- **PointNet Aggregation:** Symmetric function (max-pool) ensures **permutation invariance** of point cloud input.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Centralized EKF with stacked $\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{R}$ — assume block-diagonal $\mathbf{R}$ if sensors are independent.
- **Industry Use Cases:** Self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla), warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva), surgical robots (da Vinci), drones (Skydio), AR/VR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest).
- **Common Pitfalls:** Forgetting principal-point offset, omitting factor of 2 in LiDAR, wrong KF update order, missing units.
- **KTU CO4 Mapping:** This module directly satisfies *CO4: Apply AI techniques for robot perception, planning, and decision making* — map every answer back to this CO.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
