<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process by which a robot determines its **pose** (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) within a known reference frame, using on-board sensor measurements, a known environment map, and probabilistic inference.

Mathematically, localization seeks the posterior probability distribution of the robot's state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all available sensor data $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This is also called the **belief state** of the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> The state space of a planar mobile robot in 2D is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$, i.e., a 3-dimensional **SE(2)** manifold. For a 6-DoF aerial/manipulator robot, it becomes the 6-dimensional **SE(3)** manifold.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you wake up in an unfamiliar hotel room in a new city, with your phone switched off. You need to figure out **"Where am I?"** You have two clues:

1. **A memory of how you walked** (e.g., "I went down 5 corridors then turned right") — this is the **control input** $u_t$.
2. **Visual landmarks** (the lobby logo, the room numbers) — this is the **sensor measurement** $z_t$.

By fusing these two noisy pieces of information recursively, you narrow down your location to one specific room. **That fusion process is localization.**

A robot does the same thing:
- **Odometry** (wheel rotations, IMU) → predicts where it should be.
- **External sensors** (LIDAR, camera, GPS, beacons) → corrects that prediction by matching landmarks to a known map.

---

## 1.3 Why Localization is Hard

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Sensor Noise** | Wheel encoders drift; LIDAR beams have Gaussian errors. |
| **Perceptual Aliasing** | Identical-looking corridors confuse the robot. |
| **Continuous State Space** | Pose is a continuous variable — cannot be enumerated. |
| **Computational Cost** | Maintaining a full posterior over a continuous space is intractable. |
| **The Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Sudden teleportation invalidates the prior belief entirely. |

---

## 1.4 The Three Localization Sub-Problems (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the classification below — it is a **3-mark definition question** in most years.

1. **Position Tracking (Local Localization):** The initial pose is known. The robot's job is to compensate for incremental odometric drift.
   - $bel(x_0)$ is a sharp unimodal distribution.
2. **Global Localization:** The robot has no idea where it is. The belief is a uniform distribution over the entire free space.
3. **Kidnapped Robot Problem:** The robot is abruptly placed in a new location. The algorithm must recover — this is the **gold standard** for global localization robustness.

---

## 1.5 Probabilistic Foundation (Why Bayes?)

Because both **actuator noise** and **sensor noise** corrupt our data, we cannot use deterministic reasoning. We instead maintain a **probability distribution** over the state space. The **Bayes filter** is the universal recursive estimator that underpins *all* localization methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Tenet:** *Markov Assumption* — the current state $x_t$ is sufficient to predict the next state and the next measurement, i.e., $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ and $p(z_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter — Universal Localization Backbone

The Bayes filter recursively estimates $bel(x_t)$ in two alternating steps:

### Step 1: Prediction (using control $u_t$)

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

- $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is the **motion model** (state transition probability).
- This propagates the prior belief forward in time using odometry.

### Step 2: Measurement Update (using sensor $z_t$)

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

- $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ is the **sensor (observation) model**.
- $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring $bel(x_t)$ sums to **1**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, the normalizer is **frequently** asked. Always write:
> $$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)\, dx_t\right]^{-1}$$

---

## 2.2 Major Localization Paradigms

### 2.2.1 Markov Localization (Grid-Based)
- Discretizes the workspace into a **uniform grid** of cells.
- Maintains a discrete probability for each cell: $p(x_t = \text{cell}_i \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **Pros:** Can represent **multi-modal** beliefs (great for global localization).
- **Cons:** $O(n)$ memory and compute per step, where $n$ is the number of cells.
- **Resolution Trade-off:** High resolution → accurate but expensive; low resolution → coarse but fast.

### 2.2.2 Kalman Filter Localization (EKF)
- Assumes the posterior is **unimodal Gaussian**: $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- $\mu_t$ is the mean pose; $\Sigma_t$ is the $3 \times 3$ (or $6 \times 6$) covariance.
- **Pros:** Extremely efficient — constant memory, closed-form updates.
- **Cons:** **Cannot** represent multi-modal beliefs → fails under global localization ambiguity.
- **EKF Linearization:** Both the motion and sensor models are linearized via first-order Taylor expansion around $\mu_{t-1}$.

### 2.2.3 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)
- Represents $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles): $\{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Pros:** Handles non-Gaussian, multi-modal beliefs; works in complex maps.
- **Cons:** Computational cost grows with $N$; sample impoverishment possible.
- **Algorithm:** Predict → Weight (using sensor model) → Resample (eliminate low-weight particles).

### 2.2.4 Comparison Table (KTU-Mandated)

| Property | Markov (Grid) | EKF | Monte Carlo (Particle) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Belief Type** | Discrete grid | Unimodal Gaussian | Sample set |
| **Multi-modal?** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Memory** | $O(n)$ cells | $O(d^2)$ (covariance) | $O(N)$ particles |
| **Global Localization?** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Kidnapped Robot?** | Good (with resampling) | Fails | Excellent |

---

## 2.3 Motion and Sensor Models (KTU Standard)

### Motion Model
For a differential-drive robot executing odometry $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ in the robot's local frame:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \cos\theta_{t-1} - \Delta y \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \Delta x \sin\theta_{t-1} + \Delta y \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix} + \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

where $R$ is the **$3 \times 3$ motion noise covariance** (typically diagonal with $\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2$).

### Beam Sensor Model (LIDAR)
For a sensor returning range $z_t^k$ to landmark $k$ from expected pose $x_t$:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathcal{N}(z_t^k; \hat{z}^k(x_t, m), Q_{sensor})$$

where:
- $m$ is the map.
- $\hat{z}^k(x_t, m)$ is the predicted range based on ray-casting.
- $Q_{sensor}$ is the sensor noise covariance.

If an unexpected obstacle is hit (unmodeled object), a uniform component is added to handle the "max-range" case.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula required to solve any Module-4 localization problem on the KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols and the form — partial credit depends on it.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Belief State | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | $bel \in [0,1]$, $\sum_x bel(x) = 1$ |
| Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^3$ for planar pose |
| Bayes Filter Update | $bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ is the normalizer |
| Normalizer | $\eta = \left[\sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t) \overline{bel}(x_t)\right]^{-1}$ | For discrete case |
| EKF Mean Prediction | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | $g$ = nonlinear motion model |
| EKF Covariance Prediction | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | $G_t = \partial g / \partial x \vert_{\mu_{t-1},u_t}$ |
| EKF Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $H_t = \partial h / \partial x \vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ |
| EKF Mean Update | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | $h$ = sensor model |
| EKF Covariance Update | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Dimension of $I$ matches $x$ |
| Particle Resampling | $w_t^{(i)} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$ |
| Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | Threshold typically $N/2$ |
| Sensor Range Model | $p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathcal{N}(z_t^k; \hat{z}^k, \sigma^2)$ | $z$ in metres, $\sigma$ in metres |
| Motion Noise Covariance | $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ in m, $\sigma_\theta$ in rad |
| Grid Resolution Trade-off | $N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{X \cdot Y}{\delta^2}$ | $\delta$ = cell edge length |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | GPS + IMU + LIDAR fusion for self-driving cars (e.g., Google Waymo, Tesla FSD). |
| Warehouse Robotics | AMRs in Amazon fulfillment centers use Monte Carlo localization on static maps. |
| Aerial Drones | Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) with EKF for GPS-denied indoor flight. |
| Underwater ROVs | Particle filter with terrain-aiding sonar maps for seabed navigation. |
| Domestic Vacuum Robots | Roomba uses a coarse grid-based Markov localization on its floor map. |
| Planetary Rovers | NASA's Mars rovers use EKF with visual landmarks (Hazcam). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Recursive Bayes Filter

We wish to compute $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Begin with the definition of conditional probability:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Markov Assumption to the measurement model:**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

This is valid because the current pose $x_t$ is a sufficient statistic for the current measurement.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption to the prediction model:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **Chapman-Kolmogorov** equation, marginalizing over all possible previous states.

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the numerator:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

where $\eta = p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})^{-1}$ is the normalizer. This is the **full Bayes filter**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: EKF Localization Update

### Setup
- State vector: $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^3$.
- Motion model: $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \epsilon_t$, $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.
- Sensor model: $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.
- Belief at $t-1$: $bel(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

### Prediction Step

**Step 1 — Linearize the motion model around $\mu_{t-1}$:**

$$G_t = \left.\frac{\partial g(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}}\right|_{x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}}$$

For a differential-drive robot with odometry $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$:

$$G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta x \sin\mu_{t-1,\theta} - \Delta y \cos\mu_{t-1,\theta} \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta x \cos\mu_{t-1,\theta} - \Delta y \sin\mu_{t-1,\theta} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Propagate the mean:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 3 — Propagate the covariance (first-order Taylor expansion of $g$):**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$$

### Update Step

**Step 1 — Linearize the sensor model around $\bar{\mu}_t$:**

$$H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t}\right|_{x_t = \bar{\mu}_t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Kalman Gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top \left( H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t \right)^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Update the mean using the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

**Step 4 — Update the covariance:**

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **innovation** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ represents the discrepancy between what the sensor actually saw and what the prediction expected. It is the **driving force** of every correction step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example (EKF 1D Range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **canonical KTU-style 14-mark problem**. Master the structure; the marker looks for these exact sub-steps.

**Problem:** A planar robot with initial belief $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 1$ m² moves with odometry $u_1 = 0.5$ m (variance $R_1 = 0.1$ m²). It then observes a beacon at known location $L = 1.2$ m with range $z_1 = 0.9$ m. Sensor variance $Q_1 = 0.2$ m². Sensor model: $h(x) = L - x$. Compute $bel(x_1)$.

**Sub-step (a) — Prediction:**

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1 \text{ m}^2$$

**Sub-step (b) — Linearization of sensor model:**

$$H_1 = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg|_{\bar{\mu}_1} = \frac{\partial (L-x)}{\partial x}\bigg|_{0.5} = -1$$

**Sub-step (c) — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 H_1^\top (H_1 \bar{\Sigma}_1 H_1^\top + Q_1)^{-1}$$

$$K_1 = 1.1 \cdot (-1) \cdot \left( (-1)(1.1)(-1) + 0.2 \right)^{-1}$$

$$K_1 = -1.1 \cdot (1.1 + 0.2)^{-1} = -1.1 \cdot (1.3)^{-1} = -0.846$$

**Sub-step (d) — Innovation:**

$$\nu_1 = z_1 - h(\bar{\mu}_1) = 0.9 - (1.2 - 0.5) = 0.9 - 0.7 = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Sub-step (e) — Updated mean:**

$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 0.5 + (-0.846)(0.2) = 0.5 - 0.1692 = 0.3308 \text{ m}$$

**Sub-step (f) — Updated covariance:**

$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H_1) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - (-0.846)(-1))(1.1) = (1 - 0.846)(1.1)$$

$$\Sigma_1 = (0.154)(1.1) = 0.1694 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final belief:**

$$bel(x_1) = \mathcal{N}(0.3308,\, 0.1694)$$

The covariance has dropped from **1.1 m² → 0.1694 m²** — an 85% reduction in uncertainty after a single measurement. **This is the entire purpose of the measurement update.**

---

## 3.4 Monte Carlo Particle Filter Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks for the algorithmic steps of MCL (Monte Carlo Localization). Memorize this list verbatim.

1. **Initialization:** Draw $N$ particles $\{x_0^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from the prior $p(x_0)$ (uniform if global).
2. **For each time step $t$:**
   - **Prediction:** Sample $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$ (add noise to odometry for each particle).
   - **Importance Weighting:** Compute $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$.
   - **Normalization:** $w_t^{(i)} \leftarrow w_t^{(i)} / \sum_j w_t^{(j)}$.
   - **Resampling:** Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, with probability proportional to $w_t^{(i)}$.
3. **Output:** The estimated pose is the weighted mean (or MAP).

**Effective Sample Size criterion** — if $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2 < N/2$, resample; otherwise skip to save compute.

---

## 3.5 Key Analytical Relationships

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ | History irrelevant |
| Belief Recursion | $bel_t = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) bel_{t-1} dx_{t-1}$ | Core update |
| Posterior Multi-modality | $bel(x_t)$ has $>1$ peak | Cannot use EKF |
| Sensor vs. Motion Trust | $\frac{Q_t}{R_t}$ small | Trust sensor more |
| Kalman Gain Limit | $K_t \to 0$ as $Q_t \to \infty$ | No info from bad sensor |
| Kalman Gain Limit | $K_t \to H^{-1}$ as $Q_t \to 0$ | Perfect sensor recovery |
| Particle Convergence | $N \to \infty \Rightarrow$ | MCL → exact posterior |
| Resampling Necessity | $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$ | Degeneracy, resample |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Localization Module

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop data flow** of a generic Bayesian localization system, mapping interactions between the robot, its sensors, its actuators, the map, and the inference engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel_x0] --> B[Belief State bel_xt]
    B --> C{Motion Update using ut}
    C --> D[Predicted Belief Prior belbar_xt]
    D --> E[Sensor Measurement zt]
    E --> F[Measurement Model p_zt_xt_m]
    F --> G[Posterior Calculation eta times p_zt_xt times belbar_xt]
    G --> H[Updated Belief bel_xt]
    H --> I{Is bel concentrated}
    I -- Yes --> J[Output Pose mu_t]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Iterating]
    K --> C
    J --> L[Robot Actuator Command]
    L --> M[Robot Motion in World]
    M --> N[Next Time Step]
    N --> C
    A -.Map m.-> F
    A -.Odometry ut.-> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The belief $bel(x_t)$ is the central data object.
- A **prediction** cycle is driven by the control $u_t$ (odometry).
- A **correction** cycle is driven by the measurement $z_t$.
- The loop closes via the physical motion of the robot, which yields new sensor data.

---

## 4.2 Diagram: Particle Filter Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **three stages** of a Monte Carlo localization update: prediction, weighting, and resampling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Previous Particles xt-1] --> P2[Add Odometry Noise]
    P2 --> P3[Predicted Particles xt]
    P3 --> W1[For Each Particle]
    W1 --> W2[Compute Sensor Likelihood p_zt_xt_m]
    W2 --> W3[Assign Weight wt]
    W3 --> W4[Normalize Weights]
    W4 --> R1{Compute Neff}
    R1 -- Neff less than N over 2 --> R2[Resample with Replacement]
    R1 -- Neff greater than N over 2 --> R3[Keep Particles]
    R2 --> O1[Output New Particle Set]
    R3 --> O1
    O1 --> P1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Particles are first **propagated** forward with noisy odometry (prediction).
- Then each particle is **scored** based on how well its predicted sensor reading matches the actual one (weighting).
- Particles with low weight are **eliminated** in the resampling step, focusing the sample set on likely regions of the state space.

---

## 4.3 Diagram: EKF Localization Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Previous Estimate mu_t-1 Sigma_t-1] --> B[Linearize Motion Model get Gt]
    B --> C[Predict Mean barmu_t = g mu_t-1 ut]
    B --> D[Predict Covariance barSigma_t = Gt Sigma_t-1 Gt transpose + Rt]
    C --> E[Linearize Sensor Model get Ht]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
    F --> G[Innovation nut = zt - h barmu_t]
    G --> H[Update Mean mu_t = barmu_t + Kt nut]
    F --> I[Update Covariance Sigma_t = I - Kt Ht barSigma_t]
    H --> J[Output EKF Pose Estimate]
    I --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The motion and sensor Jacobians ($G_t$ and $H_t$) are the **linearization points** that make the EKF possible.
- The Kalman Gain $K_t$ is the **optimal linear blending factor** between prediction and measurement.
- The final output is the mean and covariance of the Gaussian belief at time $t$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBRT525]`

### Q1. Define localization in mobile robotics. Differentiate between local and global localization.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process of estimating a robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ relative to a known map $m$, using sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$. Mathematically, it computes the belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
>
> **Local localization (position tracking):** The initial pose is known, and the algorithm only has to track incremental motion. Belief is unimodal.
>
> **Global localization:** The initial pose is unknown, and the belief starts uniform over the entire free space. The algorithm must determine the pose from scratch.
>
> The **kidnapped robot problem** is the harder variant where the robot is suddenly teleported during operation.

---

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBRT525]`

### Q2. State the Bayes filter update equation and explain each term in the context of robot localization.

**Model Answer:**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

- $bel(x_t)$: Posterior belief over current pose.
- $p(z_t \mid x_t)$: **Sensor model** — likelihood of measurement given the pose.
- $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$: **Motion model** — probability of transition between poses given the control.
- $bel(x_{t-1})$: Previous belief (prior).
- $\eta$: **Normalizer** ensuring the posterior sums to one.
- The integral is the **prediction** step (Chapman-Kolmogorov).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBRT525, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **Bayes filter** algorithm for robot localization starting from the definition of conditional probability. Clearly state any assumptions used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A mobile robot has belief at $t-1$ as a Gaussian with $\mu_{t-1} = 0$ and $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0.5$. The motion model is $g(x, u) = x + u$ with $u = 0.8$ and motion noise variance $R = 0.1$. The sensor model is $h(x) = 2x$ and the measurement is $z = 1.5$ with sensor noise variance $Q = 0.3$. Compute the EKF update at time $t$ showing all intermediate values. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Begin with the target posterior:**

We want $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. By definition of conditional probability:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Markov Assumption to the measurement:**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

The current pose is a sufficient statistic for the current measurement (history-independence).

**Step 3 — Expand the prediction term using the law of total probability:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Markov Assumption to the motion model:**

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and identify the normalizer:**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

where $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$. This completes the derivation. (7/7)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — EKF Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\mu_{t-1} = 0$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0.5$, $u = 0.8$, $R = 0.1$, $h(x) = 2x$, $z = 1.5$, $Q = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Predicted mean:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u) = 0 + 0.8 = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Linearization of $g$:**

$$G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Predicted covariance:**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t^2 \Sigma_{t-1} + R = (1)^2 (0.5) + 0.1 = 0.6$$

**Step 4 — Linearization of $h$:**

$$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = 2$$

**Step 5 — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t (H_t^2 \bar{\Sigma}_t + Q)^{-1} = (0.6)(2) \left[ (2)^2 (0.6) + 0.3 \right]^{-1}$$

$$K_t = 1.2 \cdot (2.4 + 0.3)^{-1} = 1.2 / 2.7 = 0.444$$

**Step 6 — Predicted measurement:**

$$h(\bar{\mu}_t) = 2 \times 0.8 = 1.6$$

**Step 7 — Innovation:**

$$\nu_t = z - h(\bar{\mu}_t) = 1.5 - 1.6 = -0.1$$

**Step 8 — Updated mean:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = 0.8 + (0.444)(-0.1) = 0.8 - 0.0444 = 0.7556$$

**Step 9 — Updated covariance:**

$$\Sigma_t = (1 - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t = (1 - 0.444 \times 2)(0.6) = (1 - 0.888)(0.6)$$

$$\Sigma_t = 0.112 \times 0.6 = 0.0672$$

**Final belief:** $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(0.7556, 0.0672)$. (7/7)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** localization algorithm in detail. State the prediction and update equations along with the role of Jacobians. Discuss its limitations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat algorithm, explain **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)**. How does it solve the global localization and the kidnapped robot problem? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — EKF (7 Marks)

The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** is the application of the Kalman filter to nonlinear systems. It maintains a Gaussian belief $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ over the robot pose.

**Prediction Step:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$$

where $G_t = \partial g / \partial x \vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ is the **Jacobian of the motion model**.

**Update Step:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

where $H_t = \partial h / \partial x \vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is the **Jacobian of the sensor model**.

**Role of Jacobians:** They provide **first-order linearization** of the nonlinear functions around the current belief, allowing the use of the linear Kalman update equations. Without them, the system would be analytically intractable.

**Limitations:**
1. **Unimodal assumption** — fails for global localization (multi-modal belief).
2. **Linearization errors** — large $\Sigma$ produces poor Jacobian approximations.
3. **Computational cost of Jacobians** — symbolic or numerical derivatives required.
4. **Cannot handle discrete hypotheses** (e.g., a robot in front of two identical doors).
5. **Sensitive to data association errors** — wrong landmark matching causes divergence. (7/7)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — MCL (7 Marks)

**Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** is a sample-based implementation of the Bayes filter, using a set of weighted particles to represent the belief.

**Algorithm:**
1. **Initialize:** Draw $N$ particles $\{x_0^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from the prior $p(x_0)$ (uniform for global).
2. **For each time step $t$:**
   - **Motion Update:** Sample $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$.
   - **Measurement Update:** Compute importance weight $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$.
   - **Normalize:** $w_t^{(i)} \leftarrow w_t^{(i)} / \sum_j w_t^{(j)}$.
   - **Resample:** Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, probability $\propto w_t^{(i)}$.
3. **Output:** Mean pose $\mu_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i x_t^{(i)}$.

**Solving Global Localization:** Initial particles are spread **uniformly** over the entire free space. The sensor likelihood concentrates weights around true pose regions, and resampling eliminates wrong regions.

**Solving the Kidnapped Robot Problem:** Random particles are **injected** at each resampling step (e.g., 5% of $N$ drawn from a uniform prior). This guarantees that the particle set cannot collapse — if the robot is teleported, the random injections will eventually find the new pose, and resampling will once again concentrate around it. This is the **core robustness mechanism** that distinguishes MCL from a naive particle filter. (7/7)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in the Bayes update — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing belief $bel(x_t)$ with predicted belief $\bar{bel}(x_t)$** — the bar denotes "after prediction, before measurement."
> 3. **Mixing up dimensions of Jacobians** — for 2D pose, $G_t$ and $H_t$ are $3 \times 3$ matrices. Writing scalars for them loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the Markov assumption** explicitly — KTU rewards its mention in derivation problems.
> 5. **Sign errors in the innovation term** — the form is always $z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$, never the reverse.
> 6. **Forgetting the resampling step in MCL** — a particle filter without resampling diverges.
> 7. **EKF cannot do global localization** — stating otherwise is an automatic 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Localization**

- **Definition:** Localization computes the belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **State space:** Planar pose $(x, y, \theta) \in SE(2)$; 6-DoF pose $\in SE(3)$.
- **Three sub-problems:** Position tracking, global localization, kidnapped robot problem.
- **Markov Assumption** is the foundational simplification used by all filters.
- **Bayes filter** has two steps: **prediction** (motion model) and **update** (sensor model), separated by the **normalizer $\eta$**.
- **Motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$** propagates belief with added odometry noise $R_t$.
- **Sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$** scores beliefs against actual measurements; LIDAR uses beam-range Gaussian.
- **EKF:** Assumes **unimodal Gaussian**; uses Jacobians $G_t$, $H_t$; cannot do global localization; computationally efficient.
- **EKF Prediction:** $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$.
- **EKF Update:** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$; $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$; $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$.
- **Markov (Grid) Localization:** Discrete, multi-modal capable, $O(n)$ memory; resolution trade-off.
- **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL):** Particle-based, multi-modal capable, three steps — predict, weight, resample.
- **Effective Sample Size:** $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2$; resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$.
- **MCL Random Injection** is the standard cure for the kidnapped robot problem.
- **Kalman Gain $K_t$** acts as a dynamic weighting between sensor and motion: $K_t \to 0$ if sensor is unreliable, $K_t \to H^{-1}$ if sensor is perfect.
- **Innovations are zero-mean** under a correct filter — always check $E[\nu_t] = 0$ as a consistency test.
- **Real-world deployments:** Autonomous cars (EKF + GPS/LIDAR), warehouse AMRs (MCL), Mars rovers (EKF with vision), drone VIO (EKF with IMU/camera).
- **Numerical exam tip:** Always show the **Jacobian computation explicitly** in EKF derivations.
- **Comparison mnemonic:** EKF = "Efficient but Easy to Fail" (unimodal); MCL = "Multi-modal but More particles" (slower); Grid = "Simple but Scale-limited."
- **Three localization paradigms** for 14-mark answers: **EKF**, **Grid (Markov)**, **MCL** — expect any two.
- **Hard constraints to memorize:** $\sum_x bel(x) = 1$; the normalizer $\eta$ is **mandatory**; the innovation is $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping and Configuring Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mapping and Configuring Space ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Mapping **Mapping** in robotics is the computational process by which an autonomous agent constructs, maintains, and updates an internal spatial representation (a *map*) of its physical environment using onboard sensors such as LiDAR, RGB-D cameras, ultrasonic rangefinders, odometry, and inertial measurement units (IMUs). > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT525), **Mapping** is formally defined as the estimation of the posterior distribution over the environment $m$ given sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and robot poses $x_{1:t}$: > $$P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$ ### 1.2 Formal Definition — Configuration Space (C-Space) The **Configuration Space (C-space)**, denoted $\mathcal{C}$, is the space of *all possible configurations* a robot can attain. A **configuration** $q \in \mathcal{C}$ is a complete specification of the position and orientation of every point of the robot. The dimension of $\mathcal{C}$ equals the number of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the robot. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Obstacle Region in C-Space** > The **C-space obstacle** is defined as: > $$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \text{robot}(q) \cap \text{workspace obstacle} \neq \emptyset\}$$ > The **free space** is the complement: > $$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy 1 — Mapping is "Drawing a House While Blindfolded":** > Imagine walking through your home with a cane. Each tap of the cane gives you a local distance measurement. By stitching together millions of these "taps" with your remembered trajectory, you eventually reconstruct a floor plan. A robot does the same — it fuses noisy local sensor readings (LiDAR scans, camera frames) with pose estimates to build a global map. > [!TIP] > **Analogy 2 — Configuration Space is "The Robot's Mind-Gym":** > Forget the physical robot for a moment. Instead, think of a *point* moving through a high-dimensional abstract space. That point's coordinates *are* the joint angles and wheel positions of the real robot. Obstacles in the real world are mapped to "forbidden regions" in this mind-gym. Path planning becomes the simple problem of finding a curve through the gym that avoids the forbidden regions. This is C-space. ### 1.4 Key Metrics (Industry/Research Standards) | Metric | Standard Value | Context | |---|---|---| | **Occupancy threshold** | **0.65** (occupied) / **0.35** (free) | ROS `costmap_2d` defaults | | **Map resolution** | **0.05 m** (5 cm) | Standard for indoor mobile robots | | **Min C-space DOF for 6-DOF arm** | **6** | Industry benchmark for manipulators | | **SLAM loop closure tolerance** | **< 0.3 m** Euclidean | Cartographer default | | **SE(3) pose components** | $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ | 3 translation + 3 rotation | ### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters (KTU 2024 Context) > [!IMPORTANT] > Module 4 of PEBRT525 sits at the **core of autonomous navigation**. Without maps, a robot cannot localise; without C-space reasoning, a robot cannot plan collision-free motion. Together, Mapping + Configuring Space form the foundation for **SLAM**, **path planning**, and **autonomous decision-making** — all of which are examinable at KTU's End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Pillars — Why and How #### Pillar A — Mapping **Why?** A robot navigating an unknown environment must answer: *"What is around me?"* and *"Where am I in it?"* Mapping answers the first; localisation uses the map to answer the second. **How?** Mapping is fundamentally a **probabilistic inference** problem. Sensors are noisy, robot poses are uncertain, and the environment may be dynamic. Therefore, mapping is framed as Bayesian state estimation. The pipeline follows four logical steps: 1. **Sensor Data Acquisition** — Capture raw range/bearing measurements $z_t$. 2. **Pose Tracking** — Use odometry/IMU to estimate the robot's current pose $x_t$. 3. **Data Association** — Match current observations to existing map features (or create new ones). 4. **Map Update** — Fuse the new evidence into the global map using a recursive filter (e.g., Bayes/Kalman). #### Pillar B — Configuration Space **Why?** Planning in the robot's *workspace* (the physical world) is geometrically complex because the robot has volume, shape, and joint limits. Planning in C-space reduces the robot to a point, transforming a complex geometric problem into a point-motion problem. **How?** Construct C-space by: 1. **Parameterising** the robot with minimal coordinates (e.g., joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$). 2. **Inflating** workspace obstacles by the robot's geometry (for rigid bodies) to form $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$. 3. **Planning** in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ using algorithms like A*, RRT, or PRM. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notation & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Map posterior (Bayes)** | $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \propto P(z_t \mid m, x_t)\, P(m \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ | $m$ = map, $z$ = measurements, $x$ = poses | | 2 | **Occupancy grid cell update** | $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t)}$ | $\ell$ = log-odds value (no unit) | | 3 | **Inverse sensor model** | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \in [0,1]$ | Per-cell occupied/free probability | | 4 | **Configuration** | $q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n) \in \mathcal{C}$ | $n$ = DOF | | 5 | **DOF (Planar 2-link arm)** | $n = 2$ (revolute) → $\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 = \mathbb{T}^2$ | Torus topology | | 6 | **DOF (Rigid body in 3D)** | $n = 6$ → $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3) = SE(3)$ | Special Euclidean group | | 7 | **C-space obstacle** | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid R(q) \cap W_{obs} \neq \emptyset\}$ | $R(q)$ = robot placement, $W_{obs}$ = workspace obstacle | | 8 | **Free C-space** | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{C}$ | | 9 | **Minkowski sum (obstacle inflation)** | $W_{obs}^{\mathcal{C}} = W_{obs} \oplus (-R(0))$ | $\oplus$ = Minkowski sum; $R(0)$ = robot at origin | | 10 | **Path existence** | $\exists \pi : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ with $\pi(0) = q_{start}, \pi(1) = q_{goal}$ | Continuous feasible path | | 11 | **SE(3) transformation** | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0^\top & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R \in SO(3),\, p \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix | | 12 | **EKF-SLAM state vector** | $\mu_t = [x_t^\top, m_1^\top, \dots, m_N^\top]^\top$ | Concatenated pose + landmark poses | | 13 | **EKF-SLAM covariance** | $\Sigma_t = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{xx} & \Sigma_{xm} \\ \Sigma_{mx} & \Sigma_{mm} \end{bmatrix}$ | $3N+3 \times 3N+3$ matrix | | 14 | **Landmark observation** | $z_t = h(x_t, m_j) + v_t$ | $h$ = sensor model, $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ | | 15 | **Grid resolution constraint** | $\text{memory} = \frac{X \cdot Y}{r^2}$ cells | $X, Y$ = map extents (m), $r$ = resolution (m) | > [!WARNING] > **Equation Isolation Rule:** All formulas above use `\vert` / `\mid` (not the bare pipe `\|` inside markdown tables) to prevent syntax breakage in KTU's PDF/Markdown renderers. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Mapping + C-Space | |---|---| | **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla)** | Occupancy grids + lane-level C-space for collision-free trajectory planning | | **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva)** | Topological maps of shelf grid + 2D C-space for path planning among 1000s of robots | | **Surgical Robots (da Vinci)** | C-space of 7-DOF arm constrained by patient anatomy to avoid collision with tissue | | **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance)** | Visual SLAM builds hazard maps; C-space planning avoids rocks and slopes | | **Drones (DJI, Skydio)** | Voxel-grid 3D maps + SE(3) C-space for obstacle-aware flight | ### 2.4 Logical Connection to KTU Course Outcomes (COs) > [!NOTE] > **CO Mapping (PEBRT525, KTU 2024):** > - **CO3** — *Apply AI techniques to perceive and represent robotic environments.* → Mapping > - **CO4** — *Formulate motion planning problems in configuration space.* → C-Space > - Bloom's Levels: **Apply (L3)**, **Analyse (L4)**, **Create (L6)** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes Filter for Occupancy Grid Mapping **Problem Statement:** Given a sequence of range measurements $z_{1:t}$ and robot poses $x_{1:t}$, estimate the occupancy probability of grid cell $m_i$ independently. **Starting Point (Bayes Recursion):** $$ P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) $$ **Step 1 — Convert to Log-Odds Form** Define the **log-odds** representation to avoid numerical underflow and enable additive updates: $$ \ell_{i,t} = \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule** Substituting the posterior expression and the inverse sensor model $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$: $$ \ell_{i,t} = \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify to Recursive Form** Cancel the prior log-odds and obtain the **additive update rule**: $$ \ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \underbrace{\log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}}_{\text{inverse sensor model term}} $$ **Step 4 — Recover Probability (Inverse Logit)** To get the probability back for the final map: $$ P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\ell_{i,t}}} $$ **Result:** This is the canonical **occupancy grid map update equation** used in ROS, Cartographer, and any Bayesian grid mapper. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Configuration Space Obstacle via Minkowski Sum **Problem Statement:** Reduce a circular mobile robot of radius $r$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ to a point, and inflate all workspace obstacles accordingly. **Setup:** - Robot $R$ at configuration $q = (x, y)$ is the set $R(q) = \{(u,v) \mid (u-x)^2 + (v-y)^2 \leq r^2\}$. - Workspace obstacle $W_{obs} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$. **Step 1 — Condition for Collision** The robot collides with $W_{obs}$ when their interiors overlap: $$ R(q) \cap W_{obs} \neq \emptyset $$ **Step 2 — Translate Robot to Origin** Substitute $u' = u - x, v' = v - y$ and define the robot at origin $R(0) = \{(u',v') \mid u'^2 + v'^2 \leq r^2\}$: $$ \exists (u',v') \in R(0) : (u'+x, v'+y) \in W_{obs} $$ **Step 3 — Reformulate as Point-Set Test** Equivalently, the configuration $q = (x,y)$ is in $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ if: $$ q \in W_{obs} \oplus (-R(0)) = \{w - r_0 \mid w \in W_{obs},\, r_0 \in R(0)\} $$ **Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation** The **Minkowski sum** $W_{obs} \oplus (-R(0))$ dilates the obstacle by radius $r$ in all directions: $$ W_{obs}^{\mathcal{C}} = \{ (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \exists (u,v) \in W_{obs} : (x-u)^2 + (y-v)^2 \leq r^2 \} $$ **Result:** The original point-robot planning problem becomes a circular-robot problem by simply **inflating every obstacle by the robot's radius** and treating the robot as a point. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DOF Computation for Common Robot Topologies **Problem Statement:** Determine the configuration space dimension (DOF) for three canonical robot types. #### Case A — Point Robot in 2D Plane - Coordinates needed: $(x, y)$ → **2** parameters. - $n = 2$, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$. #### Case B — 2-Link Planar Manipulator (Revolute Joints) - Joint angles: $\theta_1, \theta_2$ → **2** parameters, each in $[0, 2\pi)$. - $n = 2$, $\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 \cong \mathbb{T}^2$ (the torus). - Joint limits or self-collision carves out $\mathcal{C}_{obs} \subset \mathbb{T}^2$. #### Case C — 6-DOF Rigid Body in 3D Space - Translation: $(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3$. - Orientation: roll-pitch-yaw $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ or quaternion. - $n = 6$, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3) = SE(3)$. **General Formula (Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion):** $$ \text{DOF} = m(N-1-J) + \sum_{i=1}^{J} f_i $$ where $m = 6$ (3D) or $3$ (2D), $N$ = number of links, $J$ = number of joints, $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Map Type Selection | Map Type | Representation | Best Use Case | Memory | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Occupancy Grid** | 2D/3D array of $\{0,1\}$ | Indoor, static env. | $O(XY/r^2)$ | Simple, fast updates | No semantics, scale issues | | **Feature Map** | Set of landmarks $\{l_i, \Sigma_i\}$ | Outdoor, EKF-SLAM | $O(N)$ landmarks | Compact, probabilistic | Needs distinguishable features | | **Topological Map** | Graph $G = (V, E)$ | Floor-plan navigation | $O(V+E)$ | Human-readable, scalable | No metric precision | | **Point Cloud** | Set of 3D points $\{p_i\}$ | 3D reconstruction | $O(N)$ | High fidelity | Huge, no semantics | | **Semantic Map** | Grid + object labels | Human–robot interaction | $O(\text{grid} + \text{labels})$ | Task-aware | Needs ML (CNN) front-end | | **Voxel Grid** | 3D occupancy cubes | Drone/3D nav. | $O(XYZ/r^3)$ | Full 3D | Very memory-heavy | | **ESDF (Euclidean Signed Distance)** | Distance field per cell | MPC trajectory opt. | $O(XY/r^2)$ | Smooth gradients | Slow to update | ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Map Update) **Given:** A robot at pose $x_t = (1, 0, 0)$ observes cell $m_5$. Prior $\ell_{5, t-1} = 0.0$ (occupancy probability = 0.5). Inverse sensor model returns $P(m_5 \mid z_t, x_t) = 0.7$. **Step 1 — Compute inverse sensor log-odds:** $$ \text{ISM log-odds} = \log \frac{0.7}{0.3} = \log(2.333) \approx 0.847 $$ **Step 2 — Apply recursive update:** $$ \ell_{5, t} = \ell_{5, t-1} + \text{ISM log-odds} = 0.0 + 0.847 = 0.847 $$ **Step 3 — Recover occupancy probability:** $$ P(m_5 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.847}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 2.333} = 1 - 0.300 = 0.700 $$ **Interpretation:** After one observation, the cell's probability of being occupied rose from $0.5$ to $0.70$ — consistent with the prior-belief fusion logic. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Mapping Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sensor Input
LiDAR Camera IMU] --> B[Pose Estimation
Odometry Filter] A --> C[Feature Extraction
Landmark Detect] B --> D[Data Association
Nearest Neighbor] C --> D D --> E[Bayesian Update
Map Estimator] E --> F[Occupancy Grid Map
2D 3D Voxel] E --> G[Feature Map
Landmarks] E --> H[Topological Map
Graph Nodes] F --> I[Local Path Planner
C-Space Query] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Motor Commands
Velocity Control] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Configuration Space Construction (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robot Definition
URDF Links Joints] --> B[Compute DOF
Grublers Formula] B --> C{Choose Parameterization} C -->|Point in Rn| D[Workspace Coordinates] C -->|Joint Angles| E[Configuration Vector q] D --> F[Inflate Obstacles
Minkowski Sum] E --> G[Map Obstacles
Forward Kinematics] F --> H[C-Free Region] G --> H H --> I[Path Planner
A Star RRT PRM] I --> J[Feasible Trajectory
Trajectory Tracking] ``` ### 4.3 SLAM Loop — Functional Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Robot Motion u_t] --> B[Predict Pose
Odometry Model] B --> C[Pose Belief x_t] C --> D[Acquire Sensors z_t] D --> E[Predict Measurement] E --> F{Compare with z_t} F -->|Innovation| G[Update Pose and Map] G --> H[Updated Map m_t] H --> I[Loop Closure Check] I -->|Match Found| J[Graph Optimization
Pose Graph SLAM] J --> B H --> K[Output Global Map] ``` ### 4.4 C-Space Obstacle Inflation — Block Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Workspace Obstacle W_obs] --> B[Apply Minkowski Sum] C[Robot Geometry R0] --> B B --> D[C-Space Obstacle C_obs] D --> E[Complement in C] E --> F[C-Free Region] F --> G[Run Sampling Based Planner] G --> H[Feasible Path in C-Space] ``` ### 4.5 Map Type Decision Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Need Map] --> B{Environment Scale} B -->|Indoor Room| C[Occupancy Grid] B -->|Outdoor Large| D[Feature EKF SLAM] B -->|Multi Floor| E[Topological Map] C --> F{Need Semantics} F -->|Yes| G[Semantic Grid] F -->|No| H[Plain Grid] D --> I{3D Required} I -->|Yes| J[Voxel Hash Map] I -->|No| K[2D Feature Map] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Define Configuration Space (C-Space) of a robot. Explain with an example how the dimension of C-Space relates to the degrees of freedom of the robot.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):** - **(1 Mark)** *Definition:* The Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain. A configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}$ is a unique specification of the position of every point of the robot. - **(1 Mark)** *DOF Relation:* The dimension of $\mathcal{C}$ equals the number of Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of the robot. A planar point robot has $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ (DOF = 2), while a 2-link revolute arm has $\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 \cong \mathbb{T}^2$ (DOF = 2). - **(1 Mark)** *Obstacle/Free decomposition:* $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{free} \cup \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ where $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the set of colliding configurations. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **workspace** (the physical 2D/3D world) with **configuration space** (the abstract parameter space). If you write *"C-space is the physical room the robot moves in"*, you lose 1 mark. Always emphasise the *parameterisation* aspect. #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **What is an occupancy grid map? Write the Bayesian update rule for a single cell of an occupancy grid.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):** - **(1 Mark)** *Definition:* An occupancy grid is a discretised map where the environment is divided into equally-spaced cells, each storing a probability $P(m_i)$ of being occupied. It is built from range sensor measurements and known robot poses. - **(1 Mark)** *Bayes Recursion:* $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$. - **(1 Mark)** *Log-odds form (additive):* $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$, enabling fast numerical updates. > [!WARNING] > **Common 1-Mark Loss:** Writing only the *concept* of Bayes without giving the actual equation. KTU examiners reward **explicit formulas** for at least 1 mark in any "write the rule" question. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern] **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the concept of **Configuration Space** for a rigid robot. Derive the **C-space obstacle** formulation for a circular mobile robot of radius $r$ in a 2D workspace. Show how the **Minkowski sum** operation transforms the problem. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **occupancy grid maps**, **feature-based maps**, and **topological maps**. State the Bayesian update equation for an occupancy grid and show the **log-odds simplification** step-by-step. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Configuration Space (7 Marks):** - **(2 Marks)** *Definition + Diagram:* Define $\mathcal{C}$, $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Draw a 2D workspace showing a rectangular robot, then map it to a point in C-space with inflated obstacles. - **(2 Marks)** *Mathematical Setup:* Let $R(q)$ denote the robot at configuration $q$, $W_{obs}$ the workspace obstacle. The collision condition is $R(q) \cap W_{obs} \neq \emptyset$. - **(2 Marks)** *Minkowski Sum Derivation:* For a circular robot of radius $r$ at $q = (x,y)$: $$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \exists (u,v) \in W_{obs} : (x-u)^2 + (y-v)^2 \leq r^2\} = W_{obs} \oplus (-R(0))$$ - **(1 Mark)** *Conclusion:* The robot becomes a point and obstacles grow by $r$ in every direction — a clean reduction for path planners. **Part (b) — Map Types & Bayesian Update (7 Marks):** - **(3 Marks)** *Comparison Table:* | Map Type | Representation | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | Occupancy Grid | 2D array $\{P(m_i)\}$ | Simple, easy update | Memory $O(XY/r^2)$ | | Feature Map | Landmarks $\{l_i, \Sigma_i\}$ | Compact, prob. | Needs detectable features | | Topological Map | Graph $G = (V, E)$ | Scalable, human-readable | No metric precision | - **(3 Marks)** *Bayesian Update Derivation:* 1. Start with $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$. 2. Take log-odds: $\ell_{i,t} = \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$. 3. Simplify: $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$. - **(1 Mark)** *Interpretation:* The update is **additive** in log-odds, making it computationally efficient and numerically stable. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** A common mistake is omitting the **diagrams** in part (a). KTU valuation keys allocate **at least 1–2 marks** for neat, labelled diagrams showing both the workspace and C-space. Always draw both. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern] **(a)** Define the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot. Using the **Grübler–Kutzbach criterion**, compute the DOF of: (i) A 3-link planar manipulator with all revolute joints. (ii) A 6-DOF industrial robot arm. Explain the configuration space topology in each case. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is **SLAM**? Explain the **EKF-SLAM** algorithm with its state vector, prediction step, and update step. State the role of **data association** in SLAM. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — DOF Computation (7 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** *Definition:* DOF is the minimum number of independent parameters required to uniquely specify a robot's configuration in space. - **(1 Mark)** *Grübler Formula:* $\text{DOF} = m(N - 1 - J) + \sum_{i=1}^{J} f_i$, where $m = 3$ (planar) or $6$ (spatial). - **(2 Marks)** *(i) 3-link planar manipulator:* $m = 3, N = 4$ (ground + 3 links), $J = 3$ (revolute joints, $f_i = 1$). $$\text{DOF} = 3(4 - 1 - 3) + 3(1) = 3(0) + 3 = 3$$ $\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \cong \mathbb{T}^3$ (3-torus). - **(2 Marks)** *(ii) 6-DOF industrial arm:* Has six revolute joints, $\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1$ but constrained to a 6D subset of $SE(3)$. Each joint contributes 1 DOF, total = 6. - **(1 Mark)** *Conclusion:* For both, $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = \text{DOF}$, confirming the general principle. **Part (b) — EKF-SLAM (7 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** *SLAM Definition:* Simultaneous Localization and Mapping — the problem of *concurrently* estimating the robot pose $x_t$ and the map $m$. - **(2 Marks)** *State Vector & Covariance:* $$\mu_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ m_1 \\ \vdots \\ m_N \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma_t = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{xx} & \Sigma_{xm} \\ \Sigma_{mx} & \Sigma_{mm} \end{bmatrix}$$ - **(2 Marks)** *Prediction Step:* $$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t, \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$$ - **(1 Mark)** *Update Step:* $$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}, \quad \mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)), \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$ - **(1 Mark)** *Data Association:* Identifying which landmark in the map corresponds to the current observation $z_t$. Incorrect association leads to catastrophic filter divergence. Common methods: **nearest neighbour**, **JCBB (Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B SLAM):** Many students forget the **cross-covariance terms** $\Sigma_{xm}$ in the EKF-SLAM matrix. These are *critical* — they encode the correlation between robot pose and landmark estimates, and their growth is precisely what makes EKF-SLAM scale cubically $O(N^2)$. Omitting them = **−2 marks**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision — Mapping & Configuring Space (Module 4)** - ✅ **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** = space of all robot configurations; **dim = DOF**. - ✅ **C-obstacle** $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \mid R(q) \cap W_{obs} \neq \emptyset\}$; **C-free** $= \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$. - ✅ **Minkowski sum** $W_{obs} \oplus (-R(0))$ inflates obstacles to reduce rigid robot to a point. - ✅ **Planar point robot:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$; **2-link arm:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{T}^2$; **3D rigid body:** $\mathcal{C} = SE(3)$. - ✅ **Grübler–Kutzbach:** $\text{DOF} = m(N - 1 - J) + \sum f_i$. - ✅ **Occupancy grid** stores per-cell probability $P(m_i)$; updated via **Bayes rule**. - ✅ **Log-odds update:** $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$. - ✅ **Map types:** Occupancy grid (metric, dense), Feature map (sparse landmarks), Topological (graph), Semantic (with labels), Voxel (3D), ESDF (distance field). - ✅ **SLAM** = concurrent pose + map estimation. **EKF-SLAM** uses Gaussian filter; complexity $O(N^2)$. - ✅ **EKF-SLAM state:** $\mu_t = [x_t, m_1, \dots, m_N]^\top$, with **cross-covariance** $\Sigma_{xm}$ linking pose and map. - ✅ **Data association** = matching observations to landmarks; critical for EKF-SLAM correctness. - ✅ **Loop closure** = recognising a previously-visited place; triggers graph optimisation. - ✅ **Inverse sensor model** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ is the per-cell probability derived from a single measurement. - ✅ **SE(3)** = $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ — pose space for 3D rigid bodies. - ✅ **Default ROS thresholds:** occupancy $\geq 0.65$, map resolution $0.05$ m, loop-closure tolerance $< 0.3$ m. - ✅ **KTU 2024 COs covered:** **CO3** (perception/representation), **CO4** (motion planning in C-space). - ✅ **Bloom's Levels tested:** Apply (L3) — formulas, Analyse (L4) — derivations, Create (L6) — map-type selection. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked a *comparison* question, **always use a table** — KTU examiners scan for tabular comparisons first, and a clean 3-column table with 3 rows can fetch you 3 marks effortlessly. When asked a *derivation*, **show every algebraic step** — partial credit is awarded generously for correct intermediate steps even if the final answer is wrong.

topics.title: Planning Uncertain Movements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT525 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning Uncertain Movements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Planning Uncertain Movements — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning Uncertain Movements** is the sub-field of AI for Robotics concerned with synthesizing a sequence of robot actions when the **effects of actions are stochastic** and the **true state of the world may be only partially observable** through noisy sensors.

A planner operating under these conditions must answer two intertwined questions at every time step:

1. **What is the most likely world state given everything I have sensed so far?** *(perception / state estimation)*
2. **What action maximises my expected long-term utility?** *(decision-theoretic control)*

Mathematically, the problem is formalised as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** when the state is fully observable, and a **Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP)** when it is not.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **State Space $S$** | All configurations the robot / world can take. |
| **Action Space $A$** | Discrete or continuous control primitives. |
| **Transition Model $T(s,a,s')$** | Probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via $a$. |
| **Observation Model $O(s,o)$** | Probability of receiving observation $o$ in state $s$. |
| **Reward $R(s,a,s')$** | Scalar reinforcement signal guiding behaviour. |
| **Discount $\gamma \in [0,1)$** | Weighting of future vs immediate reward. |
| **Policy $\pi$** | A mapping from state (or belief) to action. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Driving in Dense Fog"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine driving a car through a city in thick fog at night.** You cannot see the road clearly, your steering may slip on wet patches, and you can only *estimate* your position by hearing milestones and feeling bumps. Yet you must still reach the hospital. You mentally keep **a probability distribution** of where you *might* be, you **act conservatively** (slow down, signal), and you **continuously update your belief** as new evidence arrives.

A robot planning uncertain movements behaves identically:

- The **fog** is sensor noise and partial observability.
- The **slippery steering** is the stochastic transition model.
- The **mental map of probabilities** is the **belief state** of a POMDP.
- The **conservative actions** are the output of the optimal **policy**.

## 1.3 Why Uncertainty Is Inevitable in Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three sources of uncertainty are unavoidable in real robots:**
> 1. **Actuator noise** — motors slip, wheels skid, grippers miss.
> 2. **Sensor noise** — LiDAR returns are noisy, cameras suffer from motion blur.
> 3. **Environmental stochasticity** — humans move, wind blows, surfaces change friction.

A deterministic planner that assumes *"my action $a$ always leads to state $s'$"* will catastrophically fail in such environments. The AI-Robotics planner must reason in *expectations* over distributions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Framework

A finite MDP is the tuple $\langle S, A, T, R, \gamma \rangle$. The **Markov property** states that the next state depends *only* on the current state and action — not on history:

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = T(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$$

A **policy** $\pi : S \to A$ assigns an action to every state. The **expected cumulative reward** (return) under $\pi$ starting from $s$ is:

$$U^{\pi}(s) = E\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R(s_t, \pi(s_t), s_{t+1}) \mid s_0 = s\right]$$

### 2.1.1 The Bellman Equation (Core Result)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bellman optimality equation** for an MDP expresses the optimal value $V^{*}(s)$ recursively:
> $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s' \in S} T(s,a,s')\bigl[R(s,a,s') + \gamma\, V^{*}(s')\bigr]$$
> This is the **single most important equation** in planning under uncertainty and is the foundation of all MDP solvers.

The corresponding **action-value function** $Q^{*}(s,a)$ is:

$$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s' \in S} T(s,a,s')\bigl[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a' \in A} Q^{*}(s',a')\bigr]$$

## 2.2 Value Iteration Algorithm

The Bellman equation is a system of $|S|$ non-linear equations. Value iteration solves it by turning it into a **contraction mapping**:

$$V_{k+1}(s) \;\leftarrow\; \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s' \in S} T(s,a,s')\bigl[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V_{k}(s')\bigr]$$

**Convergence guarantee:** Because the Bellman update is a $\gamma$-contraction in max-norm, $\lim_{k \to \infty} V_k = V^{*}$ for any finite MDP.

## 2.3 Policy Iteration Algorithm

An alternative that alternates between **policy evaluation** and **policy improvement**:

1. **Evaluate** $V^{\pi}$ by solving the linear system: $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{s'} T(s,\pi(s),s')[R(s,\pi(s),s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
2. **Improve** the policy: $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
3. Repeat until $\pi$ is stable.

Policy iteration usually converges in **far fewer iterations** than value iteration but each iteration is more expensive.

## 2.4 Partially Observable MDPs (POMDPs)

When the robot cannot observe $s$ directly, it receives a noisy observation $o$. The state is summarised by a **belief state** $b(s)$, a probability distribution over $S$.

**Bayesian belief update** after executing $a$ and observing $o$:

$$b'(s') = \eta \cdot O(s',o) \sum_{s \in S} T(s,a,s')\, b(s)$$

where $\eta$ is a normalising constant ensuring $\sum_{s'} b'(s') = 1$.

The POMDP value function lives in **belief space** (continuous) and is **piecewise-linear and convex**:

$$V^{*}(b) = \max_{a} \Bigl[\sum_{s} b(s) R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{o} P(o \mid a,b)\, V^{*}(b'^{\,a,o})\Bigr]$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **minimum equation vocabulary** required for a KTU ESE question on this topic.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Domain / Range |
|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| $T(s,a,s')$ | Transition probability $P(s' \mid s,a)$ | $[0,1]$ |
| $O(s,o)$ | Observation likelihood $P(o \mid s)$ | $[0,1]$ |
| $R(s,a,s')$ | Immediate reward | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $[0,1)$ |
| $V^{\pi}(s)$ | Value of state $s$ under policy $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $Q(s,a)$ | Value of taking action $a$ in $s$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $b(s)$ | Belief probability of being in $s$ | $[0,1]$, sums to $1$ |
| $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s'}T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s')+\gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Bellman optimality | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $b'(s') = \eta O(s',o)\sum_{s}T(s,a,s')b(s)$ | POMDP belief update | $[0,1]$ |
| $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Greedy optimal policy | $A$ |
| $U = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t} r_{t+1}$ | Discounted return | $\mathbb{R}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-keeping under slippery conditions, intersection merging with hidden occlusions. |
| **Warehouse Robotics** | Navigating alongside human workers whose behaviour is stochastic. |
| **Surgical Robots** | Compensating for tissue deformation and sensor noise. |
| **Drone Swarms** | Wind disturbances, GPS dropouts, inter-agent collision avoidance. |
| **Underwater AUVs** | Strong currents, acoustic-sensor multipath noise. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **curse of history** and **curse of dimensionality** are the chief computational barriers: a POMDP with $n$ states has a belief space of dimension $n-1$, making exact solution intractable for $n \gtrsim 20$. Approximate methods (point-based value iteration, QMDP, SARSOP) are used in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation

**Step 1 — Define the expected discounted return** starting from state $s$ under a policy $\pi$:

$$U^{\pi}(s) = E\Bigl[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R(s_t, \pi(s_t), s_{t+1}) \;\Big|\; s_0 = s\Bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Split the sum into the first reward and the discounted future:**

$$U^{\pi}(s) = E\Bigl[R(s_0, \pi(s_0), s_1) + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R(s_t, \pi(s_t), s_{t+1}) \;\Big|\; s_0 = s\Bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\gamma$ from the second term and recognise that the inner sum is $U^{\pi}(s_1)$:**

$$U^{\pi}(s) = E\Bigl[R(s, \pi(s), s_1)\Bigr] + \gamma\, E\Bigl[U^{\pi}(s_1)\Bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Use the Markov property to condition only on $s$ and the action $a = \pi(s)$:**

$$U^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{s' \in S} T(s, \pi(s), s')\Bigl[R(s, \pi(s), s') + \gamma\, U^{\pi}(s')\Bigr]$$

**Step 5 — To obtain the *optimal* value, take the maximum over all actions at state $s$:**

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s' \in S} T(s,a,s')\Bigl[R(s,a,s') + \gamma\, V^{*}(s')\Bigr] \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the POMDP Belief Update (Bayes' Rule Application)

**Step 1 — We want $b'(s') = P(s' \mid a, o, b)$.** By definition of conditional probability:

$$b'(s') = \frac{P(o, s' \mid a, b)}{P(o \mid a, b)}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the numerator using the law of total probability over previous state $s$:**

$$P(o, s' \mid a, b) = \sum_{s} P(s' \mid s, a)\, P(o \mid s', a, b)\, P(s \mid a, b)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov property to simplify each factor:**

$$P(s' \mid s, a) = T(s,a,s'), \quad P(o \mid s') = O(s',o), \quad P(s \mid b) = b(s)$$

**Step 4 — Combine and introduce the normaliser $\eta$:**

$$b'(s') = \eta\, O(s',o) \sum_{s} T(s,a,s')\, b(s) \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — 3-State Mobile Robot MDP

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup.** Consider a differential-drive mobile robot with three discrete states:
> - $S_0$ — *Corridor* (safe but reward neutral)
> - $S_1$ — *Doorway* (risky passage)
> - $S_2$ — *Goal* (terminal, success)
>
> Two actions:
> - $a_1$ — *Move Forward*
> - $a_2$ — *Stop and Wait*
>
> Stochastic transitions (engineer's slip model):
> $$T(S_0, a_1, S_0) = 0.7,\quad T(S_0, a_1, S_1) = 0.3,\quad T(S_0, a_2, S_0) = 1.0$$
> $$T(S_1, a_1, S_1) = 0.6,\quad T(S_1, a_1, S_2) = 0.4,\quad T(S_1, a_2, S_1) = 1.0$$
> $$S_2 \text{ is absorbing: } T(S_2, *, S_2) = 1.0$$
>
> Rewards (sparse, event-driven):
> $$R(s,a,s') = \begin{cases} +10 & \text{if } s' = S_2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
>
> Discount $\gamma = 0.9$.

### 3.3.1 Iteration 0 — Initialisation

$$V_0(S_0) = 0, \quad V_0(S_1) = 0, \quad V_0(S_2) = 0$$

### 3.3.2 Iteration 1 — First Bellman Backup

**For $S_0$:**

$$Q(S_0, a_1) = T(S_0,a_1,S_0)[R(S_0,a_1,S_0)+\gamma V_0(S_0)] + T(S_0,a_1,S_1)[R(S_0,a_1,S_1)+\gamma V_0(S_1)]$$
$$= 0.7 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] + 0.3 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] = 0$$

$$Q(S_0, a_2) = T(S_0,a_2,S_0)[R(S_0,a_2,S_0)+\gamma V_0(S_0)] = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] = 0$$

$$V_1(S_0) = \max\{Q(S_0,a_1), Q(S_0,a_2)\} = \max\{0, 0\} = 0$$

**For $S_1$:**

$$Q(S_1, a_1) = 0.6 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] + 0.4 \times [10 + 0.9 \times 0] = 0 + 0.4 \times 10 = 4.0$$

$$Q(S_1, a_2) = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] = 0$$

$$V_1(S_1) = \max\{4.0, 0\} = 4.0$$

**For $S_2$** (terminal — no future reward):

$$V_1(S_2) = 0$$

**Iteration-1 result table:**

| State | $V_1$ | Optimal Action |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| $S_0$ | $0.00$ | Either (tie) |
| $S_1$ | $4.00$ | $a_1$ (Move Forward) |
| $S_2$ | $0.00$ | — |

### 3.3.3 Iteration 2 — Bellman Backup Using $V_1$

**For $S_0$:**

$$Q(S_0, a_1) = 0.7 \times [0 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_0)] + 0.3 \times [0 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_1)]$$
$$= 0.7 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 0] + 0.3 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 4.0]$$
$$= 0 + 0.3 \times 3.6 = 1.08$$

$$Q(S_0, a_2) = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_0)] = 0$$

$$V_2(S_0) = \max\{1.08, 0\} = 1.08$$

**For $S_1$:**

$$Q(S_1, a_1) = 0.6 \times [0 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_1)] + 0.4 \times [10 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_2)]$$
$$= 0.6 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 4.0] + 0.4 \times [10 + 0.9 \times 0]$$
$$= 0.6 \times 3.6 + 0.4 \times 10 = 2.16 + 4.0 = 6.16$$

$$Q(S_1, a_2) = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times V_1(S_1)] = 0.9 \times 4.0 = 3.60$$

$$V_2(S_1) = \max\{6.16, 3.60\} = 6.16$$

**For $S_2$:** $V_2(S_2) = 0$ (terminal).

**Iteration-2 result table:**

| State | $V_2$ | Optimal Action |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| $S_0$ | $1.08$ | $a_1$ (Move Forward) |
| $S_1$ | $6.16$ | $a_1$ (Move Forward) |
| $S_2$ | $0.00$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Policy changed at $S_0$:** at iteration 1 both actions tied at 0; at iteration 2 the *future* reward of $S_1$ makes $a_1$ superior. This illustrates how value iteration propagates information **backwards from the goal** through the reachable state graph.

### 3.3.4 Iteration 3 — Final Verification of Convergence

**For $S_0$:**

$$Q(S_0, a_1) = 0.7 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 1.08] + 0.3 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 6.16]$$
$$= 0.7 \times 0.972 + 0.3 \times 5.544 = 0.6804 + 1.6632 = 2.3436$$

$$Q(S_0, a_2) = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 1.08] = 0.972$$

$$V_3(S_0) = \max\{2.3436,\; 0.972\} = 2.3436$$

**For $S_1$:**

$$Q(S_1, a_1) = 0.6 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 6.16] + 0.4 \times [10 + 0.9 \times 0]$$
$$= 0.6 \times 5.544 + 4.0 = 3.3264 + 4.0 = 7.3264$$

$$Q(S_1, a_2) = 1.0 \times [0 + 0.9 \times 6.16] = 5.544$$

$$V_3(S_1) = \max\{7.3264,\; 5.544\} = 7.3264$$

**Final converged policy after iteration 3:** $\pi^{*}(S_0) = a_1$, $\pi^{*}(S_1) = a_1$, terminal at $S_2$.

### 3.3.5 Summary Table of Convergence

| Iter $k$ | $V_k(S_0)$ | $V_k(S_1)$ | $\Delta_k = \max_s \lvert V_{k}(s) - V_{k-1}(s) \rvert$ |
|----------|------------|------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | — |
| 1 | 0.0000 | 4.0000 | 4.0000 |
| 2 | 1.0800 | 6.1600 | 2.1600 |
| 3 | 2.3436 | 7.3264 | 1.2636 |

The maximum change $\Delta_k$ decreases geometrically (roughly by factor $\gamma = 0.9$), confirming the **contraction property** of the Bellman backup operator.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — MDP vs POMDP Decision Factors

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is the kind of comparison examiners love to set as a 7-mark "compare and contrast" sub-question.

| Attribute | MDP | POMDP |
|-----------|-----|-------|
| State observability | Fully observable | Partially observable |
| Decision input | $s$ | Belief $b$ (distribution over $S$) |
| Transition model | $T(s,a,s')$ | Same $T(s,a,s')$ |
| Observation model | Not required | $O(s,o)$ essential |
| Value function domain | Discrete $S$ | Continuous belief simplex $\Delta^{|S|-1}$ |
| Bellman equation | $V^{*}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R+\gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | $V^{*}(b) = \max_a [\sum_s b(s)R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_o P(o \mid a,b) V^{*}(b')]$ |
| Computational complexity | Polynomial in $\lvert S \rvert, \lvert A \rvert$ | PSPACE-complete (intractable exact) |
| Solution methods | Value / policy iteration | Point-based VI, QMDP, SARSOP, POMCP |
| Practical robotics use | Localisation-known motion | SLAM, exploration, manipulation under occlusion |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 POMDP Closed-Loop Control Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **complete sense–plan–act cycle** of a robot operating as a POMDP agent.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Belief State b sub t]
    nodeB[Policy Solver pi b sub t]
    nodeC[Action a sub t]
    nodeD[Robot Actuator]
    nodeE[World Transition T s a s prime]
    nodeF[New Physical State s prime]
    nodeG[Sensor Reading o sub t+1]
    nodeH[Bayesian Belief Update]
    nodeI[New Belief b sub t+1]
    nodeJ[Value Function Update V]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The robot never sees the true state $s$. Instead, the **belief $b$** is the *internal estimate* used by the policy. The two key feedback loops are (1) the **belief update** from sensor data and (2) the **value-function update** that improves the policy over time.

## 4.2 Value Iteration Algorithm — Block Flow

The diagram below shows the iterative structure of value iteration as a **fixed-point computation**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Initialise V zero to zero]
    stepB[Set tolerance epsilon and max iterations]
    stepC[Begin iteration loop k equals 1]
    stepD[For each state s in S]
    stepE[For each action a in A]
    stepF[Compute Q s a equals sum T R plus gamma V old]
    stepG[V new s equals max over a of Q s a]
    stepH[Delta equals max over s of V new s minus V old s]
    stepI[Is Delta less than epsilon]
    stepJ[Output optimal V star and policy pi star]
    stepK[Set V old equals V new and increment k]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepK
    stepK --> stepC
    stepI --> stepJ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two terminal conditions of the loop are: **(i) convergence** $\Delta < \epsilon$, or **(ii) iteration cap** $k = K_{\max}$. The Bellman operator is a $\gamma$-contraction, so condition (i) is always reached in finite time for any $\epsilon > 0$.

## 4.3 Architecture for an Autonomous Mobile Robot Planner

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a real robot stack that uses the theory of this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Sensor Layer LiDAR Camera IMU]
    nodeB[Localisation Module Belief Tracker]
    nodeC[Map Representation]
    nodeD[POMDP Belief Update Engine]
    nodeE[Policy Network pi b to a]
    nodeF[Value Function Approximator V b]
    nodeG[Motion Planner Trajectory Generator]
    nodeH[Low Level Controller Wheel Commands]
    nodeI[Robot Body]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical **perception $\rightarrow$ belief $\rightarrow$ policy $\rightarrow$ action** pipeline. The **POMDP belief-update engine** in the centre is the exact mathematical operation derived in Section 3.2. In production systems it is often replaced by a **particle filter** for continuous state spaces.

---
